Understanding NULL behavior in WHERE is one of the most important practical skills in SQL, and the source of some of the hardest-to-find bugs.
Why WHERE column = NULL Fails

-- WRONG: This returns zero rows, even if NULLs exist
SELECT m.meter_id, m.substation_id
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  m.substation_id = NULL;

-- CORRECT: Use IS NULL
SELECT m.meter_id, m.substation_id
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  m.substation_id IS NULL
LIMIT  5;

-- WRONG query: returns 0 rows (= NULL is always NULL, never TRUE)

-- CORRECT query:
meter_id       | substation_id
---------------+--------------
CH-BERN-99801  | (null)
CH-BERN-99802  | (null)
AT-SLZB-99901  | (null)
CH-ZRCH-99701  | (null)
AT-INNB-99501  | (null)

The = NULL comparison evaluates to NULL for every row — including rows where substation_id actually is NULL. Because WHERE keeps only TRUE rows, the result is empty. IS NULL is the only correct operator for testing whether a value is NULL. This is the same principle from Module 2, now applied in a WHERE clause where the consequences are practical: your query returns wrong results silently, with no error message to alert you.
The Hidden Row Problem

A more insidious problem occurs with inequality filters on nullable columns. Consider WHERE substation_id <> 'SUB-BERN-04'. You expect this to return every meter that is not assigned to substation SUB-BERN-04. But it also excludes every meter whose substation_id is NULL — because NULL <> 'SUB-BERN-04' evaluates to NULL, not TRUE. If 500 meters have NULL substation IDs, those 500 meters silently disappear from the result. The query runs without errors and returns plausible results — you just have 500 fewer rows than expected.

-- This excludes NULL substation_ids (they silently disappear)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS meter_count
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id <> 'SUB-BERN-04';

-- This includes NULL substation_ids
SELECT COUNT(*) AS meter_count
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id <> 'SUB-BERN-04'
   OR  substation_id IS NULL;

-- Without IS NULL: 349,200 meters (500 NULL meters missing)
-- With IS NULL:    349,700 meters (all non-SUB-BERN-04 meters)

The difference is 500 meters — the ones with NULL substation IDs. Depending on the business context, this might be a rounding error or a critical bug. For a billing query, 500 missing meters means 500 customers who do not receive invoices. The fix is explicit: add OR substation_id IS NULL whenever you want NULL rows included in an inequality filter. At HydroAlpes, Lena's code review checklist includes: 'Does any WHERE condition filter a nullable column with <>, NOT IN, or NOT LIKE? If so, verify that NULL rows are handled correctly.'
IS NOT NULL

-- Find meters that HAVE been assigned to a substation
SELECT m.meter_id, m.substation_id, m.region
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  m.substation_id IS NOT NULL
LIMIT  5;

IS NOT NULL keeps only rows where the column has a real value — any value, as long as it is not NULL. This is useful for data completeness checks (how many meters have been mapped to substations?) and for ensuring that downstream calculations do not encounter unexpected NULLs. The pair IS NULL and IS NOT NULL are the only safe operators for testing nullability. Never use = NULL, <> NULL, or NOT NULL without IS.

blocks.title: The golden rule of NULL filtering
blocks.content: If a column can contain NULL, every WHERE condition on that column must explicitly account for NULL behavior. Use IS NULL to find NULLs. Use IS NOT NULL to exclude them. When using <>, NOT IN, or NOT LIKE on a nullable column, decide whether NULLs should be included in the result and add OR column IS NULL if they should. Never assume that NULL rows will 'just work' — they will not.

Throughout Modules 3 and 4, every query has returned rows in an unspecified order — the database returned whichever rows it found fastest, with no guarantee of consistency between executions. For exploration, this is acceptable. For reports, dashboards, and pipeline outputs, it is not. The billing team needs readings sorted by date. The capacity planning team needs reservoirs sorted by water level from lowest to highest. The data quality team needs outliers sorted by magnitude. ORDER BY is the clause that puts the result set into a defined, repeatable order.
Ascending and Descending

-- Sort by daily_kw ascending (lowest first) — the default
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15' AND mr.region = 'BERN'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw ASC
LIMIT  5;

-- Sort by daily_kw descending (highest first)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15' AND mr.region = 'BERN'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5;

ASC (ascending) sorts from smallest to largest for numbers, earliest to latest for dates, and A to Z for text. DESC (descending) reverses the order. ASC is the default — if you omit the direction keyword, the database sorts ascending. Combining ORDER BY with LIMIT creates the top-N pattern you saw in lesson 2 of this module: ORDER BY daily_kw DESC LIMIT 5 gives you the five highest readings. This pattern is one of the most commonly used in data engineering for outlier detection, ranking, and sampling.
Multi-Column Sorting

-- Sort by date (ascending), then by daily_kw (descending) within each date
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-03'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
ORDER BY mr.date ASC, mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  6;

Multi-column sorting works left to right: the database first sorts by date (ascending), then within each date, sorts by daily_kw (descending). Rows with the same date appear together, and within each date group, the highest consumption appears first. Each column in ORDER BY can have its own direction — ASC or DESC — independently. This lets you express complex sort orders like 'most recent dates first, and within each date, alphabetical by region.'
ORDER BY and Aliases

ORDER BY is the one clause that can reference column aliases defined in SELECT, because it executes after SELECT in the logical execution order (Module 1, lesson 7). If you write SELECT daily_kw AS consumption in the SELECT clause, you can sort by ORDER BY consumption DESC. This is convenient when sorting by computed expressions — instead of repeating the expression in ORDER BY, you alias it in SELECT and reference the alias. However, not all databases support alias references in ORDER BY (SQL Server does, BigQuery does, some MySQL versions do not). Using the original column name or expression always works.

blocks.title: ORDER BY is the only guarantee of order
blocks.content: Without ORDER BY, the database makes no promise about row order — even if the data appears sorted in your result, that order can change between executions, after a database restart, or when the query plan changes. If your pipeline's correctness depends on row order (for example, a downstream process that reads the first row as the latest reading), ORDER BY is mandatory. Never rely on implicit order.

The previous lessons taught WHERE and ORDER BY individually. In practice, almost every analytical query uses both — filter to the relevant subset, then sort the results for consumption. This lesson shows how WHERE and ORDER BY work together in the complete query structure, reinforces the execution order, and presents HydroAlpes' most common filter-and-sort patterns. By the end of this lesson, you can write queries that answer precise analytical questions: 'the top 10 residential consumers in ZURICH during January 2025, sorted by consumption descending.'
The Complete Structure

-- The full query structure with all clauses learned so far
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw        AS consumption_kwh,
       mr.date            AS reading_date,
       mr.customer_type   AS segment
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.region = 'ZURICH'
  AND  mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  10;

This query uses every concept from Modules 3 and 4. The SELECT clause chooses specific columns and aliases them for the billing team (Module 3). The FROM clause names the table with an alias (Module 3, lesson 9). The WHERE clause combines four AND conditions: region, customer type, and a date range using the half-open interval pattern (this module, lessons 1–5). The ORDER BY clause sorts by consumption descending (this module, lesson 8). The LIMIT clause restricts output to 10 rows (Module 3, lesson 7). Each clause plays a distinct role, and together they answer a precise analytical question.
Execution Order in Practice

Tracing the execution order for this query reinforces how the database processes it. Step 1 (FROM): locate the silver.meter_readings table — 128 million rows. Step 2 (WHERE): apply the four filter conditions, reducing to perhaps 800,000 rows (ZURICH residential customers in January). Step 3 (SELECT): compute the output columns, including the consumption_kwh and segment aliases. Step 4 (ORDER BY): sort the 800,000 surviving rows by daily_kw descending. Step 5 (LIMIT): return only the top 10 rows. The database may optimize this — it does not necessarily sort all 800,000 rows when it only needs the top 10 — but the logical order remains the same.
Common Filter-and-Sort Patterns

rows: WHERE conditions ORDER BY column DESC LIMIT N
WHERE entity = X ORDER BY date DESC LIMIT N
WHERE column > threshold ORDER BY column DESC
All readings above 500 kW, highest first
WHERE column IS NULL ORDER BY date DESC
WHERE date BETWEEN start AND end ORDER BY date, entity
Monthly consumption report sorted by date and meter

blocks.content: Each pattern combines filtering (narrow to the relevant rows) with sorting (arrange for the consumer's needs). The top-N pattern is the most common — it appears in dashboards, alerts, and reports throughout HydroAlpes' analytics. The data quality scan pattern is essential for pipeline monitoring — Lena runs it daily to find new meters that need substation mapping. The time-bounded report pattern is the foundation of every periodic summary the billing team receives.

blocks.title: Filter first, sort second
blocks.content: WHERE reduces the number of rows before ORDER BY sorts them. A query that sorts 128 million rows and then filters to 10,000 wastes enormous effort. A query that filters to 10,000 rows and then sorts is fast. The execution order (WHERE before ORDER BY) naturally does this — but understanding it explains why adding a WHERE clause to a slow query often makes it dramatically faster.

This module taught the tools for filtering and sorting: WHERE, comparison operators, AND, OR, NOT, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE, IS NULL, and ORDER BY. This final lesson synthesizes those tools into the data quality query patterns that HydroAlpes' team runs daily. Data quality is not a separate activity from querying — it is querying. Every data quality check is a SQL query that uses the filtering and sorting techniques from this module. A data engineer who can write precise WHERE clauses can diagnose any data quality issue directly at the database, without writing Python scripts or downloading files.
Finding Outliers

-- Outlier detection: readings outside the expected range
-- Residential meters rarely exceed 100 kW/day
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
  AND  mr.daily_kw > 100
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  10;

This query finds residential meters that reported more than 100 kW in a single day during January — a reading that is physically unlikely for a household. The results might indicate a meter malfunction, a wiring error, or a commercial property incorrectly classified as residential. Each flagged reading gives the data quality team a specific meter to investigate. The ORDER BY DESC puts the most extreme outliers at the top, directing attention to the most likely problems first.
Detecting Missing Data

-- Completeness check: meters missing substation mapping
SELECT m.meter_id, m.region, m.installation_date
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  m.substation_id IS NULL
ORDER BY m.installation_date DESC;

This query identifies every meter that has not been mapped to a substation — a required reference for regional reporting. Sorting by installation_date descending puts the most recently installed meters first, which are the most likely to be missing mapping (they have not been processed by the registration team yet). Lena runs this query every Monday morning and sends the results to the field operations team for action.
Identifying Duplicates

-- Preview: duplicate detection uses GROUP BY and HAVING (Module 5)
-- This query counts readings per meter per day
-- A count > 1 indicates a duplicate
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.date, COUNT(*) AS reading_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
GROUP BY mr.meter_id, mr.date
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY reading_count DESC
LIMIT  10;

This query previews techniques from Module 5 (GROUP BY and HAVING), but the pattern is important enough to mention here. Duplicate detection is one of the most critical data quality checks — a duplicate reading causes double-counting in every downstream aggregation. The query groups readings by meter and date (there should be exactly one reading per meter per day), then filters to groups with more than one row. The result shows which meters have duplicate readings and how many duplicates exist, sorted by severity.
Validating Value Ranges

-- Range validation: daily_kw should be non-negative for consumption meters
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.daily_kw < 0
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw ASC
LIMIT  10;

Negative consumption values are physically impossible for standard meters and indicate sensor malfunction or ingestion errors. This query finds them and shows the most negative values first. If the Silver table has a CHECK constraint (Module 2, lesson 6), this query should always return zero rows — the constraint prevents negative values from being inserted. Running the query periodically confirms that the constraint is working and that no pipeline has bypassed it.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 5, you will learn to aggregate data with GROUP BY and HAVING — transforming individual rows into summaries. The filtering skills from this module become even more powerful when combined with aggregation: filter to a specific region and time period (WHERE), group by customer type (GROUP BY), compute totals (SUM), and filter groups by their totals (HAVING). Every analytical report at HydroAlpes uses this combination.

chapters.title: Aggregations and Grouping
chapters.description: Master SQL's aggregation functions, understand GROUP BY as the mechanism that creates summary groups, and learn HAVING for filtering aggregated results.

blocks.content: Modules 3 and 4 taught you to retrieve, compute, filter, and sort individual rows. But many of the questions HydroAlpes needs answered are not about individual rows — they are about summaries. What is the total energy consumption across all meters this month? How many meters are active in each region? What is the average daily reading for industrial customers? These questions cannot be answered by looking at individual rows. They require the database to combine many rows into a single result — a total, a count, an average. This operation is called aggregation, and it is the bridge between raw data and business insight.
From Rows to Summaries

Consider HydroAlpes' meter_readings table on January 15, 2025: 350,000 rows, one per meter. The billing team does not want to see 350,000 individual readings — they want four numbers: total consumption for BERN, ZURICH, INNSBRUCK, and SALZBURG. Aggregation collapses the 350,000 rows into 4 rows, each representing the sum of all readings in one region. The detail is gone, replaced by a summary that answers the business question directly. This collapse — many rows becoming few rows — is the defining characteristic of aggregation.

blocks.title: Aggregation collapses rows into summaries
blocks.caption: Aggregation transforms many detail rows into few summary rows. The individual meter readings disappear, replaced by regional totals. This is the fundamental trade-off: detail for insight.

blocks.content: Aggregate Functions

SQL provides five core aggregate functions that cover the vast majority of summarization needs. COUNT counts rows. SUM adds up numeric values. AVG computes the arithmetic mean. MIN finds the smallest value. MAX finds the largest value. Each function takes a column (or expression) as input and produces a single value as output. When used without GROUP BY, they operate on all rows that pass the WHERE filter and return exactly one row. When used with GROUP BY — which you will learn in lesson 5 — they operate independently on each group and return one row per group.

-- Aggregate functions without GROUP BY: one result row
-- Summarize all January 2025 readings across all regions
SELECT COUNT(*)          AS total_readings,
       SUM(daily_kw)     AS total_kw,
       AVG(daily_kw)     AS avg_kw,
       MIN(daily_kw)     AS min_kw,
       MAX(daily_kw)     AS max_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01';

One row. Five summary values. The 10.85 million individual readings from January (350,000 meters × 31 days) have been collapsed into a single row of totals. COUNT(*) counted every row. SUM added up every daily_kw value. AVG computed the mean. MIN found the smallest reading (0.00 — a vacant building). MAX found the largest (2,847.30 kW — an industrial plant in Innsbruck). This is aggregation in its purest form: the entire dataset reduced to a handful of numbers that answer the question 'what happened in January?'

blocks.title: Aggregation is the bridge to business value
blocks.content: Raw rows are data. Aggregated results are information. Nobody at HydroAlpes makes a decision based on one meter's reading for one day. They make decisions based on regional totals, monthly trends, and customer-type averages. Every dashboard, every report, and every KPI is an aggregation. Learning to aggregate data with SQL is learning to turn data into the answers that drive business decisions.

COUNT is the simplest aggregate function and one of the most frequently used. It answers the most basic question about any dataset: how many? How many readings exist for January? How many meters are in the BERN region? How many distinct customer types appear in the data? COUNT has three forms, each counting something different, and the distinction between them is one of the most common sources of confusion for new SQL users — particularly when NULL values are involved.
COUNT(*) — Count All Rows

COUNT(*) counts all rows in the result set — no exceptions. It does not look at any specific column's value, so NULL values have no effect. If the meters table has 350,000 rows, COUNT(*) returns 350,000 even if some rows have NULL values in certain columns. This is the right function for answering 'how many rows exist?' It is the most common form of COUNT and the safest because it never surprises you with NULL-related behavior.
COUNT(column) — Count Non-NULL Values

-- COUNT(column) counts only non-NULL values in that column
SELECT COUNT(*)              AS total_meters,
       COUNT(substation_id)  AS mapped_meters
FROM   silver.meters;

COUNT(substation_id) returns 349,500 — five hundred fewer than COUNT(*). The difference tells you that 500 meters have a NULL substation_id (they have not been mapped to a substation yet). This distinction is enormously useful for data quality: comparing COUNT(*) against COUNT(column) for each column reveals exactly how many NULLs exist. At HydroAlpes, Lena runs this comparison as a daily health check on every Silver table.
COUNT(DISTINCT column)

-- COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique non-NULL values
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT region)        AS distinct_regions,
       COUNT(DISTINCT customer_type)  AS distinct_types,
       COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id)       AS distinct_meters
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';

COUNT(DISTINCT region) returns 4 — the number of unique regions. COUNT(DISTINCT customer_type) returns 3 — RESIDENTIAL, COMMERCIAL, and INDUSTRIAL. COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id) returns 350,000 — every meter reported on that day. DISTINCT inside COUNT eliminates duplicates before counting, giving you the cardinality (number of unique values) of a column. This is one of the most common exploratory queries in data engineering: understanding how many distinct entities exist in each categorical column helps you gauge data completeness and distribution.
The Three COUNTs Compared

rows: Includes rows with NULLs in any column
Excludes rows where the column is NULL

blocks.title: The COUNT trap
blocks.content: Writing COUNT(substation_id) when you mean COUNT(*) silently produces a lower number if substation_id contains NULLs. The query runs without errors, returns a plausible number, and you might never notice it is wrong. When counting total rows, always use COUNT(*). Use COUNT(column) only when you specifically want to know how many non-NULL values exist in that column.

COUNT tells you how many. SUM and AVG tell you how much. SUM adds up all values in a numeric column — the total consumption across all meters, the total generation across all turbines. AVG computes the arithmetic mean — the average daily reading per meter, the average reservoir level. Together with COUNT, they form the core trio of aggregate functions that appear in virtually every analytical query. This lesson covers their syntax, their NULL behavior, and a subtle but important difference in how AVG handles missing data.
SUM — Adding Values

-- Total consumption for January 2025, all regions
SELECT SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_consumption_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01';

SUM adds every non-NULL daily_kw value across all rows that pass the WHERE filter. If a row has daily_kw = NULL (a meter that did not report), SUM skips that row entirely — it does not treat NULL as zero. This is usually the correct behavior: a missing reading should not count as zero consumption. The result is the total energy consumed across all of HydroAlpes' meters during January 2025. SUM works only on numeric columns — attempting SUM on a text column produces an error.
AVG — Computing the Mean

-- Average daily consumption per reading in January 2025
SELECT AVG(mr.daily_kw)   AS avg_daily_kw,
       COUNT(*)            AS reading_count,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)    AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01';

AVG computes the mean by dividing the SUM of non-NULL values by the COUNT of non-NULL values. This is a critical detail: AVG uses only non-NULL values in both the numerator and the denominator. If 100 rows have daily_kw values of 10 each and 20 rows have NULL, AVG returns 10.0 (sum of 1,000 divided by 100), not 8.33 (1,000 divided by 120). This behavior is mathematically correct for 'average of known values' but can be misleading if NULLs represent zeros rather than missing data.
The AVG-NULL Subtlety

The difference between 'missing' and 'zero' determines whether AVG's NULL-skipping behavior produces the answer you want. At HydroAlpes, a NULL daily_kw means the meter did not report — the reading is genuinely unknown. AVG should skip it, because including an unknown value would distort the average. But in some contexts, NULL represents a real zero that was stored as NULL by mistake (or by convention). If a meter was offline and consumed zero energy but the pipeline stored NULL instead of 0.0, AVG's average will be higher than reality because it skips those 'zero' readings.

-- If NULLs mean 'zero consumption', use COALESCE to convert them
-- COALESCE returns the first non-NULL argument
SELECT AVG(mr.daily_kw)                   AS avg_excluding_nulls,
       AVG(COALESCE(mr.daily_kw, 0))       AS avg_treating_nulls_as_zero
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15';

The COALESCE function (which you will learn in detail in later modules) replaces NULL with a specified value. COALESCE(daily_kw, 0) returns daily_kw if it is not NULL, or 0 if it is. Wrapping the column in COALESCE before passing it to AVG forces the average to treat NULLs as zeros. The difference between the two averages tells you how much NULL-skipping affected the result. At HydroAlpes, the difference is small because few meters have missing readings, but in datasets with high NULL rates, the difference can be dramatic.

blocks.title: AVG = SUM / COUNT, but which COUNT?
blocks.content: AVG(daily_kw) equals SUM(daily_kw) / COUNT(daily_kw) — using the non-NULL count. It does not equal SUM(daily_kw) / COUNT(*). If your business needs the average over all rows (including NULLs as zeros), use SUM(daily_kw) / COUNT(*) instead of AVG(daily_kw). This explicit formula makes the denominator choice visible and reviewable.

MIN and MAX find the extreme values in a column — the smallest and largest. Unlike SUM and AVG, which work only on numeric columns, MIN and MAX work on any data type that supports ordering: numbers, text (alphabetical order), dates, and timestamps. This versatility makes them useful beyond simple 'find the biggest number' queries. At HydroAlpes, MIN and MAX answer questions like: when was the earliest reading recorded, what is the maximum turbine temperature observed this month, and which region code comes last alphabetically?
MIN and MAX on Numbers

-- Find the extreme consumption values for January 2025
SELECT MIN(mr.daily_kw) AS lowest_reading,
       MAX(mr.daily_kw) AS highest_reading
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01';

The lowest reading is 0.00 kW — a meter that recorded zero consumption, likely a vacant building. The highest is 2,847.30 kW — a large industrial facility. Both extremes are operationally interesting: very low readings might indicate meter faults, and very high readings might indicate industrial capacity limits. Like SUM and AVG, MIN and MAX ignore NULL values — they return the minimum or maximum of the non-NULL values. If every value in the column is NULL, they return NULL.
MIN and MAX on Dates

-- Find the date range of available readings
SELECT MIN(mr.date) AS earliest_reading,
       MAX(mr.date) AS latest_reading
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr;

MIN(date) returns the earliest date in the table and MAX(date) returns the latest. This is one of the first queries Markus runs when exploring a new table — it tells him the time span of available data immediately. If the earliest date is unexpectedly recent, data might be missing. If the latest date is yesterday, the pipeline is current. If the latest date is three weeks ago, the pipeline has stopped running. MIN and MAX on dates are essential diagnostic tools for any time-series data.
MIN and MAX on Text

When applied to text columns, MIN returns the value that comes first alphabetically and MAX returns the value that comes last. For HydroAlpes' region column, MIN(region) returns 'BERN' (B comes before I, S, Z) and MAX(region) returns 'ZURICH.' Text comparison follows the database's collation rules — which determine whether uppercase comes before lowercase, how accented characters sort, and other locale-specific behaviors. For HydroAlpes' uppercase-only data, the default collation works correctly.
Combining Aggregates

-- A comprehensive summary of January 2025 readings
SELECT COUNT(*)          AS reading_count,
       SUM(daily_kw)     AS total_kw,
       AVG(daily_kw)     AS avg_kw,
       MIN(daily_kw)     AS min_kw,
       MAX(daily_kw)     AS max_kw,
       MIN(date)         AS first_date,
       MAX(date)         AS last_date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01';

A single query can include any number of aggregate functions in the SELECT clause. Each function operates independently on the same set of rows — the rows that pass the WHERE filter. This comprehensive summary gives HydroAlpes' operations team a quick snapshot of January: how many readings, what total consumption, what the average was, and what the extremes were. This 'summary query' pattern is the starting point for most data exploration and is often the first query run against a new dataset.

blocks.title: The spread tells a story
blocks.content: The gap between MIN and MAX — the spread — often reveals more than the average. If MIN(daily_kw) is 0.00 and MAX is 2,847.30, the data spans a 2,847x range. This tells you the data contains both vacant buildings and heavy industry. The average of 1.46 kW hides this diversity. Reporting MIN, MAX, and AVG together gives a far richer picture than AVG alone.

The previous three lessons used aggregate functions on the entire result set — one total for all rows that passed the WHERE filter. But HydroAlpes rarely needs a single total. They need totals per region, averages per customer type, counts per date. GROUP BY is the clause that splits the result set into groups based on one or more columns and applies aggregate functions to each group independently. It is the mechanism that turns 'total consumption' into 'total consumption per region' and 'average reading' into 'average reading per customer type per month.' GROUP BY is arguably the most important clause in analytical SQL.
How GROUP BY Works

GROUP BY names one or more columns. The database sorts (or hashes) the rows into groups where every row in a group has the same values in the GROUP BY columns. Then it applies the aggregate functions to each group independently. The result has one row per group. If you group by region and there are four regions, the result has four rows. If you group by customer_type and there are three customer types, the result has three rows. The number of result rows equals the number of distinct combinations in the GROUP BY columns.

-- Total consumption per region for January 2025
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)         AS reading_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

Four rows — one per region. Each row shows the total consumption and the number of readings for that region in January. Zurich leads with 5.19 million kW across 3.41 million readings (roughly 110,000 meters × 31 days). The database created four groups (one per distinct region value), computed SUM(daily_kw) and COUNT(*) within each group, and returned the results. The WHERE filter ran before GROUP BY — only January readings were included in the groups.
The Fundamental Rule

When you use GROUP BY, every column in the SELECT clause must either appear in the GROUP BY clause or be inside an aggregate function. This rule exists because GROUP BY collapses many rows into one row per group, and the database needs to know what to do with each column. The region column is in GROUP BY, so the database knows its value is the same for every row in the group — it can show it directly. The daily_kw column is not in GROUP BY, so the database must aggregate it — SUM, AVG, COUNT, or another function. If you write SELECT region, daily_kw FROM ... GROUP BY region, the database has no instruction for daily_kw: should it show the first value, the last, the average? SQL does not guess — it rejects the query with an error.

-- WRONG: daily_kw is not in GROUP BY and not in an aggregate function
-- This query fails with: column "mr.daily_kw" must appear in the
-- GROUP BY clause or be used in an aggregate function
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.daily_kw     -- Error: what should this be for the group?
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
GROUP BY mr.region;

-- CORRECT: daily_kw is inside an aggregate function
SELECT mr.region,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) AS avg_kw    -- Clear: average of the group
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
GROUP BY mr.region;

This rule is strict and enforced by the database — your query will not run if it violates the rule. The error message is clear: 'column must appear in the GROUP BY clause or be used in an aggregate function.' When you see this error, check your SELECT list for any column that is neither grouped nor aggregated, then either add it to GROUP BY or wrap it in an aggregate function.

blocks.title: The most common GROUP BY mistake
blocks.content: New SQL users often add a column to GROUP BY just to silence the error, without considering whether grouping by that column changes the result. Adding meter_id to GROUP BY changes the result from 4 rows (one per region) to 350,000 rows (one per meter) — because each meter has a unique meter_id. The query runs without errors, but the aggregation is now per-meter rather than per-region. Always think about what you want the groups to be before adding columns to GROUP BY.

Grouping by a single column produces one row per unique value in that column — four rows when grouping by region, three when grouping by customer_type. But many analytical questions need finer granularity: total consumption per region per customer type, or average reading per region per month. Multi-column grouping creates one row for every unique combination of the grouped columns. If you group by region (4 values) and customer_type (3 values), you get up to 12 rows — one for each region-customer combination that exists in the data.
Two-Column Grouping

-- Total consumption per region per customer type, January 2025
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)         AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type
ORDER BY mr.region, mr.customer_type;

region    | customer_type | total_kw       | readings
----------+---------------+----------------+---------
BERN      | COMMERCIAL    |    982,451.30  |  310,000
BERN      | INDUSTRIAL    |  1,241,890.50  |   93,000
BERN      | RESIDENTIAL   |  2,299,549.40  |2,542,000
INNSBRUCK | COMMERCIAL    |    612,340.70  |  228,000
INNSBRUCK | INDUSTRIAL    |    891,021.30  |   68,200
INNSBRUCK | RESIDENTIAL   |  1,387,681.80  |1,873,800
SALZBURG  | COMMERCIAL    |    702,113.40  |  244,000
SALZBURG  | INDUSTRIAL    |    988,200.80  |   77,500
SALZBURG  | RESIDENTIAL   |  1,544,253.20  |2,003,500
ZURICH    | COMMERCIAL    |  1,122,890.20  |  384,000
ZURICH    | INDUSTRIAL    |  1,564,120.80  |  112,000
ZURICH    | RESIDENTIAL   |  2,500,329.50  |2,914,000

Twelve rows: 4 regions × 3 customer types. Each row represents a unique combination. BERN-RESIDENTIAL shows 2.3 million kW across 2.54 million readings. ZURICH-INDUSTRIAL shows 1.56 million kW across 112,000 readings. The billing team uses this exact breakdown to apply different rates per customer type per region. Without multi-column grouping, they would need to run 12 separate queries — one for each combination.
Three-Column Grouping

Adding a third column further subdivides the groups. Grouping by region, customer_type, and date produces one row per region per customer type per day. For January (31 days), that is 4 × 3 × 31 = 372 rows — a daily breakdown of consumption by region and customer type. Each additional GROUP BY column multiplies the number of groups by the number of distinct values in that column. This is the progressively finer grouping model: region gives you 4 groups, region + customer_type gives 12, region + customer_type + date gives 372.

-- Daily consumption per region per customer type (first 3 days)
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       mr.date,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS daily_total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-03'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type, mr.date
ORDER BY mr.date, mr.customer_type;

Nine rows: 3 customer types × 3 days, all in BERN. Each row is a daily total for one customer type in one region. Residential consumption dominates at roughly 74,000 kW per day, industrial averages around 40,000, and commercial around 32,000. This daily granularity by category and region is exactly what HydroAlpes' Gold-layer tables store for trend analysis, billing, and capacity planning.

blocks.title: Granularity determines usefulness
blocks.content: Grouping by fewer columns gives you a big-picture summary (4 rows, one per region). Grouping by more columns gives you detailed breakdowns (372 rows for daily-region-type). The right granularity depends on the consumer: a CEO dashboard might need 4 rows, a billing system needs 372. Choose GROUP BY columns based on the question being asked, not on what looks interesting.

WHERE filters individual rows before grouping. But sometimes you need to filter after grouping — keeping only groups that meet a condition on the aggregated result. Which regions had total consumption above 4 million kW in January? Which customer types have more than 1 million readings? These questions cannot be answered with WHERE because the condition depends on the aggregated value, which does not exist until after GROUP BY runs. HAVING is the clause that filters groups based on aggregate conditions, and it is the final piece of the aggregation puzzle.
WHERE vs HAVING

The distinction is execution timing. WHERE runs at step 2 — before GROUP BY — and filters individual rows based on their column values. HAVING runs at step 4 — after GROUP BY — and filters groups based on aggregated values. WHERE cannot reference aggregate functions (SUM, COUNT, AVG) because those values have not been computed yet. HAVING can reference aggregate functions because the groups and their aggregates already exist. WHERE reduces the number of rows entering the grouping. HAVING reduces the number of groups in the result.

-- Find regions where total January consumption exceeded 4 million kW
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region
HAVING SUM(mr.daily_kw) > 4000000
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

Four regions entered the GROUP BY. The HAVING clause filtered out SALZBURG (3.23M kW) and INNSBRUCK (2.89M kW) because their totals did not exceed 4 million. Only ZURICH and BERN survived. Notice that the HAVING condition uses SUM(mr.daily_kw), not the alias total_kw — because HAVING executes before SELECT computes the aliases (Module 1, lesson 7). PostgreSQL actually does allow aliases in HAVING, but most other databases do not, so using the full expression is the portable habit.
The Complete Execution Flow

blocks.title: How WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, and SELECT interact
blocks.content:
FROM   silver.meter_readings       128,000,000 rows
  │
  ▼
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01'         10,850,000 rows (January only)
       date <  '2025-02-01'
  │
  ▼
GROUP BY region                     4 groups
  │                                 (BERN, ZURICH, INNSBRUCK, SALZBURG)
  ▼
HAVING SUM(daily_kw) > 4000000      2 groups survive
  │                                 (BERN, ZURICH)
  ▼
SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw)        2 rows with computed columns
  │
  ▼
ORDER BY total_kw DESC              2 rows, sorted
blocks.caption: WHERE reduces rows before grouping. GROUP BY creates groups. HAVING filters groups after aggregation. SELECT computes the output. ORDER BY sorts the final result.

blocks.content: Common HAVING Patterns

-- Regions with more than 3 million readings in January
SELECT mr.region,
       COUNT(*) AS reading_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3000000;

-- Customer types with average consumption above 2 kW
SELECT mr.customer_type,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) AS avg_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
GROUP BY mr.customer_type
HAVING AVG(mr.daily_kw) > 2.0;

HAVING can use any aggregate function — COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX — and any comparison operator. You can also combine multiple HAVING conditions with AND and OR, just like WHERE. The pattern is always the same: GROUP BY creates groups, HAVING filters them based on aggregate values. If the condition can be evaluated on individual rows (like region = 'BERN'), it belongs in WHERE. If it can only be evaluated after aggregation (like SUM(daily_kw) > 4000000), it belongs in HAVING.

blocks.title: Do not use HAVING for row-level filters
blocks.content: Putting a row-level condition in HAVING instead of WHERE produces correct results but wastes performance. HAVING SUM(daily_kw) > 0 WHERE region = 'BERN' is correct. But writing HAVING region = 'BERN' instead of WHERE region = 'BERN' forces the database to group all rows from all regions before discarding three of the four groups. WHERE eliminates the unwanted rows before grouping, so the database has less work to do. Use WHERE for row filters, HAVING for group filters.

Module 2 (lesson 9) and Module 4 (lesson 7) covered how NULL behaves in comparisons and filters. Aggregation introduces a new dimension to the NULL problem: how do aggregate functions handle NULL values when they appear in the column being aggregated? The short answer — all aggregate functions except COUNT(*) ignore NULLs — is simple enough to state, but its implications for data engineering are subtle and consequential. A SUM that ignores NULLs might undercount if NULLs represent zeros. An AVG that ignores NULLs might overestimate if NULLs represent low values. Understanding these implications is essential for producing correct aggregations.
The Rules

rows: Counts all rows, including those with NULLs in any column
Always returns the true row count
Counts only rows where column is not NULL
Returns a lower count if NULLs exist
Total may be lower if NULLs represent real values
Averages only non-NULL values (both numerator and denominator)
Average may be higher if NULLs represent low values
Correct unless NULLs represent a lower value

blocks.content: Detecting NULL Impact

-- Detect how many NULLs exist in daily_kw for January 2025
SELECT COUNT(*)            AS total_rows,
       COUNT(daily_kw)     AS non_null_readings,
       COUNT(*) - COUNT(daily_kw) AS null_readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01';

The difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(daily_kw) reveals that 350 rows have NULL daily_kw values in January. These are meters that did not report. SUM(daily_kw) excluded those 350 readings, and AVG(daily_kw) used 10,849,650 as the denominator instead of 10,850,000. With only 350 NULLs out of 10.85 million rows, the impact is negligible. But in datasets with higher NULL rates — sensor networks with unreliable connectivity, external feeds with frequent gaps — the impact can be significant.
NULL in GROUP BY

When a GROUP BY column contains NULLs, all NULL values are grouped together into a single group. If you group by substation_id and 500 meters have NULL substation IDs, those 500 meters form one group where the substation_id column shows NULL. This behavior is useful — it lets you see the aggregated metrics for unassigned meters — but it can also be misleading if you do not expect it. A report showing 86 substations might actually show 85 real substations plus one NULL group, and treating the NULL group as a real substation produces incorrect results.

-- NULLs form their own group in GROUP BY
SELECT m.substation_id,
       COUNT(*) AS meter_count
FROM   silver.meters AS m
GROUP BY m.substation_id
ORDER BY meter_count DESC
LIMIT  5;

substation_id | meter_count
--------------+------------
SUB-ZRCH-01   |      12,340
SUB-BERN-04   |      11,890
SUB-SLZB-01   |      10,760
SUB-INNB-02   |      10,210
(null)         |         500

The last row shows that 500 meters have no assigned substation — they form the NULL group. This is exactly the data quality information Lena needs: 500 meters need substation mapping. But a downstream query that counts the rows in this result and reports '86 substations' would be wrong — there are only 85 real substations plus one NULL group. When GROUP BY produces a NULL group, verify whether downstream consumers need to handle it specially.

blocks.title: The NULL audit query
blocks.content: Run SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(column) for every column to see the NULL rate across the table. At HydroAlpes, Lena runs this as a daily health check: SELECT COUNT(*) AS rows, COUNT(meter_id) AS meter_id_present, COUNT(daily_kw) AS daily_kw_present, COUNT(substation_id) AS substation_present FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE date = CURRENT_DATE - 1. Any sudden drop in a column's count signals a pipeline issue or a source problem.

Aggregation produces summary rows, and sorting arranges them for consumption. The two work together naturally: aggregate to produce totals or averages, then sort to put the most important groups first. The combination of GROUP BY with ORDER BY on an aggregate column is the foundation of every ranked report: top regions by consumption, top meters by reading count, bottom substations by availability. This lesson shows how to sort aggregated results and presents the complete query structure that includes every clause learned so far.
Sorting by Aggregate Columns

-- Regions ranked by total consumption, highest first
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)   AS total_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw)   AS avg_kw,
       COUNT(*)           AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

ORDER BY total_kw DESC sorts the four groups by total consumption, highest first. Notice that ORDER BY uses the alias total_kw defined in SELECT — this works because ORDER BY executes after SELECT in the logical execution order. You could also write ORDER BY SUM(mr.daily_kw) DESC, repeating the expression, which is equivalent but less readable. The alias approach is preferred when it is supported by the database.
The Complete Query Structure

With Modules 3–5 complete, you now know every clause in the standard analytical query. The full structure, in written order, is: SELECT (choose columns and aggregates), FROM (name the table), WHERE (filter rows before grouping), GROUP BY (create groups), HAVING (filter groups after aggregation), ORDER BY (sort the final result), LIMIT (restrict the number of rows). Each clause is optional except SELECT and FROM, and they must appear in this order. The database executes them in a different order — FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, SELECT, ORDER BY, LIMIT — as you learned in Module 1.

-- The complete analytical query with every clause
SELECT   mr.region,
         mr.customer_type,
         SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
         COUNT(*)         AS readings
FROM     silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE    mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND    mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type
HAVING   SUM(mr.daily_kw) > 500000
ORDER BY total_kw DESC
LIMIT    5;

region | customer_type | total_kw       | readings
-------+---------------+----------------+---------
ZURICH | RESIDENTIAL   |  2,500,329.50  | 2,914,000
BERN   | RESIDENTIAL   |  2,299,549.40  | 2,542,000
ZURICH | INDUSTRIAL    |  1,564,120.80  |   112,000
SALZBURG| RESIDENTIAL  |  1,544,253.20  | 2,003,500
INNSBRUCK| RESIDENTIAL |  1,387,681.80  | 1,873,800

This query uses every clause: SELECT chooses the output. FROM names the table. WHERE filters to January. GROUP BY creates region-customer groups. HAVING keeps only groups with more than 500,000 kW total. ORDER BY ranks the surviving groups by total consumption. LIMIT keeps the top 5. The result shows that residential customers in Zurich and Bern are the two largest consumption groups, followed by Zurich industrial and Salzburg residential. This is the kind of ranked, filtered, aggregated report that powers HydroAlpes' executive dashboards.

blocks.title: The analytical query template
blocks.content: Almost every analytical query you write will follow this template: filter rows (WHERE), group by dimensions (GROUP BY), aggregate measures (SUM, AVG, COUNT), filter groups (HAVING), and sort (ORDER BY). The specific columns, conditions, and functions change, but the structure remains the same. Once you internalize this template, you can write any aggregation query by filling in the blanks.

This module taught aggregation from the ground up: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP BY, multi-column grouping, HAVING, NULL behavior, and sorting aggregated results. This final lesson brings those techniques together into the reporting queries that HydroAlpes' teams use daily. Each query is realistic — these are the kinds of summaries that billing teams, operations teams, and executives actually consume. By writing and understanding these queries, you are doing exactly what a junior data engineer does in the first months on the job.
Monthly Billing Summary

-- Gold-layer billing summary: monthly totals per region per customer type
-- This query feeds the billing team's invoice generation system
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)        AS total_consumption_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) * 0.001 AS total_consumption_mwh,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) AS active_meters,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw)        AS avg_daily_reading
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type
ORDER BY mr.region, mr.customer_type;

This query produces the monthly billing summary that HydroAlpes sends to each regional office. It groups by region and customer type (12 rows), computes total consumption in both kW and MWh (using an expression inside SUM), counts the number of distinct active meters, and computes the average daily reading. The ORDER BY ensures consistent ordering for the downstream system that reads this data. This is the kind of query that runs on the first of every month as part of the Gold-layer pipeline.
Turbine Performance Summary

-- Daily turbine performance: average output and max temperature
-- Operations team uses this to schedule maintenance
SELECT tt.turbine_id,
       AVG(tt.power_output)  AS avg_power_kw,
       MAX(tt.temp)          AS max_temp_c,
       COUNT(*)              AS reading_count
FROM   silver.turbine_telemetry AS tt
WHERE  tt.ts >= '2025-01-15 00:00:00'
  AND  tt.ts <  '2025-01-16 00:00:00'
GROUP BY tt.turbine_id
HAVING MAX(tt.temp) > 80
ORDER BY max_temp_c DESC
LIMIT  10;

This query identifies the turbines that exceeded 80°C on January 15 — a threshold that triggers a maintenance review. It groups telemetry readings by turbine, computes the average power output and maximum temperature, and uses HAVING to filter to only the turbines that exceeded the threshold. The ORDER BY puts the hottest turbines first, directing the operations team's attention to the most urgent cases. This is the kind of operational alert query that runs daily and feeds an exception report.
Top-Consuming Meters

-- Top 10 consuming meters in January 2025
-- Capacity planning team uses this for demand forecasting
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)   AS monthly_total_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw)   AS avg_daily_kw,
       COUNT(*)           AS days_reported
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.meter_id, mr.region, mr.customer_type
ORDER BY monthly_total_kw DESC
LIMIT  10;

This query groups by meter_id (plus region and customer_type, which are functionally dependent on the meter but required by the GROUP BY rule) and computes each meter's monthly total, daily average, and number of reporting days. Sorting by monthly total descending and limiting to 10 gives the capacity planning team the highest-demand meters. If a meter reports fewer than 31 days (visible in the days_reported column), its monthly total is understated — a data quality insight that comes for free alongside the business metric.
Data Completeness Check

-- How many meters reported each day in January?
-- A sudden drop indicates a pipeline failure or source outage
SELECT mr.date,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) AS meters_reporting
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.date
ORDER BY mr.date;

This query produces 31 rows — one per day in January — showing how many distinct meters reported each day. If the count is consistently around 350,000 and then drops to 280,000 on January 18, something went wrong on that day: a pipeline failure, a source outage, or a data quality issue. This daily completeness check is one of the most valuable monitoring queries in any data engineering platform. Lena has it scheduled as an Airflow task that alerts the team when the count drops below 340,000.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 6, you will learn joins — the operation that combines data from multiple tables. Every reporting query in this lesson operated on a single table. Joins let you enrich meter readings with substation names, turbine telemetry with reservoir information, and consumption data with energy prices. Aggregation combined with joins is the complete foundation of analytical SQL — and by the end of Module 6, you will have both.

chapters.title: Joins — Combining Tables
chapters.description: Master every SQL join type, understand how joins combine tables through matching keys, and learn to handle the column ambiguity and data loss problems that joins create.

blocks.content: Every module so far has queried a single table. Module 3 selected columns from meter_readings. Module 4 filtered rows in meter_readings. Module 5 aggregated meter_readings by region and customer type. But HydroAlpes has nine tables (Module 2, lesson 8), and the most valuable insights come from combining them: meter readings enriched with substation names, turbine telemetry linked to reservoir information, consumption data matched with energy prices. This module teaches joins — the SQL operation that connects rows from different tables based on matching key values. Joins are the most powerful and most commonly misunderstood operation in SQL, and mastering them is what transforms a data engineer from someone who can query a table into someone who can query a data model.
Why Not One Giant Table?

If combining tables is so important, why not store everything in one table? Imagine merging meter_readings with meters, substations, and regions into a single flat table. Every row would contain the reading (meter_id, daily_kw, date) plus the meter metadata (customer_type, installation_date) plus the substation details (substation_name, capacity_mw) plus the region information (region_name, country). For 350,000 daily readings, the substation name 'Alpine Grid Station Bern-4' would be repeated in every row that belongs to that substation — hundreds of thousands of identical copies. If the substation's capacity changes, you would need to update every row that references it. If one row has a typo in the substation name, it becomes inconsistent with the others.
Normalization

The practice of separating data into multiple related tables is called normalization. Each table stores one type of entity: meters in the meters table, substations in the substations table, readings in the meter_readings table. Information about each entity is stored exactly once. The substation name 'Alpine Grid Station Bern-4' appears in one row of the substations table, not in millions of rows of meter_readings. When the substation's capacity changes, you update one row. Consistency is automatic because there is only one copy to update. This design eliminates redundancy, prevents inconsistency, and makes storage efficient.

blocks.title: Denormalized (one table) vs normalized (separate tables)
blocks.caption: Normalization stores each fact once and connects tables through keys. Joins reconstruct the combined view when needed — without the redundancy, inconsistency, and storage waste of the flat approach.

visual-recommendation.description: A side-by-side comparison showing a denormalized flat table with highlighted redundant cells versus normalized tables connected by foreign key arrows, emphasizing storage efficiency and consistency.

blocks.content: Joins Reconnect What Normalization Separated

Normalization separates data for storage efficiency and consistency. Joins reconnect it for analytical use. When the billing team needs meter readings with substation names and region details, a join combines meter_readings with substations and regions into a single result set — as if the data had been stored in one table. The join produces the combined view on demand, without the storage and consistency costs of actually storing it that way. This is the fundamental bargain of relational databases: store data normalized (separate tables, no redundancy), query it denormalized (joined tables, complete picture).

The foreign keys you learned in Module 2 (lesson 5) are the roadmap for joins. Each foreign key relationship — meter_readings.meter_id references meters.meter_id, meters.substation_id references substations.substation_id — tells you which columns to use when joining two tables. In this module, you will learn every type of join SQL offers, starting with the most common (INNER JOIN) and progressing through LEFT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN, CROSS JOIN, self-joins, and multi-table joins. By the end, you will be able to combine any of HydroAlpes' nine tables into the analytical views that power dashboards, reports, and billing.

blocks.title: Joins are the core of data engineering SQL
blocks.content: Single-table queries are exploration. Multi-table queries are production. Almost every Gold-layer table at HydroAlpes is produced by a query that joins fact tables with dimension tables. Learning joins is the transition from 'I can look at one table' to 'I can build analytical datasets from the entire data model.' This module is the most important in the course.

An INNER JOIN combines rows from two tables where the join key matches in both tables. If a meter reading has a meter_id that exists in the meters table, the two rows are combined into one result row that contains columns from both tables. If a meter reading has a meter_id that does not exist in the meters table, that reading is excluded from the result. If a meter exists in the meters table but has no readings, that meter is also excluded. INNER JOIN keeps only the rows where both sides have a match — the intersection of the two tables based on the join key.
The Syntax

-- Join meter readings with meter metadata
-- Only readings where the meter exists in the meters table are returned
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       m.customer_type,
       m.installation_date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

The result contains columns from both tables: meter_id, daily_kw, and date come from meter_readings, while customer_type and installation_date come from meters. The ON clause specifies the join condition: match rows where meter_readings.meter_id equals meters.meter_id. For each reading, the database finds the matching meter row and combines them. If no match exists, the reading is excluded. The INNER keyword is optional — JOIN without a prefix defaults to INNER JOIN in SQL — but including it makes the join type explicit and readable.
How INNER JOIN Works

blocks.title: INNER JOIN: only matching rows survive
blocks.caption: INNER JOIN keeps only rows where the key matches in both tables. M-999 (reading without a meter) and M-004 (meter without a reading) are both excluded from the result.

blocks.content: The ON Clause

The ON clause defines which columns the join matches on. ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id tells the database to pair rows where the meter_id values are equal. The ON clause can include multiple conditions joined with AND: ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id AND mr.region = m.region — though single-column joins are far more common. The columns in the ON clause do not need to have the same name (you could join ON mr.meter_id = m.id if the meters table used a different column name), but they must have compatible types.
When Rows Disappear

The most important property of INNER JOIN is that unmatched rows are silently excluded. If 500 meters in meter_readings have not been registered in the meters table yet, those 500 meters' readings vanish from the result. The query returns no error and no warning — it simply has fewer rows than expected. At HydroAlpes, this is the reason Lena requires a COUNT(*) check after every INNER JOIN in the pipeline: compare the reading count before and after the join to detect unexpected row loss. If the count drops by 500, those 500 meters need investigation.

blocks.title: Silent data loss in INNER JOIN
blocks.content: INNER JOIN drops unmatched rows without warning. If your meter_readings table has 350,000 rows for one day and the join result has only 349,500, the 500 missing rows had meter_ids that do not exist in the meters table. This is the single most common data quality issue with joins. Always verify row counts before and after a join, especially in production pipelines.

sections.title: LEFT JOIN — Keeping All Left Rows

blocks.content: INNER JOIN excludes unmatched rows from both tables. But often you want to keep all rows from one table even when they have no match in the other. HydroAlpes needs all meter readings in the result, even for meters that have not been registered yet — the readings are real data that should not disappear just because the meter metadata is incomplete. LEFT JOIN solves this: it keeps every row from the left table (the one in the FROM clause) and fills in NULLs for unmatched columns from the right table (the one after JOIN). LEFT JOIN is the most commonly used join type in data engineering because it preserves all source data.
The Syntax

-- LEFT JOIN: keep ALL readings, even when meter metadata is missing
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       m.customer_type,          -- NULL if no match in meters
       m.installation_date       -- NULL if no match in meters
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY m.customer_type NULLS LAST
LIMIT  5;

The last row shows meter CH-BERN-99801 with NULL customer_type and NULL installation_date. This meter has a reading (3.21 kW) but does not exist in the meters table yet — it was recently installed and has not been registered. With INNER JOIN, this reading would have vanished. With LEFT JOIN, the reading is preserved, and the missing metadata columns are filled with NULL. This is exactly what HydroAlpes' pipeline needs: keep all readings, flag the ones missing metadata, and send the list of unregistered meters to the field team.
How LEFT JOIN Works

blocks.title: LEFT JOIN: all left rows survive, unmatched get NULLs
blocks.caption: LEFT JOIN preserves every row from the left table. Unmatched rows get NULLs for the right table's columns. Rows only in the right table (M-004) are excluded.

blocks.content: Finding Unmatched Rows

A powerful pattern built on LEFT JOIN: join two tables and filter to rows where the right side is NULL. This finds rows in the left table that have no match in the right table — orphan rows. At HydroAlpes, this pattern identifies meters with readings but no metadata, turbines with telemetry but no registration, and substations referenced by meters but missing from the substations table.

-- Find readings for unregistered meters (readings without metadata)
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  m.meter_id IS NULL          -- right side is NULL = no match
  AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY mr.meter_id;

The WHERE m.meter_id IS NULL condition keeps only rows where the LEFT JOIN found no match in the meters table. These are readings for meters that have not been registered. This is the anti-join pattern — finding what exists in one table but not in another — and it is one of the most useful data quality tools in SQL. You will see a more formal anti-join using NOT EXISTS in Module 7, but the LEFT JOIN plus IS NULL approach is the most commonly used version in practice.

blocks.title: LEFT JOIN is the safe default
blocks.content: When in doubt about which join type to use, start with LEFT JOIN. It preserves all your source data and makes missing matches visible (as NULLs) rather than silently dropping rows. You can always filter out NULLs later if you want INNER JOIN behavior. But recovering silently dropped rows from an INNER JOIN requires rewriting the query. At HydroAlpes, LEFT JOIN is the default in all Silver-to-Gold pipelines.

sections.title: RIGHT JOIN and FULL OUTER JOIN

blocks.content: LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table. RIGHT JOIN is the mirror: it keeps all rows from the right table and fills NULLs for unmatched left-side columns. FULL OUTER JOIN keeps all rows from both tables — matched rows are combined, and unmatched rows from either side get NULLs. These two join types are less common than INNER and LEFT, but FULL OUTER JOIN is essential for one specific data engineering task: reconciliation — comparing two datasets to find what exists in one but not the other.
RIGHT JOIN

RIGHT JOIN keeps every row from the right table, filling NULLs for columns from the left table when no match exists. In practice, RIGHT JOIN is rarely used because it can always be rewritten as a LEFT JOIN by swapping the table order. SELECT ... FROM meter_readings RIGHT JOIN meters ON ... is equivalent to SELECT ... FROM meters LEFT JOIN meter_readings ON ... — the tables are simply listed in reverse order. Most SQL style guides recommend using LEFT JOIN consistently rather than mixing LEFT and RIGHT, because consistent direction makes queries easier to read.
FULL OUTER JOIN

-- Reconciliation: find meters that exist in only one table
-- FULL OUTER JOIN keeps ALL rows from both tables
SELECT COALESCE(mr.meter_id, m.meter_id) AS meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       m.customer_type,
       CASE
         WHEN mr.meter_id IS NULL THEN 'In meters only (no readings)'
         WHEN m.meter_id  IS NULL THEN 'In readings only (unregistered)'
         ELSE 'In both tables'
       END AS match_status
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  FULL OUTER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
   AND mr.date = '2025-01-15'
WHERE  mr.meter_id IS NULL
    OR m.meter_id  IS NULL
ORDER BY match_status, meter_id
LIMIT  8;

meter_id       | daily_kw | customer_type | match_status
---------------+----------+---------------+------------------------------
CH-BERN-99801  |     3.21 | (null)        | In readings only (unregistered)
CH-BERN-99802  |     4.55 | (null)        | In readings only (unregistered)
AT-SLZB-99901  |     2.87 | (null)        | In readings only (unregistered)
CH-ZRCH-00999  |  (null)  | RESIDENTIAL   | In meters only (no readings)
AT-INNB-00888  |  (null)  | COMMERCIAL    | In meters only (no readings)
CH-BERN-00777  |  (null)  | RESIDENTIAL   | In meters only (no readings)
CH-ZRCH-00666  |  (null)  | INDUSTRIAL    | In meters only (no readings)
AT-SLZB-00555  |  (null)  | RESIDENTIAL   | In meters only (no readings)

The FULL OUTER JOIN produces three categories of rows: meters with both readings and metadata (matched — filtered out by the WHERE clause here), meters with readings but no metadata (unregistered — NULL on the right side), and meters with metadata but no readings (inactive or faulty — NULL on the left side). The COALESCE function picks the non-NULL meter_id from whichever side has it, and the CASE expression classifies each row. This reconciliation query is how HydroAlpes audits data completeness: every meter should appear in both tables, and any mismatch requires investigation.
When to Use Each Join Type

rows: When you need data from both tables and unmatched rows are irrelevant
Billing report (only billed meters need metadata)
All left rows, NULLs for unmatched right
When the left table is the primary data and you want to enrich it
All readings with optional substation info
All right rows, NULLs for unmatched left
Rarely used — rewrite as LEFT JOIN
Equivalent to LEFT JOIN with swapped table order
Reconciliation: finding what exists in only one table
Audit: which meters are missing readings or metadata?

blocks.title: The 80/20 of join types
blocks.content: In practice, LEFT JOIN accounts for roughly 60% of joins in data engineering queries, INNER JOIN for roughly 30%, FULL OUTER JOIN for roughly 8%, and RIGHT JOIN for less than 2%. If you master LEFT JOIN and INNER JOIN, you can handle the vast majority of production queries. FULL OUTER JOIN appears mainly in data quality and reconciliation scripts. RIGHT JOIN is almost never the best choice.

Every join type so far uses an ON clause to match rows by key. CROSS JOIN is different: it produces every possible pair of rows from two tables, with no matching condition at all. If the left table has 4 rows and the right table has 31 rows, CROSS JOIN produces 4 × 31 = 124 rows — every combination. This sounds dangerous on large tables (350,000 meters × 365 days = 127.75 million rows), and it is. But CROSS JOIN has one essential use case in data engineering: generating scaffold tables that represent every expected combination, which you then compare against actual data to find gaps.
The Syntax

-- Generate every date-region combination for January 2025
-- This scaffold will be used to find missing data
SELECT d.date,
       r.region_id AS region
FROM   (
         SELECT generate_series('2025-01-01'::date,
                                '2025-01-31'::date,
                                '1 day'::interval)::date AS date
       ) AS d
  CROSS JOIN silver.regions AS r
ORDER BY d.date, r.region_id
LIMIT  8;

The result is a scaffold: every date in January paired with every region. Thirty-one days × 4 regions = 124 rows. No row represents actual data — it represents an expected combination. By LEFT JOINing this scaffold with the actual meter readings (grouped by date and region), you can find which date-region combinations have no data — the gaps in your pipeline. A missing combination means no meters in that region reported on that day, which signals a pipeline failure or a source outage.
The Gap Detection Pattern

-- Find date-region combinations with no meter readings
-- Step 1: scaffold (all expected combinations)
-- Step 2: LEFT JOIN with actual data
-- Step 3: filter to where actual data is NULL (gaps)
WITH scaffold AS (
  SELECT d.date, r.region_id AS region
  FROM   generate_series('2025-01-01'::date,
                          '2025-01-31'::date,
                          '1 day'::interval) AS d(date)
    CROSS JOIN silver.regions AS r
),
actual AS (
  SELECT mr.date, mr.region, COUNT(*) AS readings
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
  GROUP BY mr.date, mr.region
)
SELECT s.date, s.region
FROM   scaffold AS s
  LEFT JOIN actual AS a
    ON s.date = a.date AND s.region = a.region
WHERE  a.date IS NULL
ORDER BY s.date, s.region;

This query uses a CTE (WITH clause, which you will learn formally in Module 7) to build the scaffold and the actual data, then LEFT JOINs them and filters to gaps. If the result is empty, every date-region combination has at least one reading — the pipeline is complete. If rows appear, those date-region combinations have zero readings and need investigation. This is one of the most valuable data quality patterns in production data engineering, and CROSS JOIN is its foundation.

blocks.title: CROSS JOIN and table size
blocks.content: CROSS JOIN produces N × M rows. With small dimension tables (4 regions, 31 dates), this is fine — 124 rows. With large tables (350,000 meters × 365 days), the result is 127.75 million rows — potentially overwhelming memory and taking minutes to execute. Never CROSS JOIN two large tables without understanding the result size. The safe pattern is: CROSS JOIN small dimension tables to build scaffolds, then LEFT JOIN with large fact tables.

Every join so far has combined two different tables. A self-join combines a table with itself — using two aliases to treat the same table as two separate entities. This sounds unusual, but it solves a specific category of problems: comparing rows within the same table. HydroAlpes needs to compare each meter's today reading with its yesterday reading, find turbines in the same reservoir with different performance levels, and detect consecutive days where a meter's consumption changed dramatically. Each of these requires comparing one row of a table with another row of the same table — which is exactly what a self-join does.
Why Self-Joins Are Needed

In Python, comparing two rows of the same dataset is straightforward: access row[i] and row[i-1]. In SQL, rows have no position — there is no concept of 'the previous row' without explicit instructions. A self-join provides those instructions by giving the table two aliases and joining them on a condition that pairs the rows you want to compare. The table appears twice in the FROM clause, each with a different alias, and the ON clause defines how the two copies relate to each other.

-- Compare each meter's reading today vs yesterday
-- Self-join: meter_readings appears twice with different aliases
SELECT today.meter_id,
       today.daily_kw   AS today_kw,
       yesterday.daily_kw AS yesterday_kw,
       today.daily_kw - yesterday.daily_kw AS change_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS today
  INNER JOIN silver.meter_readings AS yesterday
    ON today.meter_id = yesterday.meter_id
   AND today.date = yesterday.date + INTERVAL '1 day'
WHERE  today.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  today.region = 'BERN'
ORDER BY ABS(today.daily_kw - yesterday.daily_kw) DESC
LIMIT  5;

The table meter_readings appears twice: once as today and once as yesterday. The ON clause pairs rows with the same meter_id where today's date is exactly one day after yesterday's date. The result shows each meter's reading on January 15 alongside its reading on January 14, with the change computed. Meter CH-BERN-65004 increased by 666.40 kW — an industrial facility that ramped up production. Meters 00142 and 00001 decreased — normal residential fluctuation. Sorting by absolute change puts the most dramatic shifts first.
When Self-Joins Are Appropriate

Self-joins solve the 'compare rows within the same table' problem, but they can be expensive — the database must match every row in the table against potentially many other rows in the same table. For the day-over-day comparison above, the join is efficient because the composite condition (same meter, consecutive dates) narrows the match to exactly one pair per meter. For looser conditions (all pairs of turbines in the same reservoir), the result set can grow rapidly. In Module 8, you will learn window functions (LAG and LEAD), which solve many of the same problems more efficiently. Self-joins remain the right choice when window functions cannot express the required logic.

blocks.title: Aliases are mandatory for self-joins
blocks.content: When a table appears twice in the FROM clause, the database cannot distinguish which instance you mean without aliases. SELECT meter_id FROM meter_readings JOIN meter_readings ON ... is ambiguous — which meter_readings does meter_id come from? Aliases resolve this: FROM meter_readings AS today JOIN meter_readings AS yesterday makes every column reference unambiguous. Today.meter_id and yesterday.meter_id clearly refer to different instances.

sections.title: Join Conditions and the ON Clause

blocks.content: Every join so far used a simple equality condition: ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id. But the ON clause can express more complex relationships. Multi-column joins match on two or more columns simultaneously. Range joins match rows where a value falls within a range. Inequality joins match rows based on greater-than or less-than relationships. Understanding these variations lets you handle the real-world join scenarios that go beyond simple key matching — like matching energy readings to the price period they belong to, or assigning meter readings to the billing month they should be charged in.
Multi-Column Joins

-- Multi-column join: match on both meter_id AND date
-- Useful when joining two fact tables at the same grain
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       dq.is_valid,
       dq.quality_score
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.data_quality_flags AS dq
    ON mr.meter_id = dq.meter_id
   AND mr.date     = dq.date
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

The ON clause matches on two columns: meter_id and date. A row from meter_readings matches a row from data_quality_flags only when both the meter and the date are the same. This is necessary because both tables have the same composite grain (one row per meter per day), so a single-column match on meter_id alone would pair one meter's readings with quality flags from every day — creating a many-to-many explosion. Multi-column joins ensure the pairing is precise.
Range Joins

-- Range join: match readings to the energy price period they fall in
-- Energy prices change hourly; readings are daily — match to the day's price
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       ep.price_eur_mwh
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.energy_prices AS ep
    ON mr.date = ep.price_timestamp::date
   AND ep.market_zone = 'CH'
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

This join matches meter readings to energy prices by casting the price timestamp to a date and matching it against the reading date. The additional condition ep.market_zone = 'CH' filters to Swiss prices (since HydroAlpes operates in both Swiss and Austrian markets). Range joins are common in data engineering for temporal matching: matching events to the time period they belong to, matching records to the effective date of a slowly changing dimension, or assigning transactions to billing cycles.
ON vs WHERE for Filter Conditions

A condition in the ON clause and a condition in the WHERE clause behave differently for outer joins. For INNER JOIN, they produce the same result — the database applies both conditions to determine which rows to keep. For LEFT JOIN, an ON condition filters the right table before the join, while a WHERE condition filters the result after the join. If you put ep.market_zone = 'CH' in the ON clause of a LEFT JOIN, readings without a matching Swiss price get NULLs but are still included. If you put it in the WHERE clause, those NULL rows are filtered out — effectively converting the LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN for that condition.

left.label: Condition in ON (outer join)
points: Filters the right table before joining
Unmatched left rows get NULLs and survive
Preserves LEFT JOIN behavior
Use for conditions on the right table

right.label: Condition in WHERE (outer join)
points: Filters the result after joining
