Unmatched left rows (with NULLs) are excluded
Effectively converts LEFT JOIN to INNER JOIN
Use for conditions on the left table or the joined result

blocks.title: The accidental INNER JOIN
blocks.content: Placing a filter on the right table's column in WHERE (instead of ON) during a LEFT JOIN silently converts it to an INNER JOIN for that condition. WHERE ep.price_eur_mwh > 50 excludes rows where ep.price_eur_mwh is NULL — which includes all unmatched left rows. If you intended LEFT JOIN behavior, move the condition to ON. This is one of the subtlest and most common join mistakes.

When you join two tables, the result temporarily contains columns from both tables. If both tables have a column with the same name — which is common, because foreign key columns often share names with the primary key they reference — the database cannot determine which table's column you mean in the SELECT or WHERE clause. This is the column ambiguity problem. It produces an error like 'column reference is ambiguous' and is one of the most frequent errors new SQL users encounter when writing their first joins. The solution is simple: qualify every column with its table alias.
The Error

-- This query FAILS: meter_id exists in both tables
SELECT meter_id,           -- ambiguous: which table?
       daily_kw,
       customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id;

-- Error: column reference "meter_id" is ambiguous

The error is clear: meter_id exists in both meter_readings and meters. The database does not guess which one you mean — it rejects the query. The fix is to prefix the column with the table alias: mr.meter_id or m.meter_id. Both contain the same value (they matched in the ON clause), so either alias works for the output. But the database needs you to be explicit.

-- CORRECT: qualify every column with its table alias
SELECT mr.meter_id,         -- unambiguous: from meter_readings
       mr.daily_kw,          -- only in meter_readings
       m.customer_type,      -- only in meters
       m.installation_date   -- only in meters
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

Every column is now prefixed with its source table alias. Even daily_kw (which exists only in meter_readings) and installation_date (which exists only in meters) are qualified. Strictly, only meter_id requires qualification because it is the only ambiguous column. But qualifying every column — as HydroAlpes' style guide requires — prevents future ambiguity if someone adds a column to the meters table that shares a name with a meter_readings column.
The USING Clause

When the join columns have the same name in both tables, SQL provides the USING clause as an alternative to ON. Instead of ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id, you write USING (meter_id). The USING clause automatically handles the ambiguity: the shared column appears once in the result (not duplicated), and you reference it without a table prefix. USING is more concise than ON for same-named columns, but it only works when the column names match exactly. If the columns have different names (like readings.meter_code and meters.meter_id), you must use ON.

-- USING clause: cleaner syntax when column names match
SELECT meter_id,             -- no alias needed: USING resolves ambiguity
       mr.daily_kw,
       m.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    USING (meter_id)
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

With USING (meter_id), the result contains one meter_id column instead of two, and you reference it without a prefix. Some teams prefer USING for its conciseness. HydroAlpes uses ON exclusively because it works in all cases (same or different column names) and makes the join relationship fully explicit. Either approach is correct — the choice is a style preference. Both produce identical results.

blocks.title: Qualify everything in joins
blocks.content: The safest practice — and HydroAlpes' standard — is to qualify every column in a join query with its table alias, even when the column is unambiguous. This prevents the query from breaking when new columns are added to either table and makes the data lineage immediately visible: any reader can see which table each column comes from without checking the schema.

Real-world analytical queries rarely join just two tables. HydroAlpes' Gold-layer billing query joins meter_readings with meters (for customer type), substations (for substation name and capacity), and regions (for country). Each join adds one more table's columns to the result, enriching the data with additional context. This lesson shows how to chain multiple joins in a single query and how to read the resulting multi-join query structure.
Chaining Joins

-- Four-table join: readings → meters → substations → regions
-- Each join adds one dimension table's context to the fact data
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       m.customer_type,
       s.name            AS substation_name,
       s.capacity_mw,
       r.name            AS region_name,
       r.country
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.substations AS s
    ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.regions AS r
    ON m.region = r.region_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

meter_id       | daily_kw | date       | customer_type | substation_name           | capacity_mw | region_name   | country
---------------+----------+------------+---------------+---------------------------+-------------+---------------+--------
CH-BERN-00142  |    18.34 | 2025-01-15 | RESIDENTIAL   | Alpine Grid Station B-4   |         120 | Bern Canton   | CH
CH-BERN-00143  |     7.91 | 2025-01-15 | RESIDENTIAL   | Alpine Grid Station B-4   |         120 | Bern Canton   | CH
CH-BERN-00003  |    31.20 | 2025-01-15 | COMMERCIAL    | Alpine Grid Station B-4   |         120 | Bern Canton   | CH
CH-BERN-65004  |  1,871.20 | 2025-01-15 | INDUSTRIAL    | Industrial Hub B-1        |         350 | Bern Canton   | CH
CH-BERN-99801  |     3.21 | 2025-01-15 | (null)         | (null)                    |      (null) | (null)         | (null)

The query chains three LEFT JOINs. The first joins meter_readings with meters on meter_id. The second joins the result with substations using the substation_id from the meters table. The third joins with regions using the region code from meters. Each join adds columns from one dimension table. The last row (meter CH-BERN-99801) has NULLs for all dimension columns because it is an unregistered meter — the LEFT JOIN preserves it with NULL metadata, exactly as designed.
Join Order and Readability

Multi-table joins are evaluated left to right: the database first joins meter_readings with meters, producing an intermediate result. Then it joins that intermediate result with substations. Then it joins the second intermediate result with regions. Each join operates on the result of all previous joins. This left-to-right flow means the join order in the query reflects the data flow: start with the primary fact table, then progressively enrich it with dimension tables. HydroAlpes' convention places each JOIN on its own line with the ON clause indented beneath it, making the chain visually scannable.
Following the Foreign Key Map

The entity-relationship diagram from Module 2 (lesson 8) is the roadmap for multi-table joins. Each arrow in the ER diagram represents a foreign key relationship, and each relationship translates to one JOIN clause. The four-table join above follows three arrows: meter_readings → meters (on meter_id), meters → substations (on substation_id), and meters → regions (on region). You can trace any path through the ER diagram and translate it to a chain of joins. The longer the path, the more tables in the query. HydroAlpes' data model is designed so that no common analytical query requires more than four joins.

blocks.title: The four-table join path through the ER diagram
blocks.content:
meter_readings ──meter_id──▶ meters ──substation_id──▶ substations
      │                        │
      │                        └──region──▶ regions
      │
   (fact table)             (dimension tables)

Query join chain:
  FROM   meter_readings AS mr
    JOIN meters         AS m  ON mr.meter_id      = m.meter_id
    JOIN substations    AS s  ON m.substation_id   = s.substation_id
    JOIN regions        AS r  ON m.region          = r.region_id
blocks.caption: Each foreign key arrow in the ER diagram becomes one JOIN clause. The fact table is always the starting point in FROM, and dimension tables are joined progressively.

blocks.title: Each join can multiply or reduce rows
blocks.content: A one-to-many join increases the row count (one substation matches many meters). A many-to-one join preserves the row count (each meter matches one substation). If a multi-table join produces more rows than the original fact table, a one-to-many relationship in the chain is creating duplicates. Always verify the row count after a complex join to ensure it matches your expectation.

This module taught every join type in SQL: INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN, CROSS JOIN, self-joins, and multi-table joins. This final lesson synthesizes those types into the join patterns that HydroAlpes' data engineering team uses in production. Each pattern solves a specific and common problem: enriching fact data with dimension context, detecting gaps in time series, reconciling two datasets, and deduplicating records. These patterns appear in every data engineering pipeline and will serve you throughout your career.
Pattern 1: Dimension Enrichment

-- Enrich fact data with dimension context
-- This is the most common join pattern in data engineering
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       m.customer_type,
       s.name AS substation_name
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.substations AS s
    ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

Dimension enrichment joins fact tables (events, measurements) with dimension tables (descriptive context) to produce analytical datasets. The fact table goes in FROM, the dimension tables follow as LEFT JOINs, and the ON clauses follow the foreign key relationships. LEFT JOIN is used instead of INNER JOIN to preserve all fact rows, even those with missing dimension data. This pattern produces the denormalized Gold-layer tables that dashboards and reports consume. At HydroAlpes, every Gold table is produced by a dimension enrichment query.
Pattern 2: Gap Detection

-- Detect days where a specific meter has no reading
-- CROSS JOIN builds the scaffold, LEFT JOIN finds gaps
WITH expected_dates AS (
  SELECT generate_series('2025-01-01'::date,
                          '2025-01-31'::date,
                          '1 day'::interval)::date AS date
)
SELECT ed.date AS missing_date
FROM   expected_dates AS ed
  LEFT JOIN silver.meter_readings AS mr
    ON ed.date = mr.date
   AND mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
WHERE  mr.date IS NULL
ORDER BY ed.date;

This query identifies days in January where meter CH-BERN-00142 has no reading. The scaffold (expected_dates) lists every day. The LEFT JOIN matches each day to a reading for that specific meter. The WHERE mr.date IS NULL filter keeps only unmatched days — the gaps. If the result is empty, the meter reported every day. If three dates appear, three readings are missing. This pattern scales to any entity-time combination: days without turbine telemetry, hours without price data, months without billing records.
Pattern 3: Reconciliation

Reconciliation compares two datasets to find discrepancies: rows present in one but missing from the other, or rows present in both with different values. The FULL OUTER JOIN from lesson 4 is the core tool. HydroAlpes uses reconciliation queries to compare Bronze and Silver data (ensuring no readings were lost during transformation), to compare the meters table against an external registration system (ensuring all registered meters are accounted for), and to compare pipeline output against expected totals from the billing system.
Pattern 4: Latest Record Per Entity

-- Find the most recent reading for each meter
-- Self-join approach (window function version in Module 8 is cleaner)
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN (
    SELECT meter_id, MAX(date) AS max_date
    FROM   silver.meter_readings
    GROUP BY meter_id
  ) AS latest
    ON mr.meter_id = latest.meter_id
   AND mr.date     = latest.max_date
WHERE  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

This query finds the most recent reading for each meter by first computing the latest date per meter in a subquery, then joining back to the original table to retrieve the full row. The subquery (in the FROM clause) groups by meter_id and selects the MAX(date). The join matches each meter to its latest date and retrieves the corresponding daily_kw. This is a common deduplication and latest-record pattern in data engineering. Module 8 will teach a cleaner approach using the ROW_NUMBER window function, but the join-based version is widely used and worth understanding.
The Join Decision Framework

rows: Find missing data in a time series?
Find the latest/best record per entity?
Compare a row with its neighbor?

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 7, you will learn subqueries and Common Table Expressions — tools for composing complex logic by nesting one query inside another. Several patterns from this lesson (the gap detection scaffold, the latest-record subquery) already previewed subqueries. Module 7 formalizes these techniques and adds new ones: scalar subqueries, EXISTS, correlated subqueries, and recursive CTEs. Combined with joins, subqueries give you the full toolkit for building any analytical query HydroAlpes needs.

chapters.title: Subqueries and Common Table Expressions
chapters.description: Master subqueries as queries nested inside other queries, learn Common Table Expressions as the modern tool for composing complex logic, and understand when each approach is appropriate.

blocks.content: Every query so far has answered its question in a single pass: select columns, filter rows, aggregate groups, join tables. But some questions require intermediate results. HydroAlpes needs to find meters whose daily consumption exceeds the average for their region — but the regional average is itself a computation that requires GROUP BY. You cannot put a GROUP BY inside a WHERE clause. The answer must be computed first, then used as input to the next question. This is the problem that subqueries solve: they let you nest one query inside another, using the inner query's result as part of the outer query's logic.
The Two-Step Problem

Consider the question: which meters consumed more than the overall average on January 15? In Python, you would solve this in two steps — first compute the average, then filter to rows above it. In SQL, you could run two separate queries: one to compute the average, then a second that uses the numeric result in a WHERE clause. But this is fragile and manual. A subquery lets you embed the first query inside the second, so the database computes the average and uses it in the filter in a single statement. The inner query (compute the average) runs first, and its result feeds the outer query (filter to rows above it).

-- Step 1 (manual): compute the average
SELECT AVG(daily_kw) FROM silver.meter_readings
WHERE date = '2025-01-15';
-- Result: 1.46

-- Step 2 (manual): use the result
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw
FROM silver.meter_readings
WHERE date = '2025-01-15' AND daily_kw > 1.46;

-- Combined with a subquery: one statement, no manual step
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.daily_kw > (
         SELECT AVG(daily_kw)
         FROM   silver.meter_readings
         WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
       );

The subquery — the parenthesized SELECT inside the WHERE clause — computes the average of daily_kw for January 15. The database evaluates this inner query first, gets the result (1.46), and then uses that value in the outer query's comparison: daily_kw > 1.46. The two queries become one statement. The result is self-contained, reproducible, and does not depend on a human copying a number between queries. This composability — building complex logic from simpler pieces — is one of SQL's most powerful features.
Where Subqueries Can Appear

Subqueries can appear in three places in a SQL statement. In the WHERE clause (as shown above), they provide values or sets for comparison. In the FROM clause, they create derived tables — temporary result sets that the outer query treats as if they were real tables. In the SELECT clause, they compute a single value for each row of the outer query. Each placement serves a different purpose, and this module dedicates a lesson to each. You will also learn Common Table Expressions (CTEs), which provide a cleaner syntax for the same composability that subqueries offer.

rows: Provides a value or set for filtering (IN, comparison, EXISTS)
Creates a derived table (inline virtual table)
Computes a scalar value for each output row
Names and reuses intermediate results for readability

blocks.title: Subqueries are building blocks
blocks.content: Every subquery is a complete, valid SQL query on its own. You can run it independently to see what it produces, then nest it inside the outer query. This makes complex queries debuggable: if the result is wrong, run each subquery independently to find which one produces unexpected output. Build from the inside out, test each piece, then compose.

A scalar subquery is a subquery that returns exactly one value — one row, one column. It can be used anywhere a single value is expected: in a WHERE comparison, in a SELECT expression, or in a HAVING condition. The 'average daily consumption' subquery from lesson 1 is a scalar subquery — it returns one number (1.46). Scalar subqueries are the simplest form of subquery and the most intuitive: they compute a value, and the outer query uses that value as if you had typed it directly.
Scalar Subquery in WHERE

-- Find meters consuming more than double the overall average
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.daily_kw > 2 * (
         SELECT AVG(daily_kw)
         FROM   silver.meter_readings
         WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
       )
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5;

meter_id       | daily_kw  | region
---------------+-----------+-----------
AT-INNB-91002  |  2,847.30 | INNSBRUCK
CH-ZRCH-88431  |  2,103.50 | ZURICH
AT-SLZB-77210  |  1,942.80 | SALZBURG
CH-BERN-65004  |  1,871.20 | BERN
AT-INNB-43318  |  1,654.90 | INNSBRUCK

The subquery computes AVG(daily_kw) for January 15 — approximately 1.46 kW. The outer query multiplies that by 2 (2.92 kW) and filters to meters exceeding that threshold. The result shows the five highest-consuming meters, all well above the doubled average. The subquery executes once, produces one number, and the outer query uses it for every row's comparison. This is the defining characteristic of a scalar subquery: one execution, one result, used as a constant.
Scalar Subquery in SELECT

-- Add the overall average as a column alongside each row
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       (SELECT AVG(daily_kw)
        FROM   silver.meter_readings
        WHERE  date = '2025-01-15') AS overall_avg,
       mr.daily_kw - (
        SELECT AVG(daily_kw)
        FROM   silver.meter_readings
        WHERE  date = '2025-01-15') AS diff_from_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  4;

The scalar subquery appears twice in the SELECT clause: once to show the overall average as a column, and once in a subtraction to compute each meter's deviation from the average. Every row gets the same overall_avg value (1.46) because the subquery returns one constant. This pattern — displaying both the individual value and the aggregate alongside each other — is a preview of what window functions (Module 8) do more elegantly. For now, the scalar subquery approach works and demonstrates the concept clearly.
When Scalar Subqueries Fail

A scalar subquery must return exactly one row and one column. If it returns zero rows, the result is NULL (which may cause the comparison to behave unexpectedly). If it returns more than one row, the database raises an error: 'more than one row returned by a subquery used as an expression.' This error means your subquery is not sufficiently filtered — it returned multiple values where only one was expected. The fix is to add WHERE conditions or an aggregate function (like AVG or MAX) to ensure the subquery produces exactly one value.

blocks.title: The multi-row error
blocks.content: If you write WHERE daily_kw > (SELECT daily_kw FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE region = 'BERN') and BERN has 90,000 meters, the subquery returns 90,000 rows — far more than one. The database cannot compare daily_kw against 90,000 values using the > operator, so it raises an error. Either aggregate the subquery (AVG, MAX, MIN) to produce one value, or use IN instead of > to compare against a set. The error message is your signal to rethink the subquery's design.

A subquery in the FROM clause creates a derived table — a temporary, inline result set that the outer query treats as if it were a real table. You can SELECT from it, JOIN it, filter it, and aggregate it just like any other table. The derived table exists only for the duration of the query and is not stored anywhere. This pattern is useful when you need to pre-process data before the outer query operates on it — for example, aggregating meter readings by region in the subquery and then joining the aggregated results with the regions dimension table in the outer query.
The Syntax

-- Step 1 (subquery): compute regional totals
-- Step 2 (outer): join with regions dimension for context
SELECT reg.name         AS region_name,
       reg.country,
       totals.total_kw,
       totals.meter_count
FROM   (
         SELECT region,
                SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
                COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id) AS meter_count
         FROM   silver.meter_readings
         WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01'
           AND  date <  '2025-02-01'
         GROUP BY region
       ) AS totals
  INNER JOIN silver.regions AS reg
    ON totals.region = reg.region_id
ORDER BY totals.total_kw DESC;

The subquery in the FROM clause aggregates meter_readings by region, producing four rows with regional totals and meter counts. The outer query joins this derived table (aliased as totals) with the regions dimension table to add the region name and country. The derived table is treated exactly like a real table — it has columns (region, total_kw, meter_count), it can be joined, and it can be filtered. The alias AS totals is mandatory — every derived table must have an alias so the outer query can reference its columns.
Why Pre-Aggregate in a Subquery?

You might wonder why not just join meter_readings with regions directly and then aggregate. Both approaches produce the same result, but pre-aggregating in a subquery can be significantly more efficient. Joining 128 million meter_readings rows with 4 regions rows and then aggregating means the database must process all 128 million rows through the join before aggregating. Pre-aggregating in the subquery reduces 128 million rows to 4 rows first, then joins those 4 rows with the 4 regions rows — a much lighter operation. The database optimizer often rewrites your query to achieve this optimization automatically, but writing the pre-aggregation explicitly makes the intent clear.
Readability Concerns

Derived tables work well for one level of nesting. But when you nest subqueries inside subqueries inside subqueries — three or more levels deep — the query becomes difficult to read. Each level of nesting increases indentation and forces the reader to mentally track which part of the query they are inside. This readability problem is one of the main reasons Common Table Expressions (CTEs) were added to SQL. CTEs — which you will learn in lesson 7 — provide the same composability as derived tables but with a flat, top-to-bottom structure that is far easier to read and debug.

blocks.title: Derived tables must have aliases
blocks.content: Every subquery in the FROM clause must be aliased: FROM (...) AS totals. Without the alias, the database has no name to reference the derived table's columns, and the query fails with a syntax error. Choose descriptive aliases — totals, daily_stats, bern_readings — that tell the reader what the derived table contains, not generic names like t1 or sub.

In Module 4 (lesson 5) you learned to use IN with a hardcoded list: WHERE region IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH'). But what if the list is dynamic — determined by a query rather than typed by hand? HydroAlpes needs all readings for meters that belong to substations with capacity above 200 MW. The list of qualifying substations is not hardcoded — it depends on the data in the substations table. A subquery inside IN produces this list dynamically: the inner query finds the qualifying substations, and the outer query uses that list to filter meter readings.
The IN Subquery Pattern

-- Find readings for meters connected to high-capacity substations
-- The list of qualifying substations comes from a subquery
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.meter_id IN (
         SELECT m.meter_id
         FROM   silver.meters AS m
           INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s
             ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
         WHERE  s.capacity_mw > 200
       )
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5;

The subquery joins meters with substations, filters to substations above 200 MW capacity, and returns the list of qualifying meter IDs. The outer query uses that list in the IN clause to filter meter_readings. The result shows only readings from meters connected to high-capacity substations — all in Zurich, which has the largest substations. This two-step logic (find qualifying entities, then filter facts by those entities) is one of the most common subquery patterns in data engineering.
NOT IN — Excluding a Dynamic List

-- Find readings for meters NOT connected to high-capacity substations
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.meter_id NOT IN (
         SELECT m.meter_id
         FROM   silver.meters AS m
           INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s
             ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
         WHERE  s.capacity_mw > 200
       )
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

NOT IN reverses the filter: the outer query keeps only meters that are not in the subquery's result. This finds readings for meters connected to smaller substations. NOT IN works well when the subquery result is small and contains no NULLs. However, NOT IN has a dangerous interaction with NULL values: if any value in the subquery result is NULL, the entire NOT IN condition evaluates to NULL for every row, and no rows are returned. If the subquery might produce NULLs, use NOT EXISTS instead — which you will learn in the next lesson.
IN Subquery vs JOIN

The IN subquery pattern can often be rewritten as a JOIN. The query above could be written as a three-table join: meter_readings JOIN meters JOIN substations WHERE capacity_mw > 200. Both approaches produce the same result. The IN subquery is often more readable when the filtering logic is the primary purpose — 'find readings for meters that meet this criterion.' The JOIN approach is better when you need columns from the filtered table in the result. At HydroAlpes, Lena chooses IN subqueries for existence checks and joins for enrichment, but the choice is largely a matter of readability and team convention.

blocks.title: NOT IN and NULL: the silent empty result
blocks.content: If any value in the subquery's result is NULL, NOT IN returns no rows at all — for every row in the outer query. This happens because NULL compared with NOT IN produces NULL (unknown), and WHERE keeps only TRUE rows. The fix: either filter NULLs out of the subquery (WHERE meter_id IS NOT NULL), or use NOT EXISTS, which handles NULLs correctly. This is one of the most dangerous NULL traps in SQL.

IN checks whether a value matches any value in a list. EXISTS checks whether a related row exists at all — it does not care about the values, only about the existence of at least one matching row. The distinction is subtle but important. HydroAlpes needs to find meters that have at least one reading above 100 kW. IN would require the subquery to return all meter_ids with high readings, then match them against the outer query. EXISTS just checks: for each meter, does any high reading exist? EXISTS is often more efficient than IN for large datasets and handles NULLs correctly, making it the preferred tool for existence checks.
The Semi-Join Pattern

-- Find meters that have at least one reading above 100 kW in January
SELECT m.meter_id, m.customer_type, m.region
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  EXISTS (
         SELECT 1
         FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
         WHERE  mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
           AND  mr.daily_kw > 100
           AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
           AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
       )
ORDER BY m.region, m.customer_type
LIMIT  5;

meter_id       | customer_type | region
---------------+---------------+---------
CH-BERN-58331  | INDUSTRIAL    | BERN
CH-BERN-65004  | INDUSTRIAL    | BERN
CH-BERN-52017  | COMMERCIAL    | BERN
AT-INNB-43318  | INDUSTRIAL    | INNSBRUCK
AT-INNB-91002  | INDUSTRIAL    | INNSBRUCK

For each row in the meters table, the database checks: does at least one row exist in meter_readings where the meter_id matches and daily_kw exceeds 100? If yes, the meter is included. If no, it is excluded. The SELECT 1 inside the subquery is a convention — EXISTS does not use the subquery's output values, it only checks whether any rows exist. You could write SELECT * or SELECT 'anything' and the result would be the same. This pattern is called a semi-join because it joins the tables conceptually (matching on meter_id) but returns columns only from the outer table.
NOT EXISTS — The Anti-Join

-- Find meters that have NO readings at all in January (inactive meters)
SELECT m.meter_id, m.customer_type, m.region
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
         SELECT 1
         FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
         WHERE  mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
           AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
           AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
       )
ORDER BY m.region
LIMIT  5;

NOT EXISTS is the inverse: it keeps meters for which no matching reading exists. These are inactive meters — registered in the meters table but producing no data in January. The result is the data quality list that HydroAlpes' operations team uses to investigate offline meters. NOT EXISTS is the safe alternative to NOT IN when the subquery might contain NULLs, because EXISTS only checks for row existence and never compares values — NULL values in the subquery do not cause the empty-result trap that NOT IN suffers from.
EXISTS vs IN

left.label: IN (value matching)
points: Compares a column value against a list of values
Subquery returns a list of values
NOT IN breaks with NULLs in the subquery result
Often simpler for straightforward value lookups
Subquery runs independently of the outer query

right.label: EXISTS (row existence)
points: Checks whether at least one related row exists
Subquery returns TRUE/FALSE (rows exist or not)
NOT EXISTS handles NULLs correctly
Often more efficient for large datasets
Subquery references the outer query (correlated)

blocks.title: EXISTS is always NULL-safe
blocks.content: The EXISTS operator checks for the existence of rows, not for specific values. Because it never compares values, NULL values in either table cannot cause the unexpected behavior that plagues NOT IN. At HydroAlpes, Lena's SQL style guide specifies NOT EXISTS over NOT IN for all anti-join patterns, precisely because of this NULL safety.

Every subquery so far has been independent — the inner query can run on its own, without knowing about the outer query. A correlated subquery is different: it references a column from the outer query, so it executes once for each row of the outer query. The EXISTS subquery from lesson 5 is actually a correlated subquery — it references m.meter_id from the outer query inside the WHERE clause of the inner query. Correlated subqueries are the most powerful form of subquery, but they can be expensive because the inner query runs repeatedly, not just once.
How Correlation Works

In a correlated subquery, the inner query references a value from the current row of the outer query. For each row the outer query processes, the database evaluates the inner query with that row's values substituted in. Consider finding meters whose daily consumption exceeds their own historical average. The inner query computes the average for each specific meter, using the outer query's meter_id to filter. For meter CH-BERN-00142, the inner query computes AVG(daily_kw) WHERE meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'. For meter CH-BERN-00143, it computes AVG(daily_kw) WHERE meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00143'. Each meter gets its own personalized average.

-- Find readings that exceed the meter's own historical average
-- The subquery references the outer query's meter_id (correlated)
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
  AND  mr.daily_kw > (
         SELECT AVG(mr2.daily_kw)
         FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr2
         WHERE  mr2.meter_id = mr.meter_id   -- correlation: references outer query
           AND  mr2.date >= '2024-01-01'
           AND  mr2.date <  '2025-01-15'
       )
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5;

The inner query computes AVG(daily_kw) for the specific meter from the outer query — notice mr2.meter_id = mr.meter_id, where mr is the outer table alias and mr2 is the inner. For each outer row, the database substitutes the outer row's meter_id into the inner query and computes that meter's historical average. Only readings that exceed their meter's own average appear in the result. This per-row customization is what makes correlated subqueries uniquely powerful — and uniquely expensive.
Performance Implications

A correlated subquery executes once per row of the outer query. If the outer query produces 90,000 rows (BERN meters on January 15), the inner query runs 90,000 times. Modern databases optimize this using techniques like caching and semi-join conversion, but the fundamental cost remains higher than a non-correlated subquery (which runs once). For many correlated subquery patterns, a JOIN or a window function (Module 8) achieves the same result more efficiently. The 'readings above own average' query above could be rewritten using a self-join with a pre-aggregated subquery, or using window functions — both of which are typically faster.

blocks.title: Use correlated subqueries as a last resort
blocks.content: Correlated subqueries are powerful but expensive. Before writing one, ask: can this be done with a JOIN and a GROUP BY? Can a window function (Module 8) compute the per-row aggregate without a subquery? If either alternative works, prefer it. At HydroAlpes, correlated subqueries appear mainly in data quality scripts that run against small datasets, not in production pipelines that process millions of rows.

Subqueries nested inside FROM and WHERE clauses work, but they have a readability problem: the reader must parse the query from the inside out, understanding the innermost subquery first before making sense of the outer query. When subqueries are nested three levels deep, the query becomes a maze of parentheses and indentation. Common Table Expressions — CTEs, written with the WITH keyword — solve this problem by letting you name intermediate results and define them at the top of the query, before the main SELECT. The reader can follow the logic top to bottom, like reading a script, rather than inside out.
The Syntax

-- CTE version: name the intermediate result, then use it
WITH regional_totals AS (
  SELECT mr.region,
         SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
         COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) AS meter_count
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
    AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
  GROUP BY mr.region
)
SELECT r.name          AS region_name,
       r.country,
       rt.total_kw,
       rt.meter_count
FROM   regional_totals AS rt
  INNER JOIN silver.regions AS r
    ON rt.region = r.region_id
ORDER BY rt.total_kw DESC;

The WITH clause defines a named result set called regional_totals. The query inside the parentheses is a complete SELECT that aggregates meter readings by region. The main query (after the CTE definition) references regional_totals as if it were a real table — joining it with the regions dimension table. The result is identical to the derived table version from lesson 3, but the structure reads top to bottom: first define the intermediate result, then use it. This top-down flow is why CTEs are overwhelmingly preferred over nested subqueries in modern SQL.
CTE vs Derived Table

left.label: Derived Table (FROM subquery)
points: Defined inside the FROM clause
Read inside-out
Cannot be referenced more than once
Nesting creates deep indentation
Works in all databases

right.label: CTE (WITH clause)
points: Defined before the main query
Read top-to-bottom
Can be referenced multiple times in the main query
Multiple CTEs stay flat
Supported by PostgreSQL, Spark SQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, and SQL Server

blocks.content: The key advantage beyond readability is reusability: a CTE can be referenced multiple times in the main query. If you need regional_totals in both a join and a comparison, the CTE is defined once and used twice. A derived table would need to be written twice — duplicating the logic and creating a maintenance burden. At HydroAlpes, CTEs are the standard for any query with intermediate computation. Derived tables are used only when a quick inline subquery is simpler than a full CTE.

blocks.title: CTEs are the modern standard
blocks.content: CTEs were introduced in the SQL:1999 standard and are now supported by every major database. In data engineering, CTEs have largely replaced nested subqueries as the primary tool for composing complex queries. dbt (a popular transformation framework) is built entirely around CTEs — every dbt model is a SELECT statement, and complex models chain multiple CTEs. Learning CTEs now prepares you for dbt and every other modern SQL tool.

The real power of CTEs emerges when you chain multiple named results in a single WITH block, each building on the previous one. HydroAlpes' Silver-to-Gold transformation often requires three or more steps: filter the raw data, aggregate by dimension, join with reference tables, and compute derived metrics. Each step becomes one CTE, and the final SELECT combines them into the Gold output. This progressive refinement pattern — each CTE consuming the previous one's output — is the foundation of readable, maintainable SQL pipelines.
The Multi-CTE Pattern

-- HydroAlpes Gold-layer billing query with three CTEs
-- Each CTE does one job; the final SELECT assembles the result

WITH january_readings AS (
  -- Step 1: filter to January 2025, clean data only
  SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
    AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
    AND  mr.daily_kw >= 0
),
region_customer_totals AS (
  -- Step 2: aggregate by region and customer type
  SELECT jr.region,
         m.customer_type,
         SUM(jr.daily_kw)          AS total_kw,
         COUNT(DISTINCT jr.meter_id) AS active_meters
  FROM   january_readings AS jr
    LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m
      ON jr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  GROUP BY jr.region, m.customer_type
),
enriched AS (
  -- Step 3: join with regions dimension for display names
  SELECT r.name         AS region_name,
         r.country,
         rct.customer_type,
         rct.total_kw,
         rct.active_meters
  FROM   region_customer_totals AS rct
    INNER JOIN silver.regions AS r
      ON rct.region = r.region_id
)
-- Final: select and sort the assembled result
SELECT region_name,
       country,
       customer_type,
       total_kw,
       total_kw * 0.001 AS total_mwh,
       active_meters
FROM   enriched
ORDER BY region_name, customer_type;

region_name   | country | customer_type | total_kw       | total_mwh    | active_meters
--------------+---------+---------------+----------------+--------------+--------------
Bern Canton   | CH      | COMMERCIAL    |    982,451.30  |      982.45  |        10,000
Bern Canton   | CH      | INDUSTRIAL    |  1,241,890.50  |    1,241.89  |         3,000
Bern Canton   | CH      | RESIDENTIAL   |  2,299,549.40  |    2,299.55  |        82,000
Innsbruck     | AT      | COMMERCIAL    |    612,340.70  |      612.34  |         7,400
...

Three CTEs, each performing one step. january_readings filters the source data. region_customer_totals aggregates and joins with meters for customer type. enriched adds region names from the dimension table. The final SELECT adds a unit conversion and sorts the result. Each CTE is independently testable — you can run any CTE in isolation by replacing the final query with SELECT * FROM that_cte. This debuggability is one of the strongest practical advantages of CTEs over nested subqueries.
The Pipeline Analogy

Chained CTEs work like a data pipeline within a single query. Data flows from the first CTE through each successive one, being filtered, aggregated, and enriched at each stage — exactly like the Bronze-Silver-Gold architecture operates across tables. The first CTE is the Bronze stage (raw data with basic filtering). The middle CTEs are the Silver stage (aggregation, joining, validation). The final SELECT is the Gold stage (formatting, derived metrics, consumer-ready output). This mental model — CTEs as pipeline stages — makes complex queries intuitive to design and maintain.

blocks.title: One job per CTE
blocks.content: Each CTE should do exactly one thing: filter, aggregate, join, or compute. If a CTE does filtering and aggregation and joining, split it into three CTEs. The individual CTEs become simpler, more testable, and easier to modify when requirements change. At HydroAlpes, Lena's review standard is: if a CTE has more than one GROUP BY or more than one JOIN, it is doing too much and should be split.

Standard CTEs define a named result set and use it in the main query. Recursive CTEs go further: they reference themselves, iterating until a termination condition is met. This self-referencing capability lets SQL process hierarchical data — trees and graphs — that would otherwise require procedural code. HydroAlpes' infrastructure has a natural hierarchy: regions contain substations, substations connect to meters, and reservoirs connect to turbines. A recursive CTE can traverse this hierarchy to answer questions like: which region does meter CH-BERN-00142 ultimately belong to, tracing through the substation to the region?
The Structure

A recursive CTE has two parts. The anchor member is a regular SELECT that produces the starting rows — the root of the hierarchy. The recursive member is a SELECT that joins the CTE with itself, producing the next level of the hierarchy. The two parts are connected with UNION ALL. The database executes the anchor first, then repeatedly executes the recursive member until it produces no new rows. This automatic iteration replaces the explicit loops you would need in Python to traverse a tree structure.

-- Recursive CTE: traverse the region → substation → meter hierarchy
-- Find all meters in the BERN region by walking the hierarchy

WITH RECURSIVE meter_hierarchy AS (
  -- Anchor: start with the BERN region
  SELECT r.region_id,
         r.name AS region_name,
         NULL::VARCHAR AS substation_id,
         NULL::VARCHAR AS meter_id,
         1 AS level
  FROM   silver.regions AS r
  WHERE  r.region_id = 'BERN'

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive step 1: find substations in the region
  SELECT mh.region_id,
         mh.region_name,
         s.substation_id,
         NULL::VARCHAR AS meter_id,
         mh.level + 1
  FROM   meter_hierarchy AS mh
    INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s
      ON mh.region_id = s.region
  WHERE  mh.level = 1

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive step 2: find meters for each substation
  SELECT mh.region_id,
         mh.region_name,
         mh.substation_id,
         m.meter_id,
         mh.level + 1
  FROM   meter_hierarchy AS mh
    INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
      ON mh.substation_id = m.substation_id
  WHERE  mh.level = 2
)
SELECT region_name, substation_id, meter_id
FROM   meter_hierarchy
WHERE  level = 3
LIMIT  5;

region_name | substation_id | meter_id
------------+---------------+---------------
Bern Canton | SUB-BERN-01   | CH-BERN-00001
Bern Canton | SUB-BERN-01   | CH-BERN-00002
Bern Canton | SUB-BERN-01   | CH-BERN-00003
Bern Canton | SUB-BERN-04   | CH-BERN-00142
Bern Canton | SUB-BERN-04   | CH-BERN-00143

The recursive CTE starts with the BERN region (level 1), finds its substations (level 2), then finds meters for each substation (level 3). The level column tracks the depth of the hierarchy. The final SELECT filters to level 3 (meters) to show the leaf nodes. This three-level hierarchy is simple enough to solve with regular JOINs — and in practice, you would use joins for this specific query. Recursive CTEs become essential when the hierarchy has variable depth (a manager reporting to a manager reporting to a director) or when the depth is not known in advance.
When Recursive CTEs Are Needed

Recursive CTEs are essential for variable-depth hierarchies: organizational charts, bill-of-materials structures, and network topologies where the number of levels is not fixed. At HydroAlpes, the current three-level hierarchy (region → substation → meter) has a fixed depth, so regular joins suffice. But if HydroAlpes introduces sub-regions (BERN-NORTH, BERN-SOUTH) in the mid-level track, the hierarchy becomes variable-depth, and recursive CTEs become the natural tool. For now, understanding the concept and syntax is the goal — you will encounter more complex recursive patterns in the mid-level SQL course.

blocks.title: Infinite recursion
blocks.content: A recursive CTE without a proper termination condition runs forever — or until the database hits a recursion depth limit and returns an error. The termination condition is usually a WHERE clause in the recursive member that limits the depth (WHERE level < 10) or a relationship that naturally terminates (each row references a parent, and roots have no parent). Always include a depth limit when writing recursive CTEs against data whose structure you do not fully control.

This module introduced four tools for composing complex SQL logic: scalar subqueries, table subqueries (in FROM and WHERE), correlated subqueries, and Common Table Expressions. Each tool solves a different problem, and choosing the right one for each situation makes your queries more readable, more efficient, and easier to maintain. This lesson provides a decision framework that HydroAlpes' team uses when writing production queries, and summarizes the module by tracing a complete analytical question through the composition approach that best fits it.
The Decision Framework

rows: Compare a column against a single computed value
Simple, runs once, produces one value
Filter by a dynamic list of values
IN for simple value lists; EXISTS for existence checks and NULL safety
CTE for readability; derived table for quick inline use
Top-to-bottom readability, each stage testable independently
Per-row comparison with row's own aggregate
Correlated subquery (or window function in Module 8)
Correlated subquery when window function cannot express the logic
Only tool that handles unknown-depth tree traversal

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' Style Preferences

Lena's team follows three guidelines when choosing composition tools. First, prefer CTEs over nested subqueries for anything that requires more than one intermediate step — CTEs are readable, testable, and maintainable. Second, prefer EXISTS over NOT IN for anti-join patterns — EXISTS handles NULLs correctly and is never slower. Third, prefer window functions (Module 8) over correlated subqueries for per-row aggregates — window functions are almost always more efficient. These preferences reflect years of production experience and apply to the vast majority of analytical queries at HydroAlpes.
A Complete Example

-- Find the top-consuming meter per region in January 2025
-- Using CTEs for each logical step

WITH january AS (
  SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
),
meter_totals AS (
  SELECT meter_id, region,
         SUM(daily_kw) AS monthly_total
  FROM   january
  GROUP BY meter_id, region
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT meter_id, region, monthly_total,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY monthly_total DESC) AS rn
  FROM   meter_totals
)
SELECT r.meter_id,
       r.region,
       r.monthly_total
FROM   ranked AS r
WHERE  r.rn = 1
ORDER BY r.monthly_total DESC;

Three CTEs: january filters the date range, meter_totals aggregates per meter per region, and ranked uses a window function (previewing Module 8) to assign rank 1 to the highest-consuming meter in each region. The final SELECT picks only rank 1 rows. Each CTE does one job. Each is independently testable. The logic reads top to bottom. This is the CTE style that HydroAlpes uses for all Gold-layer transformation queries, and it is the style you will encounter in dbt, in production pipelines, and in senior engineers' code reviews.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 8, you will learn window functions — the ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, and running aggregate operations that appeared briefly in this lesson's final example. Window functions compute aggregates without collapsing rows, solving many of the problems that correlated subqueries and self-joins address — but more efficiently and more readably. Combined with CTEs, window functions give you the complete analytical SQL toolkit.

chapters.title: Window Functions
chapters.description: Master window functions as aggregations that preserve row-level detail, learn the OVER clause with PARTITION BY and ORDER BY, and understand ranking, offset, and running aggregate patterns.

blocks.content: Module 5 taught aggregation with GROUP BY — collapsing many rows into summary rows. Module 7 showed how subqueries and CTEs can work around aggregation's limitations by computing aggregates separately and joining them back. But both approaches involve a fundamental trade-off: GROUP BY destroys detail. Once you collapse 350,000 meter readings into 4 regional totals, the individual readings are gone from the result. If you need both the individual reading and the regional total in the same row — for example, each meter's daily consumption alongside its region's average — GROUP BY alone cannot help. Window functions solve this problem by computing aggregates without collapsing rows.
The Problem

HydroAlpes' capacity planning team needs a report showing each meter's daily reading alongside the average for its region. For meter CH-BERN-00142, the report should show 18.34 kW (the individual reading) and 1.54 kW (the BERN average) on the same row. With GROUP BY, you can compute the regional average but lose the individual readings. With a scalar subquery (Module 7, lesson 2), you can add the average as a column but the subquery runs once and returns the same value for every row — it cannot compute a different average for each region without becoming a correlated subquery that runs once per row.

-- GROUP BY gives the average but loses individual readings
SELECT region, AVG(daily_kw) AS avg_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region;
-- Result: 4 rows. Individual meter readings are gone.

region    | avg_kw
----------+-------
BERN      |   1.54
ZURICH    |   1.52
SALZBURG  |   1.39
INNSBRUCK |   1.33
The Window Function Solution

-- Window function: compute the average WITHOUT collapsing rows
SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw,
       region,
       AVG(daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY region) AS region_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

Every row is preserved — all 350,000 individual readings remain. But each row now includes an additional column, region_avg, containing the average for that row's region. The AVG function did not collapse the rows because of the OVER clause: OVER (PARTITION BY region) tells the database to compute the average across all rows that share the same region value, and attach the result to each row. The individual readings stay intact. The aggregate is added alongside them. This is the defining property of window functions: aggregation without collapse.

blocks.title: GROUP BY collapses rows; window functions preserve them
blocks.caption: GROUP BY produces few rows with aggregates. Window functions produce the same number of rows as the input, with the aggregate attached to each row as an additional column.

visual-recommendation.description: A side-by-side comparison showing GROUP BY collapsing 350K rows into 4 summary rows versus a window function preserving all 350K rows with an additional aggregate column attached. Color-coded partitions for each region.

blocks.title: Window functions are the analytics powerhouse
blocks.content: Window functions are the single most powerful analytical feature in SQL. They enable rankings, running totals, moving averages, row comparisons, and deduplication — all without subqueries, self-joins, or GROUP BY. Many problems that required complex correlated subqueries (Module 7, lesson 6) or self-joins (Module 6, lesson 6) can be solved with a single window function. This module teaches the most important analytical tool in your SQL toolkit.

The OVER clause is what transforms a regular aggregate function into a window function. Without OVER, AVG(daily_kw) collapses all rows into one. With OVER(), AVG(daily_kw) OVER() computes the average across all rows but attaches the result to every row without collapsing. The parentheses after OVER can be empty (compute across all rows) or contain PARTITION BY and ORDER BY clauses that control which rows the function considers. Understanding OVER is the key to understanding every window function in SQL.
OVER() — The Simplest Window

-- OVER() with empty parentheses: compute across ALL rows
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) OVER ()    AS total_all_meters,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER ()    AS avg_all_meters,
       COUNT(*)         OVER ()    AS count_all_meters
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  4;

Every row has the same total_all_meters (145,908.20), the same avg_all_meters (1.54), and the same count_all_meters (95,000) — because OVER() defines the window as all rows that pass the WHERE filter. The window function computed these aggregates across all 95,000 BERN readings on January 15 and attached the results to every row. The individual meter_id and daily_kw values are preserved. This is OVER at its simplest: the entire result set is one window.
The Window Concept

The term 'window' refers to the set of rows that the function operates on for each output row. With OVER(), the window is the entire result set — every row sees the same aggregate. With OVER (PARTITION BY region), the window is all rows that share the same region — each region has its own window, and the function computes independently within each window. With OVER (ORDER BY date), the window gains a sequential order, and the function can compute running totals, ranks, and row offsets. The OVER clause defines the window; the function computes within it.
Any Aggregate Becomes a Window Function

Every standard aggregate function — SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX — can be used as a window function by adding OVER. SUM(daily_kw) OVER() computes the total. AVG(daily_kw) OVER() computes the mean. COUNT(*) OVER() counts the rows. MIN and MAX find extremes. The OVER clause does not change what the function computes — it changes how the result is attached to the output. Without OVER, the function collapses rows. With OVER, it preserves rows and attaches the aggregate. This duality is elegant: the same functions you learned in Module 5 become window functions with the addition of two words.

blocks.title: OVER is the toggle
blocks.content: Think of OVER as a toggle that switches an aggregate function from 'collapse mode' to 'attach mode.' Without OVER, SUM collapses all rows into one total. With OVER, SUM computes the same total but attaches it to each row as an additional column. The computation is the same. The output structure is different. This mental model makes window functions intuitive: they are the aggregates you already know, running in a different mode.

OVER() treats all rows as one window. PARTITION BY splits the rows into separate windows — groups — within which the function operates independently. AVG(daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY region) computes a separate average for each region: BERN rows get the BERN average, ZURICH rows get the ZURICH average. The partition does not collapse rows (unlike GROUP BY) — it simply defines the boundaries within which each window function computes. PARTITION BY is the clause that makes window functions analytically powerful, because it lets you compute per-group values while keeping every individual row.
Single-Column Partitioning

-- Each meter's reading alongside its region's average and total
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.region,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw)   OVER (PARTITION BY mr.region) AS region_avg,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)   OVER (PARTITION BY mr.region) AS region_total,
       COUNT(*)           OVER (PARTITION BY mr.region) AS region_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY mr.region, mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  8;

All BERN rows share the same region_avg (1.54), region_total (145,908.20), and region_count (95,000) — because PARTITION BY region creates a window of all BERN rows and computes within it. INNSBRUCK rows get their own values (1.33, 93,260.80, 70,000). Each partition is independent — the BERN computation does not see INNSBRUCK rows and vice versa. The individual meter readings and meter IDs are fully preserved alongside the partition-level aggregates.
Multi-Column Partitioning

-- Partition by region AND customer_type for finer granularity
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         PARTITION BY mr.region, mr.customer_type
       ) AS segment_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
ORDER BY mr.customer_type, mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  6;

meter_id       | daily_kw  | region | customer_type | segment_avg
---------------+-----------+--------+---------------+------------
CH-BERN-52017  |    712.80 | BERN   | COMMERCIAL    |       3.17
CH-BERN-48892  |    687.40 | BERN   | COMMERCIAL    |       3.17
CH-BERN-65004  |  1,871.20 | BERN   | INDUSTRIAL    |      13.35
CH-BERN-58331  |    943.50 | BERN   | INDUSTRIAL    |      13.35
CH-BERN-00142  |     18.34 | BERN   | RESIDENTIAL   |       0.90
CH-BERN-00143  |      7.91 | BERN   | RESIDENTIAL   |       0.90

Now the partition is region plus customer_type, creating finer windows: BERN-COMMERCIAL, BERN-INDUSTRIAL, BERN-RESIDENTIAL. Each segment gets its own average. Industrial meters in BERN average 13.35 kW — much higher than the 0.90 kW residential average. This segment-level context alongside individual readings is exactly what HydroAlpes' capacity planning team needs: is this meter consuming more or less than similar meters in its category?

blocks.title: PARTITION BY is like GROUP BY without the collapse
blocks.content: PARTITION BY creates the same groups that GROUP BY would create — one per distinct combination of the partition columns. The difference is what happens to the rows inside each group. GROUP BY collapses them into one summary row. PARTITION BY keeps them all, attaching the aggregate to each row. If you understand GROUP BY, you understand PARTITION BY — the grouping logic is identical.

PARTITION BY defines which rows belong to the window. ORDER BY inside OVER defines the sequence of rows within the window — and this sequence fundamentally changes how aggregate window functions behave. Without ORDER BY, SUM(daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY region) computes the total of all rows in the partition — every row gets the same total. With ORDER BY, SUM(daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date) computes a running total — each row gets the sum of all rows up to and including its position in the sequence. This transformation from total to running total is one of the most powerful capabilities in analytical SQL.
Running Totals

-- Running total of daily consumption for BERN, ordered by date
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS running_total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-07'
ORDER BY mr.date;

Each row's running_total_kw is the sum of daily_kw from the first row up to and including the current row. January 1 shows 12.40 (just that day). January 2 shows 29.12 (12.40 + 16.72). January 3 shows 50.20 (29.12 + 21.08). The running total accumulates as you move down the ordered rows. This happens because ORDER BY inside OVER changes the default window frame: instead of considering all rows in the partition, the function considers only rows from the start of the partition up to the current row.
Running Averages

-- Running average: updates as each day is included
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (ORDER BY mr.date) AS running_avg_kw,
       COUNT(*)         OVER (ORDER BY mr.date) AS days_so_far
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-07'
ORDER BY mr.date;

The running average starts at 12.40 on day 1, shifts to 14.56 after including day 2, and converges toward 15.80 by day 7 as more data points are included. AVG with ORDER BY inside OVER computes the mean of all rows from the start up to the current row — a progressively stabilizing average. COUNT with ORDER BY shows how many rows have been included so far. This pattern is useful for monitoring whether a metric is trending up or down over time and when it stabilizes.

blocks.title: ORDER BY inside OVER changes the computation
blocks.content: Without ORDER BY, SUM OVER (PARTITION BY region) gives every row the same total — the sum of the entire partition. With ORDER BY, SUM OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date) gives each row a running total that grows as dates increase. Adding ORDER BY inside OVER is not just sorting the output — it fundamentally changes what the function computes. This is the most common source of confusion with window functions.

Ranking is one of the most common analytical operations: which meters consume the most? Which turbines produce the most power? What is each meter's rank within its region? SQL provides three ranking functions — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK — each handling ties differently. All three use the OVER clause with ORDER BY to define the ranking sequence, and optionally PARTITION BY to rank within groups. Ranking functions are window-only — they have no non-window equivalent because ranking inherently requires knowing a row's position relative to other rows.
ROW_NUMBER — Unique Sequential Numbers

-- Assign a unique rank to each meter within its region
-- Highest consumption gets rank 1
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.region,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
         PARTITION BY mr.region
         ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
       ) AS rank_in_region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY mr.region, rank_in_region
LIMIT  8;

ROW_NUMBER assigns a unique integer to each row within its partition, starting at 1. The numbering follows the ORDER BY sequence — highest daily_kw gets 1, second highest gets 2. Each region's numbering restarts at 1 because PARTITION BY mr.region creates independent windows. If two meters have identical daily_kw values (a tie), ROW_NUMBER still assigns different numbers — the tie-breaking is arbitrary. This makes ROW_NUMBER ideal for deduplication (keep only rank 1) and top-N per group (keep ranks 1 through N).
The Three Functions Compared

rows: Assigns different numbers to ties (arbitrary order)
Same number for ties, then skips
Same number for ties, no gaps

blocks.content: For HydroAlpes, ROW_NUMBER is the most commonly used because data engineering tasks usually need unique row identifiers: deduplicating records (keep the latest reading per meter), extracting top-N per group (top 5 consumers per region), and pagination. RANK and DENSE_RANK are useful for reporting where ties should be visible — 'two meters tied for highest consumption in BERN' — but the analytical difference matters mainly for presentation, not for pipeline logic.
The Top-N Per Group Pattern

-- Top 3 consuming meters per region using ROW_NUMBER
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT mr.meter_id,
         mr.daily_kw,
         mr.region,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY mr.region
           ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
         ) AS rn
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, region
FROM   ranked
WHERE  rn <= 3
ORDER BY region, rn;

This is the top-N per group pattern — one of the most important patterns in analytical SQL. The CTE assigns a rank within each region. The outer query filters to ranks 1 through 3. The result contains the three highest-consuming meters per region — 12 rows total (3 per region × 4 regions). This pattern replaces the complex subquery-based 'latest record per entity' approach from Module 6 (lesson 10) with a cleaner, more readable solution.

blocks.title: ROW_NUMBER + CTE = deduplication
blocks.content: The top-N pattern with N=1 is SQL's standard deduplication technique. Assign ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY tiebreaker DESC) AS rn, then filter WHERE rn = 1. This keeps exactly one row per key — the row that the tiebreaker (usually a date or timestamp) identifies as the most relevant. At HydroAlpes, this pattern deduplicates Bronze data where the same reading was ingested twice.

sections.title: LAG and LEAD — Accessing Other Rows

blocks.content: In Module 6 (lesson 6), you learned that SQL rows have no position — and that comparing one row with a neighboring row requires a self-join. Window functions provide a far cleaner solution: LAG accesses a value from a previous row in the window, and LEAD accesses a value from a following row. Both require ORDER BY inside OVER to define what 'previous' and 'following' mean. LAG and LEAD are the window functions that replace self-joins for day-over-day comparisons, gap detection, and trend analysis — the operations that data engineers perform most frequently on time-series data.
LAG — Looking Backward

-- Compare each day's reading with the previous day (LAG)
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       LAG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (ORDER BY mr.date) AS prev_day_kw,
       mr.daily_kw - LAG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (ORDER BY mr.date) AS day_change
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-07'
ORDER BY mr.date;

LAG(daily_kw) OVER (ORDER BY date) returns the daily_kw value from the previous row in date order. For January 2, the previous row is January 1 (12.40). For January 3, the previous row is January 2 (16.72). The first row (January 1) has no previous row, so LAG returns NULL. The day_change column shows the difference between today and yesterday — positive values mean consumption increased, negative values mean it decreased. This same analysis required a self-join in Module 6; the window function version is a single expression.
LEAD — Looking Forward

LEAD is the mirror of LAG: it returns a value from the next row in the window order. LEAD(daily_kw) OVER (ORDER BY date) returns the next day's reading. The last row in the window has no next row, so LEAD returns NULL. LEAD is useful for computing forward-looking metrics: how does today's reading compare to tomorrow's? Both LAG and LEAD accept an optional offset parameter — LAG(daily_kw, 7) returns the reading from 7 rows back (approximately one week if there are daily readings), enabling week-over-week comparisons.
The Default Value Parameter

-- LAG with a default value: replace NULL with 0 for the first row
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       LAG(mr.daily_kw, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY mr.date) AS prev_day_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-03'
ORDER BY mr.date;

LAG accepts three arguments: the column to look up, the offset (default 1), and a default value to return when no previous row exists. LAG(daily_kw, 1, 0) returns the previous row's daily_kw, looking back 1 row, and returns 0 if there is no previous row. This eliminates the NULL in the first row, which can simplify downstream calculations that do not handle NULLs well. Use the default parameter when NULL would cause problems; omit it when NULL correctly signals 'no previous data.'

blocks.title: LAG and LEAD replace self-joins
blocks.content: The day-over-day comparison that required a self-join in Module 6 (lesson 6) — joining the table with itself on meter_id and date = date + 1 day — can be written as a single LAG expression. The window function version is shorter, easier to read, and typically faster because the database can compute LAG in a single pass over the ordered partition rather than performing a full self-join. At HydroAlpes, Lena rewrote all self-join-based comparisons to LAG/LEAD when the team adopted window functions.

Lesson 4 showed that adding ORDER BY inside OVER turns SUM into a running total. But the running total grew from the beginning of the partition to the current row — an unbounded start. What if you need a 7-day moving average that considers only the most recent 7 days, not the entire history? SQL provides frame specifications — the ROWS BETWEEN clause — that control exactly which rows the window function considers relative to the current row. Frame specifications unlock moving averages, sliding sums, and bounded aggregations that are essential for time-series analysis.
The Default Frame

When you write SUM(daily_kw) OVER (ORDER BY date), the database uses a default frame: ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW. This means the window starts at the first row of the partition and extends to the current row — producing a running total that grows as you move down the ordered rows. The default frame is why ORDER BY inside OVER changes SUM from a total to a running total. Understanding the default frame demystifies the behavior: it is not magic, it is a specific frame specification that happens to be the default.
The 7-Day Moving Average

-- 7-day moving average: average of the current row and the 6 preceding rows
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) AS moving_avg_7d
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-14'
ORDER BY mr.date;

ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW defines a sliding window of 7 rows: the current row plus the 6 rows before it. For January 7 (the first row with a full 7-day window), the average is computed over January 1–7. For January 8, the window slides: January 2–8. January 1 drops out and January 8 enters. This sliding behavior is what makes it a moving average — the window moves forward as you progress through the data. The first 6 rows have fewer than 7 rows in their window, so their averages are based on whatever rows are available.
Common Frame Specifications

rows: ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
From the start of the partition to the current row
Running total, running average (the default with ORDER BY)
ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
The current row and 6 rows before it
ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
Partition-level aggregate (same as PARTITION BY without ORDER BY)
The previous row, the current row, and the next row

blocks.title: Moving averages smooth noise
blocks.content: Individual daily readings fluctuate: 12.40, 16.72, 21.08, 14.55. The 7-day moving average smooths these fluctuations: 15.80, 16.81, 16.31, 16.46. The smoothed line reveals the trend that the daily noise obscures. Moving averages are standard in energy analytics, financial analysis, and any time-series domain where short-term fluctuations should not drive long-term decisions. At HydroAlpes, reservoir level trends use a 30-day moving average to separate seasonal patterns from daily weather effects.

FIRST_VALUE and LAST_VALUE return the first or last value in the window's frame. FIRST_VALUE(daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY date) returns the first day's reading for each region — the January 1 value that serves as the baseline for the month. LAST_VALUE returns the last value, but it has a subtle trap that catches many SQL users: the default frame with ORDER BY only extends to the current row, not to the end of the partition. This means LAST_VALUE often returns the current row's value rather than the partition's actual last value — unless you explicitly set the frame.
FIRST_VALUE — The Baseline

-- Each day's reading alongside the first day's reading (baseline)
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       FIRST_VALUE(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS first_day_kw,
       mr.daily_kw - FIRST_VALUE(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS change_from_baseline
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-07'
ORDER BY mr.date;

FIRST_VALUE returns 12.40 for every row — the January 1 reading. The change_from_baseline column shows how each day compares to the starting point. This is a common reporting pattern: show how a metric has moved relative to a reference point (start of month, start of quarter, start of year). At HydroAlpes, FIRST_VALUE computes the baseline reservoir level at the start of each month for capacity monitoring.
The LAST_VALUE Trap

-- WRONG: LAST_VALUE with default frame returns the CURRENT row's value
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       LAST_VALUE(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS last_value_wrong    -- Returns current row, not last row!
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-05'
ORDER BY mr.date;

-- CORRECT: Set the frame to include the entire partition
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       LAST_VALUE(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
       ) AS last_value_correct  -- Returns the actual last row's value
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-05'
ORDER BY mr.date;

The 'wrong' version returns a different value for each row because the default frame ends at CURRENT ROW — so LAST_VALUE returns the current row's own value, which is useless. The 'correct' version explicitly sets the frame to ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING, extending the window to the entire partition. Now LAST_VALUE returns the actual last value (January 5's reading) for every row. This trap is the most common LAST_VALUE mistake, and the fix is always the same: set the frame explicitly.

blocks.title: Always set the frame for LAST_VALUE
blocks.content: LAST_VALUE with the default frame (which ends at CURRENT ROW) is almost never what you want. Always include ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING when using LAST_VALUE. FIRST_VALUE does not have this problem because the default frame starts at UNBOUNDED PRECEDING, which is where the first value lives. This asymmetry is a design quirk of SQL that trips up experienced engineers.

The previous lessons taught individual window functions and their mechanics. This lesson combines them into the practical patterns that HydroAlpes' data engineering team uses in production: deduplication with ROW_NUMBER, gap detection with LAG, percentile classification with NTILE, and partition-level filtering with window aggregates. Each pattern solves a specific, recurring data engineering problem and replaces a more complex approach (self-join, correlated subquery, or multiple CTEs) with a single window function expression.
Deduplication

-- Deduplicate: keep only the most recent reading per meter per day
-- (handles cases where Bronze ingestion produced duplicates)
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT mr.*,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY mr.meter_id, mr.date
           ORDER BY mr.date DESC  -- in case of true duplicates, pick arbitrarily
         ) AS rn
  FROM   bronze.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region
FROM   ranked
WHERE  rn = 1;

ROW_NUMBER partitioned by the composite key (meter_id, date) assigns rank 1 to one row per key combination. If duplicates exist (two rows for the same meter on the same day), only rank 1 survives. This is HydroAlpes' standard deduplication pattern at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary. It handles any number of duplicates — whether 2 copies or 20 — and always produces exactly one row per meter per day. The pattern is deterministic only if the ORDER BY produces a unique sort; with true duplicates, the choice between them is arbitrary, which is acceptable because they are identical.
Gap Detection with LAG

-- Detect gaps: find days where a meter missed a reading
-- A gap occurs when the previous reading date is more than 1 day ago
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.date,
       LAG(mr.date) OVER (
         PARTITION BY mr.meter_id
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS prev_date,
       mr.date - LAG(mr.date) OVER (
         PARTITION BY mr.meter_id
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS days_since_prev
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-31'
ORDER BY mr.date;
