You can compose multi-step transformations with CTEs that read like pipeline stages. You can rank, compare, and window across partitions. You can create the tables and views that make all of this possible. These are not classroom skills — they are the skills that data engineers use on their first day, their hundredth day, and every day in between. The foundation is built. Go use it.

modules.title: Why SQL Matters for Data Engineers
modules.description: Understand why SQL endures as the universal data language, how it fits into the modern data engineering stack, and how the HydroAlpes scenario maps to a real-world data engineering context.

sections.title: The Language Every Data Platform Speaks

blocks.content: Every data platform you will encounter as a data engineer — from a small PostgreSQL database on a single server to a massive Spark cluster processing terabytes — speaks SQL. The operational database where HydroAlpes stores its customer records uses SQL. The analytical warehouse where HydroAlpes runs billing reports uses SQL. The Spark engine that processes millions of daily meter readings has a SQL interface. The cloud data lakehouse that stores years of historical turbine telemetry accepts SQL queries. No other language has this reach. Python is essential for scripting and orchestration, but it cannot query a database directly. SQL is the language that bridges them all.
A Language from 1974

SQL — Structured Query Language — was designed at IBM in the early 1970s based on a mathematical model of data called the relational model. The original idea was deceptively simple: store data in tables with rows and columns, and let users describe the data they want without specifying how to retrieve it. That idea turned out to be one of the most durable in computing history. Fifty years later, every major database, warehouse, and analytical engine implements SQL. Languages, frameworks, and tools have come and gone in that time — but SQL persists because the problem it solves (getting structured data out of storage) never goes away.

blocks.title: Why SQL endures
blocks.content: SQL survives not because it is the best possible language, but because it solves a universal problem (querying structured data) in a way that is simple enough to learn in a week and powerful enough to handle terabyte-scale analytics. Every time a new database or engine is created, the designers face a choice: invent a new query language or support SQL. They almost always choose SQL, because users already know it.
SQL in the Modern Data Stack

The modern data engineering stack has many layers. Data arrives from source systems — sensors, applications, APIs. It flows through ingestion pipelines into storage layers. It gets transformed, cleaned, and enriched. It reaches warehouses and lakehouses where analysts and dashboards consume it. SQL appears at almost every layer. Ingestion tools use SQL to extract data from source databases. Transformation tools like dbt express every transformation as a SQL query. Warehouses like Snowflake and BigQuery use SQL as their primary interface. Even Apache Spark, a distributed engine designed for Python and Scala, added Spark SQL because so many engineers think in SQL.

blocks.title: Where SQL appears in the data engineering stack
blocks.content:
Sources              Ingestion         Transformation      Consumption
┌─────────┐       ┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────┐
│ Sensors │       │ Extract  │      │ Clean,       │    │Dashboard │
│ APIs    │ ───▶  │ from DB  │ ───▶ │ validate,    │ ─▶ │ Reports  │
│ Files   │       │ via SQL  │      │ aggregate    │    │ ML models│
└─────────┘       └──────────┘      │ via SQL      │    └──────────┘
                                    └──────────────┘
                       ▲                  ▲                  ▲
                       │                  │                  │
                      SQL                SQL                SQL
                   (extract)         (transform)          (query)
blocks.caption: SQL is not confined to one stage of the pipeline. It appears in extraction, transformation, and consumption — making it the single most transferable skill a data engineer can have.

visual-recommendation.description: A polished data stack diagram showing four horizontal stages (sources, ingestion, transformation, consumption) with SQL callouts at each stage where SQL appears, using HydroAlpes brand colors.

blocks.content: HydroAlpes — Where SQL Runs

HydroAlpes is a Swiss-Austrian hydroelectric company that you will get to know well throughout this course. Their data engineering team manages pipelines that process 350,000 daily meter readings, telemetry from 1,800 hydro turbines, water levels from 12 Alpine reservoirs, and hourly energy prices from the European EPEX SPOT exchange. SQL runs at every layer of their stack. The operational PostgreSQL database that stores meter metadata uses SQL. The Spark jobs that transform raw meter files into clean analytical datasets use Spark SQL. The cloud warehouse that serves dashboards and billing reports uses SQL. When Lena, the lead data engineer, needs to check data quality, she writes a SQL query. When Markus, the junior engineer, needs to debug a pipeline, he runs a SQL query against the staging database.

This universality is why SQL is the first language HydroAlpes teaches every new data engineer — before Spark, before Airflow, before any framework. A data engineer who knows SQL can query any database, debug any pipeline, and communicate with any team. A data engineer who knows Python but not SQL can write scripts, but cannot interact with the data directly where it lives. In this course, you will learn SQL from scratch, using HydroAlpes' data to make every concept concrete and every query realistic.

blocks.title: Common misconception
blocks.content: SQL is sometimes dismissed as a 'beginner' language because its syntax looks simple. This is misleading. Writing a basic SELECT query is simple. Writing an efficient analytical query with window functions, CTEs, and multi-table joins that runs correctly against millions of rows requires genuine expertise. SQL is easy to start but deep enough to sustain an entire career.

sections.title: What SQL Is and What It Is Not

blocks.content: When you write Python, you tell the computer exactly how to perform each step: open this file, loop through each row, check this condition, add this value to a running total, write the result. You control every detail of the process. SQL works differently. When you write a SQL query, you describe the result you want — which columns, from which table, with which filters — and the database figures out how to produce it. You never write a loop. You never manage memory. You never specify which rows to process first. This difference in philosophy is the most important thing to understand about SQL before writing your first query.
Declarative vs Imperative

Programming languages fall into two broad categories based on how they express computation. Imperative languages like Python tell the computer what to do step by step — they are recipes with explicit instructions. Declarative languages like SQL describe the desired result and leave the execution strategy to the system. The distinction is like the difference between giving someone turn-by-turn driving directions ('turn left on Main Street, drive 2 km, turn right on Oak Avenue') versus giving them a destination ('take me to the nearest hospital'). With directions, you control the route. With a destination, the navigator chooses the best route based on traffic, road conditions, and distance.

left.label: Python (Imperative)
points: You describe how to compute the result, step by step
You manage loops, variables, and data structures
You control the order of operations
More control, more responsibility
Flexible — can do anything

right.label: SQL (Declarative)
points: You describe what result you want
The database manages execution strategy
The database optimizer chooses the best order
Less control, less boilerplate
Specialized — best at structured data operations

blocks.content: The Same Question, Two Languages

Consider a simple question: what is the total energy consumption per region for HydroAlpes? In Python, you would open a file, read it into a data structure, loop through each row, maintain a dictionary of region totals, add each row's consumption to the correct region, and finally print the dictionary. In SQL, you write one sentence: SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw) FROM meter_readings GROUP BY region. The SQL version does not mention files, loops, dictionaries, or iteration. It states the desired output — region totals — and the database determines the fastest way to produce it.

# Python approach: imperative, step-by-step
import csv

region_totals = {}  # dictionary to accumulate totals

with open('meter_readings.csv') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:  # loop through every row
        region = row['region']
        kw = float(row['daily_kw'])
        if region not in region_totals:
            region_totals[region] = 0
        region_totals[region] += kw  # accumulate manually

for region, total in sorted(region_totals.items()):
    print(f"{region}: {total:.2f} kW")

-- SQL approach: declarative, describe the result
SELECT region,
       SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   meter_readings
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;

The Python version is 14 lines of code that you must read sequentially to understand. The SQL version is 4 lines that read almost like an English sentence: select the region and the sum of daily_kw from the meter_readings table, grouped by region, ordered by region. Both produce the same result, but the SQL version lets the database choose the execution strategy — including parallel processing, index usage, and memory management — without you writing any of that logic.
What SQL Does Not Do

SQL is specialized for structured data operations: retrieving, filtering, aggregating, joining, and transforming data that lives in tables. It is not a general-purpose programming language. You cannot write a web server in SQL, train a machine learning model in SQL alone, or build a user interface. SQL has no loops (in the traditional sense), no file handling, no network communication, and limited string processing compared to Python. This specialization is a strength, not a weakness — SQL does one thing and does it extremely well. As a data engineer, you will use SQL for the data operations and Python for everything else.

blocks.title: The optimizer advantage
blocks.content: Because SQL is declarative, the database has freedom to choose the best execution plan. A query optimizer analyzes your query, considers the data size, available indexes, and system resources, then generates an efficient plan. This means the database can often execute your query faster than hand-written procedural code, because it has information about the data that you do not. You will see this in action in Module 10 when you learn about query plans.

sections.title: SQL in the Data Engineering Stack

blocks.content: A data engineer's daily work involves many tools: Python for scripting and orchestration, Spark for distributed processing, Airflow for scheduling pipelines, and various storage systems for keeping data organized. SQL is not a replacement for any of these tools — it is the language that connects them. The PostgreSQL database that stores HydroAlpes' meter metadata speaks SQL. The Spark engine that processes millions of turbine readings has a SQL interface. The cloud warehouse that serves the billing dashboard accepts SQL queries. Understanding where SQL fits in relation to these other tools is essential before you start writing queries, because it determines when you reach for SQL and when you reach for something else.
The Four Layers

HydroAlpes' data stack has four layers, and SQL appears in three of them. The ingestion layer pulls raw data from source systems — SCADA sensors, meter uploads, weather APIs, and the EPEX energy market feed. Some of these sources are databases, and extracting data from a database means writing SQL queries. The transformation layer cleans, validates, and enriches the raw data — casting types, filtering invalid readings, joining meter data with substation reference tables. These transformations can be written in SQL, in Python, or in PySpark. The storage layer holds the data in databases, warehouses, or lakehouses — all of which expose a SQL interface. The consumption layer serves analysts, dashboards, and reports — almost exclusively through SQL queries.

rows: SELECT * FROM operational.meters WHERE updated_at > last_sync
Joining meter readings with substation reference data
Persist data in databases, warehouses, lakehouses

blocks.content: SQL and Python — Partners, Not Competitors

New data engineers sometimes wonder whether they should learn SQL or Python. The answer is both — they solve different problems. Python excels at orchestration (scheduling pipelines, handling retries, sending alerts), at working with APIs and unstructured data (parsing JSON, downloading files, calling REST endpoints), and at complex business logic that does not fit naturally into SQL's set-based model. SQL excels at querying structured data (filtering, aggregating, joining tables), at data quality checks (counting nulls, detecting duplicates, verifying referential integrity), and at analytics (window functions, running totals, rankings).

At HydroAlpes, Lena and Markus use both languages every day. Their Airflow DAGs are written in Python — Python handles the scheduling, the retries, the alerting, and the file management. But inside those DAGs, the actual data transformations are often SQL queries executed against the warehouse or the Spark engine. The Python code orchestrates when things happen and handles errors. The SQL code defines what happens to the data. This division of labor — Python for control flow, SQL for data operations — is the dominant pattern in modern data engineering.
SQL and Spark

Apache Spark is a distributed data processing engine that can handle datasets far too large for a single database. Spark has its own Python API (PySpark), but it also has Spark SQL — a SQL interface that lets you write standard SQL queries against distributed DataFrames. This means a data engineer who knows SQL can query terabytes of data on a Spark cluster without learning the PySpark API. At HydroAlpes, some pipelines are written entirely in PySpark's Python API, some use Spark SQL exclusively, and most use a mix of both. The SQL you learn in this course transfers directly to Spark SQL — the syntax is nearly identical.

blocks.title: The most transferable skill
blocks.content: If you change jobs and the new company uses Snowflake instead of PostgreSQL, your SQL skills transfer. If they use BigQuery instead of Spark, your SQL skills transfer. If they use dbt instead of custom Python pipelines, your SQL skills become even more central. SQL is the one language that works everywhere data lives in structured form. This portability is why it is the first language in this curriculum.

Every lesson in this course uses data from HydroAlpes, a fictional Swiss-Austrian hydroelectric company that manages reservoirs, hydro turbines, pumped storage facilities, and a consumer electricity grid across the Alpine region. HydroAlpes is part of the European Energy Data Alliance (EEDA), a consortium of energy companies that share data standards for interoperability. Using the same company throughout the course means you build familiarity with the data over time — by Module 5, you will recognize column names, region codes, and table relationships without needing to reread their definitions. This lesson introduces the company, its data sources, and the analytical questions that drive its data engineering work.
The Company

HydroAlpes operates 12 reservoirs and 1,800 hydro turbines across four Alpine regions: BERN and ZURICH in Switzerland, INNSBRUCK and SALZBURG in Austria. The company generates electricity by releasing water from mountain reservoirs through turbines, and during periods of low demand, it pumps water back up into the reservoirs to store energy for peak hours — a technique called pumped storage. On the consumption side, HydroAlpes manages 350,000 smart meters that record daily energy usage for residential, commercial, and industrial customers. The company also purchases and sells electricity on the EPEX SPOT exchange, Europe's energy market, where hourly prices fluctuate based on supply and demand.

blocks.caption: HydroAlpes generates data on both the production side (reservoirs, turbines) and the consumption side (meters, customers). The data engineering team processes both into analytical outputs.

visual-recommendation.description: A polished company overview diagram showing HydroAlpes' two data domains (generation and consumption), the data sources in each, and the data engineering team that connects them to business outputs. Alpine mountain imagery in the background.

blocks.content: The Data Sources

rows: Daily energy consumption per meter, reported every midnight
SCADA sensor data: RPM, temperature, water flow, power output
Hourly water level, inflow, and outflow measurements
Temperature, precipitation, and snowmelt forecasts from external API
Hourly EPEX SPOT market prices per market zone
Reference table linking meters and turbines to grid substations

blocks.content: The EEDA Data Standard

HydroAlpes is a member of the European Energy Data Alliance, a consortium of energy companies that agreed on a common set of column names and data types for core entities. This means that the meter_id column in HydroAlpes' data has the same name, the same type (VARCHAR), and the same meaning as the meter_id column in every other EEDA member's data. The shared columns are meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id, and turbine_id. HydroAlpes adds its own domain-specific columns on top of this standard — reservoir_level_m, water_flow_m3s, pumping_schedule_id, generation_kw, head_pressure_m, and dam_id — that reflect the unique characteristics of hydroelectric operations.
The Data Engineering Team

At the junior level, HydroAlpes has one data engineering team with two members: Lena, the lead data engineer who designed the pipeline architecture, and Markus, a junior engineer who joined six months ago and is learning SQL. You will see their names throughout the course — Lena when the lesson discusses design decisions or best practices, and Markus when the lesson addresses common questions and mistakes that new engineers encounter. The team is responsible for the entire data pipeline: ingesting raw data, transforming it into clean analytical datasets, and maintaining the warehouse that serves dashboards and reports.

blocks.title: Why a consistent scenario matters
blocks.content: Using the same company, the same column names, and the same data throughout the course means you build familiarity over time. By Module 4, you will not need to reread the column descriptions — you will know that meter_id identifies a meter, daily_kw is the daily energy reading in kilowatts, and region is one of BERN, ZURICH, INNSBRUCK, or SALZBURG. This familiarity frees your mental energy to focus on the new SQL concept each lesson teaches.

sections.title: Bronze, Silver, and Gold at HydroAlpes

blocks.content: Raw data from HydroAlpes' meters and turbines does not go directly to a dashboard. The daily meter CSV file has inconsistent types — the daily_kw column arrives as text, not as a number. Some rows contain negative energy values from malfunctioning sensors. Date formats vary between the Swiss and Austrian regional feeds. Before any analyst or billing system can use this data, it must be cleaned, validated, and transformed into a reliable format. HydroAlpes organizes this work into three layers — Bronze, Silver, and Gold — where each layer has a specific purpose and a clear contract about what the data looks like. This pattern is called the medallion architecture.
Bronze — The Raw Archive

The Bronze layer stores data exactly as it arrived from the source system, with no transformations, no cleaning, and no filtering. HydroAlpes' Bronze layer receives the daily meter CSV file from each region, the hourly turbine Parquet files from the SCADA system, the weather JSON responses from the forecast API, and the EPEX SPOT price feed. The only operation performed in Bronze is copying — the raw file is written to storage as-is. If a meter exports a negative daily_kw value because of a sensor glitch, that negative value appears in Bronze. If a CSV date is formatted as '22.03.2026' instead of the standard '2026-03-22', Bronze stores it unchanged.

The purpose of keeping raw data untouched is debuggability. When something goes wrong downstream — a billing report shows unexpected totals, or a reservoir capacity forecast is off — the team can always return to Bronze and examine exactly what arrived from the source. If Bronze had already been cleaned, the original evidence would be lost. Bronze is the audit trail, the source of truth, the 'before' picture that makes every 'after' picture verifiable. At HydroAlpes, Bronze data is retained for two years to support regulatory audits and historical investigations.

blocks.title: The medallion architecture at HydroAlpes
blocks.content:
Raw Sources              Bronze             Silver              Gold
┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│ Meter CSV│ ──copy──▶│ Raw CSV  │      │ Validated    │    │ Regional     │
│ SCADA    │  as-is  │ Raw Parq │ ───▶ │ Typed        │ ─▶ │ summaries    │
│ Weather  │         │ Raw JSON │ SQL  │ Joined       │SQL │ Billing      │
│ Prices   │         │ Raw CSV  │      │ Filtered     │    │ Capacity plan│
└──────────┘         └──────────┘      └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
                      No changes        SQL cleans,         SQL aggregates,
                      Exact copy        casts types,        computes KPIs,
                                        joins reference,    pivots by region
                                        removes bad rows

                      Debuggable        Reliable            Business-ready
blocks.caption: Each layer has a clear purpose. Bronze preserves raw evidence. Silver produces reliable, typed, validated data. Gold produces the aggregated outputs that business consumers need. SQL operates at every transition.

blocks.content: Silver — Cleaned and Validated

The Silver layer is where the heavy transformation work happens. A pipeline reads from Bronze, applies a series of operations, and writes the results to Silver. These operations include type casting (converting the daily_kw column from text to a proper numeric type), filtering (removing rows with negative energy values or null meter IDs), date standardization (converting all date formats to the ISO standard YYYY-MM-DD), and joining with reference tables (adding the substation name and capacity from the substation dimension table). At HydroAlpes, many of these transformations are expressed as SQL queries — a topic you will learn in detail starting in Module 3.

The contract between Silver and its consumers is strict: every column has a defined data type, no invalid values remain, reference data has been joined, and the data is stored in an efficient format for fast reading. If the Bronze CSV has a date column stored as text, Silver has that column stored as a proper DATE type. If Bronze has rows with null meter IDs (a data quality issue), Silver has removed them. This contract means that anyone reading from Silver — an analyst running a SQL query, a dashboard refreshing its data, a billing calculation — does not need to worry about data quality. The Silver pipeline has already handled it.
Gold — Business-Ready Aggregates

The Gold layer contains pre-computed aggregates and business-ready datasets that consumers use directly. While Silver contains individual meter readings (one row per meter per day), Gold contains summaries: total consumption per region per month, average turbine output per reservoir, peak demand hours per season, and billing totals per customer type. Gold queries read from Silver, apply GROUP BY aggregations, window functions, and pivot operations, and write the results to tables that dashboards and reports query directly. At HydroAlpes, Gold tables are refreshed daily after the Silver pipeline completes.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: New data engineers sometimes skip the Silver layer and go straight from Bronze to Gold — cleaning and aggregating in a single step. This works for small projects, but it creates problems at scale. When a Gold number looks wrong, you cannot tell whether the issue is a data quality problem (a bad source record) or an aggregation bug (a wrong GROUP BY). The Silver layer separates these concerns: if Silver data is clean, the bug is in the Gold query. If Silver data is wrong, the bug is in the Silver pipeline.

Before learning formal SQL syntax, it helps to read a complete query and see that SQL is not as foreign as it might look. This lesson walks through a real query against HydroAlpes' meter readings table, line by line, without formal definitions or rules. The goal is simple: by the end of this lesson, you should be able to look at a SQL query and understand roughly what it does, even if you could not write one yet. Formal syntax rules start in Module 3 — for now, we are building intuition.
A First Query

-- Find the total daily energy consumption per region
-- for residential customers in January 2025
SELECT   region,
         SUM(daily_kw) AS total_consumption_kw
FROM     silver.meter_readings
WHERE    customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
  AND    date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND    date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY total_consumption_kw DESC;

Read the query from top to bottom as if it were an English sentence. SELECT region and the sum of daily_kw (call it total_consumption_kw) FROM the meter_readings table in the silver schema WHERE the customer_type is RESIDENTIAL AND the date is in January 2025, GROUP BY region, and ORDER BY total consumption descending (highest first). Every SQL query follows this pattern: choose what you want (SELECT), say where the data lives (FROM), apply conditions (WHERE), group if needed (GROUP BY), and sort the results (ORDER BY). The database handles everything else.

The result is a table with two columns and four rows — one row per region, sorted by total consumption from highest to lowest. Zurich's residential customers consumed the most energy in January 2025. This result set is temporary — it exists only for the duration of the query and does not modify the underlying meter_readings table. Every SQL query produces a result set like this: a table of rows and columns that answers the question you asked.
Breaking Down the Clauses

rows: Chooses which columns appear in the result
region and the SUM of daily_kw
Names the table to read from
Filters rows that do not match conditions
Groups rows so aggregate functions work per group

blocks.content: What Did Not Happen

Notice what the query does not contain. There is no loop. There is no variable declaration. There is no instruction to open a file or allocate memory. There is no mention of indexes, partitions, or execution strategy. The query says what data to produce, and the database decides how to produce it efficiently. This is the declarative nature of SQL in practice — and it is why a four-line SQL query can replace a twenty-line Python script for structured data operations.

blocks.title: Reading SQL gets easier fast
blocks.content: SQL has a small vocabulary compared to most programming languages. The core clauses — SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, JOIN — cover the vast majority of queries you will write as a data engineer. Once you recognize these clauses, you can read almost any SQL query by scanning for them and understanding each one's role. This course teaches each clause in its own lesson, building up the full vocabulary one piece at a time.

SQL has a surprising property that confuses many new learners: the order in which you write a query is not the order in which the database executes it. You write SELECT first, but the database processes FROM first. You write WHERE after FROM, and the database does execute WHERE after FROM — but it executes GROUP BY before SELECT, which means you cannot use a column alias defined in SELECT inside your WHERE clause. Understanding this execution order prevents a category of errors that frustrate beginners and helps you reason about what the database is actually doing with your query.
Written Order vs Execution Order

When you write a SQL query, the clauses appear in a fixed order dictated by the language syntax: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY. This is the written order — the order the SQL language requires. But the database does not process the clauses in this order. It starts with FROM (identify the table), then WHERE (filter individual rows), then GROUP BY (group the surviving rows), then HAVING (filter groups), then SELECT (compute the output columns), and finally ORDER BY (sort the results). The written order puts SELECT first because it is what the human cares about most — the output. The execution order puts FROM first because the database needs to know where the data is before it can do anything else.

blocks.title: Written order vs logical execution order
blocks.content:
Written Order               Logical Execution Order
(how you type it)           (how the database processes it)

1. SELECT  ─────────────▶   5. SELECT
2. FROM    ─────────────▶   1. FROM     ← start here
3. WHERE   ─────────────▶   2. WHERE
4. GROUP BY ────────────▶   3. GROUP BY
5. HAVING  ─────────────▶   4. HAVING
6. ORDER BY ────────────▶   6. ORDER BY ← end here
blocks.caption: The database starts with FROM (find the data), then filters (WHERE), groups (GROUP BY), filters groups (HAVING), computes output (SELECT), and finally sorts (ORDER BY). SELECT is step 5, not step 1.

blocks.content: Why Execution Order Matters

The execution order explains several SQL behaviors that otherwise seem arbitrary. Consider this query: SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw FROM silver.meter_readings GROUP BY region HAVING total_kw > 1000000. In many databases, this query fails. The alias total_kw is defined in the SELECT clause (step 5), but HAVING executes at step 4 — before the alias exists. The database has not yet computed the SELECT clause when it evaluates HAVING, so it does not recognize total_kw. You must write HAVING SUM(daily_kw) > 1000000 instead, repeating the expression.

-- This FAILS in most databases:
-- HAVING cannot see aliases defined in SELECT
SELECT   region,
         SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM     silver.meter_readings
GROUP BY region
HAVING   total_kw > 1000000;   -- Error: column "total_kw" does not exist

-- This WORKS:
-- Repeat the expression instead of using the alias
SELECT   region,
         SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM     silver.meter_readings
GROUP BY region
HAVING   SUM(daily_kw) > 1000000;  -- Correct: uses the expression directly

The same principle explains why ORDER BY can use aliases but WHERE cannot. ORDER BY executes last (step 6), after SELECT (step 5) has created the aliases. So ORDER BY total_kw DESC works. But WHERE executes at step 2, before SELECT, so WHERE total_kw > 0 fails for the same reason HAVING total_kw > 1000000 fails — the alias does not exist yet. These are not arbitrary rules — they follow logically from the execution order.
Tracing a HydroAlpes Query

Consider the January residential consumption query from the previous lesson. The database processes it in this order. Step 1 (FROM): locate the silver.meter_readings table and identify its 350,000 rows of daily readings. Step 2 (WHERE): scan those rows and keep only the ones where customer_type is RESIDENTIAL and date is in January 2025 — perhaps 30,000 rows survive. Step 3 (GROUP BY): group the surviving rows by region, creating four groups (BERN, ZURICH, INNSBRUCK, SALZBURG). Step 4 (HAVING): no HAVING clause, so all four groups pass. Step 5 (SELECT): for each group, compute SUM(daily_kw) and label it total_consumption_kw, and include the region column. Step 6 (ORDER BY): sort the four result rows by total_consumption_kw descending.

blocks.title: The alias trap
blocks.content: The most common beginner mistake caused by execution order is using a SELECT alias in WHERE. If you write WHERE total_kw > 0 instead of WHERE daily_kw > 0, the database returns an error because total_kw does not exist at the WHERE stage. When you see this error, remember the execution order: WHERE runs before SELECT, so it cannot see anything SELECT creates.

SQL has an international standard — ANSI SQL — maintained by the American National Standards Institute. The standard defines the core syntax that every compliant database must support: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, JOIN, and the fundamental data types. But no database implements only the standard. Every database vendor adds proprietary extensions — extra functions, alternative syntax, and features that do not exist in the standard. The result is that SQL written for PostgreSQL may not run unchanged on Snowflake, and SQL written for BigQuery may need adjustments for Spark SQL. Understanding what is standard and what is dialect-specific is important because data engineers frequently work across multiple platforms.
What Is Standard

The good news is that the core of SQL — the operations you will use most often — is highly portable. SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, JOIN, and the basic aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) work identically across every major database. Window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD) are part of the ANSI standard and supported by PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Spark SQL, and SQL Server. Common Table Expressions (WITH ... AS) are also standardized and widely supported. This course teaches ANSI-standard SQL first, which means the skills you build here work on any platform.
Where Dialects Diverge

The divergences tend to appear in three areas: date and time functions (every database has its own approach to date arithmetic), string functions (subtle differences in function names and behavior), and DDL statements (CREATE TABLE syntax varies significantly). The core analytical operations — filtering, grouping, joining, and windowing — are remarkably consistent. When you encounter a dialect difference, the adjustment is usually a function name change, not a fundamental rethinking of the query.
This Course's Approach

This course uses ANSI-standard SQL as the primary syntax and PostgreSQL 16 as the reference database for examples and output formatting. PostgreSQL is a natural choice because it is open source, widely used in data engineering, and has one of the most standards-compliant SQL implementations available. When a concept has important dialect differences — like LIMIT versus TOP, or string concatenation syntax — the lesson notes the difference in a callout so you know what to expect when working with other platforms. HydroAlpes uses PostgreSQL for their operational database and Spark SQL for distributed processing, so both dialects appear naturally in the scenario.

blocks.title: The 90% rule
blocks.content: Roughly 90% of the SQL you write as a data engineer is standard SQL that works on any platform without changes. The remaining 10% — mostly date functions, string functions, and platform-specific DDL — requires minor adjustments when moving between databases. Learning standard SQL first means you are productive on any platform immediately and only need to look up the platform-specific functions when you encounter them.

When you write a SQL query, two separate pieces of software are involved: the database engine that stores data and executes the query, and the client application that sends the query and displays the results. This separation is fundamental to how SQL works in practice. The database engine runs on a server (or a cluster of servers) and manages the actual data — files on disk, indexes, query optimization, and execution. The client runs on your computer — a terminal tool, a graphical application, or a notebook — and provides the interface where you type queries and read results. Understanding this separation helps you navigate the tools you will use daily as a data engineer.
The Client-Server Model

The database engine is the server: it listens for connections, receives SQL queries, processes them against the stored data, and returns results. The client is any application that connects to the server, sends a query, and displays the response. Multiple clients can connect to the same database simultaneously — Lena might run an analytical query from her notebook while Markus runs a data quality check from the terminal, both hitting the same PostgreSQL server at HydroAlpes. The server handles both queries independently, using its own resources to process each one.

blocks.title: Client-server architecture for SQL databases
blocks.content:
┌──────────────────┐     SQL query      ┌──────────────────────┐
│   Client          │ ───────────────▶  │   Database Engine     │
│   (your tool)     │                    │   (PostgreSQL)        │
│                   │  ◀───────────────  │                      │
│   psql            │     result set     │   Stores data        │
│   DBeaver         │                    │   Parses SQL         │
│   Jupyter Notebook│                    │   Optimizes plan     │
│   Python script   │                    │   Executes query     │
└──────────────────┘                    │   Returns rows       │
                                        └──────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The client sends SQL text to the database engine. The engine parses, optimizes, and executes the query, then returns the result set to the client. The client only displays results — it never touches the data directly.

blocks.content: Common Client Tools

As a data engineer, you will use several types of SQL clients depending on the situation. Terminal clients like psql (for PostgreSQL) and mysql (for MySQL) run in the command line and are fast for quick queries and scripting. Graphical clients like DBeaver, DataGrip, and Azure Data Studio provide a visual interface with features like query autocompletion, result grids, and schema browsers. Notebook environments like Jupyter and Databricks notebooks let you mix SQL queries with Python code and visualizations. Python libraries like psycopg2 and SQLAlchemy let you send SQL queries from Python scripts — this is how pipelines execute SQL programmatically.

At HydroAlpes, Lena uses DBeaver for exploratory queries because the visual interface makes it easy to browse table structures and inspect results. Markus prefers psql in the terminal for quick checks because it starts instantly and does not require opening a separate application. Their Airflow pipelines use psycopg2, a Python library, to execute SQL queries programmatically — the pipeline connects to PostgreSQL, runs the transformation query, and logs the result without any human interaction. All three approaches send the same SQL to the same database engine. The client is a matter of preference and context.
Connecting to a Database

Every client needs four pieces of information to connect to a database: the host (the server's address), the port (which network port the database listens on — 5432 is the PostgreSQL default), the database name (a server can host multiple databases), and credentials (a username and password). These are called connection parameters, and you will encounter them in every tool and library that connects to a database. At HydroAlpes, the production database runs on a server at db.hydroalpes.internal, the development database runs at dev-db.hydroalpes.internal, and Lena's laptop runs a local PostgreSQL instance at localhost for testing. The SQL queries are identical across all three — only the connection parameters change.

blocks.title: Common confusion
blocks.content: New engineers sometimes conflate the database and the client. If a query returns wrong results, the issue is in the SQL or the data — not in the client tool. DBeaver, psql, and a Python script all send the same SQL text to the same database engine. Switching clients does not change query results. If you get different results from the same query, check whether you are connected to different databases (production vs development), not whether the client is causing the difference.

This module introduced the foundation: SQL as the universal data language, its declarative nature, its position in the data engineering stack, the HydroAlpes scenario, the Bronze-Silver-Gold architecture, how a query reads and executes, the dialect landscape, and the client-server model. You have seen a complete query, traced its execution order, and understood why SQL persists across every platform and generation of technology. The remaining nine modules build on this foundation systematically, teaching you one concept at a time until you have a complete SQL toolkit for data engineering.
What Each Module Covers

rows: Module 2: Tables and the Relational Model
Tables, columns, data types, keys, constraints, schemas — the structures that SQL operates on
Selecting columns, expressions, aliases, DISTINCT, LIMIT — the building blocks of every query
WHERE, comparison operators, AND/OR, LIKE, BETWEEN, IN, NULL handling, ORDER BY
COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP BY, HAVING — transforming rows into summaries
INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS, self-joins — combining tables into richer datasets
Scalar subqueries, table subqueries, EXISTS, CTEs — composing complex logic
OVER, PARTITION BY, ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD — analytics that preserve detail
CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, ALTER TABLE, views — building pipeline infrastructure
SQL inside Spark, inside warehouses, inside dbt, query plans, indexes, transactions

blocks.content: The HydroAlpes Thread

Every module uses HydroAlpes data to make concepts concrete. In Module 2, you will see the full data model — the tables, their columns, their relationships, and the constraints that keep the data reliable. In Module 3, you will write your first SELECT queries against meter_readings. In Module 5, you will compute the regional consumption aggregates that HydroAlpes uses for billing. In Module 6, you will join meter readings with substations, turbines with reservoirs, and prices with generation data — the multi-table queries that produce HydroAlpes' analytical Gold layer. In Module 8, you will use window functions to compute running totals of reservoir levels and rank turbines by performance within each region.

By Module 10, you will trace a complete analytical question through every technique you have learned: defining the tables (Module 2), selecting the right columns (Module 3), filtering to the relevant period and customer type (Module 4), aggregating by region (Module 5), joining with dimension tables for context (Module 6), composing the query with CTEs (Module 7), adding analytical calculations with window functions (Module 8), and understanding how the database executes the whole thing efficiently (Module 10). Each module adds one layer of capability, and the HydroAlpes scenario ensures that every layer feels practical and connected.
Set-Based Thinking

The single most important skill you will develop in this course is not syntax — it is a way of thinking. Python programmers think in terms of individual rows: for each row, do something. SQL operates on sets of rows simultaneously: describe the operation, and the database applies it to every qualifying row at once. This shift from row-by-row thinking to set-based thinking is what separates someone who can write SQL from someone who writes SQL well. Every module reinforces this mental model, starting with Module 2's introduction to how tables organize data as sets of rows that conform to the same structure.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 2, you will learn the structures that SQL operates on — tables, columns, rows, data types, and the relationships that connect them. You will see HydroAlpes' full data model and understand why the data is organized the way it is. The foundation from this module — SQL's universality, its declarative nature, and the execution order — will make every concept in Module 2 clearer. The building blocks are in place. Now it is time to see what they build.

modules.title: Tables and the Relational Model
modules.description: Understand tables as the fundamental unit of structured data, learn how columns, rows, data types, keys, and constraints create the contracts that make SQL reliable, and gain familiarity with HydroAlpes' schema.

blocks.content: In Module 1 you learned that SQL is a declarative language for querying structured data, and that the database engine handles execution while you describe the result you want. But before you can query anything, you need to understand the structure that SQL operates on: the table. A table is the fundamental unit of organized data in every relational database. HydroAlpes' meter readings live in a table. Their turbine telemetry lives in another table. Their substation reference data lives in a third. Every SQL query you will write in this course reads from at least one table, and understanding what a table is — not just what it looks like, but what rules it enforces — is the foundation that makes everything else possible.
The Spreadsheet Analogy

A table looks a lot like a spreadsheet: it has columns across the top and rows going down. Each column has a name — meter_id, daily_kw, date, region — and each row represents one record. If you have used Excel or Google Sheets, the visual layout of a table will feel familiar. But a database table enforces rules that a spreadsheet does not. Every value in a column must be the same type — you cannot put text in a numeric column. Every row must have the same set of columns — you cannot add an extra column to just one row. And the database itself enforces these rules automatically, rejecting any data that violates them. A spreadsheet lets you put anything anywhere. A table does not.

-- A glimpse at what HydroAlpes' meter_readings table looks like
-- Each row is one meter's reading for one day
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  5;

meter_id       | daily_kw | date       | region    | customer_type
---------------+----------+------------+-----------+--------------
CH-BERN-00142  |    18.34 | 2025-01-15 | BERN      | RESIDENTIAL
CH-BERN-00143  |     7.91 | 2025-01-15 | BERN      | RESIDENTIAL
AT-SLZB-08821  |   142.60 | 2025-01-15 | SALZBURG  | COMMERCIAL
CH-ZRCH-45002  |    22.17 | 2025-01-15 | ZURICH    | RESIDENTIAL
AT-INNB-33410  |   487.30 | 2025-01-15 | INNSBRUCK | INDUSTRIAL

Five rows, five columns. Each row represents one meter's reading on January 15, 2025. The meter_id column holds text identifiers. The daily_kw column holds decimal numbers. The date column holds dates. The region column holds text. The customer_type column holds text from a fixed set of values. Every row follows the same structure, and every value in a column is the same type. This uniformity is what makes SQL possible — the database can apply the same operation (filter, sort, aggregate) to every row because every row has the same shape.
Columns Define the Structure

Columns are the structural skeleton of a table. When you create a table, you declare its columns — their names and their types — and that declaration becomes a contract. Every row that enters the table must provide a value (or an explicit NULL) for every column. No row can have a column that the table does not define. No row can omit a column that the table requires. This contract means that any SQL query can rely on the column names and types being exactly as declared. When you write WHERE daily_kw > 0, you know that daily_kw exists in every row and that it contains a number — because the table's column definition guarantees it.
Rows Are the Data

Rows are the actual data that conforms to the column structure. HydroAlpes' meter_readings table has 350,000 rows on any given day — one for each smart meter. Each row is independent: it does not know about the rows before or after it, and it has no inherent position or order. This is a crucial difference from a spreadsheet, where row 5 comes after row 4. In a table, rows have no position. When you query a table without an ORDER BY clause, the database may return rows in any order — and that order can change between executions. If you want a specific order, you must ask for it explicitly.

blocks.title: Common misconception
blocks.content: New SQL users often assume that a table stores rows in the order they were inserted, and that SELECT without ORDER BY returns them in that order. This is not guaranteed. The database stores rows in whatever physical arrangement is most efficient, and query results without ORDER BY can appear in any order. Always use ORDER BY when the order of results matters.
Tables vs Files

left.label: CSV File
points: Columns defined by the header row — no enforcement
Any value can go in any cell — text in a numeric column is allowed
Rows can have different numbers of fields (malformed data)
No built-in mechanism to prevent duplicate rows
Reading requires parsing the entire file from disk

right.label: Database Table
points: Columns defined by the schema — enforced by the database
Values must match the column type — the database rejects violations
Every row has exactly the declared columns — no exceptions
Primary keys and unique constraints prevent duplicates
Indexes and the query optimizer enable fast selective reads

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' Bronze layer stores raw data as CSV and Parquet files — unstructured storage with no enforcement. Their Silver and Gold layers store data in database tables with enforced schemas. The transition from file to table is the transition from 'anything goes' to 'only valid data is allowed.' This is exactly why the medallion architecture separates raw ingestion (Bronze files) from validated storage (Silver tables). SQL operates on the table side of this boundary, where structure is guaranteed.

blocks.title: Key takeaway
blocks.content: A table is not just a visual layout of rows and columns — it is a contract. The column definitions guarantee that every row has the same structure and that every value has the correct type. This contract is what makes SQL reliable: your queries can assume the data's shape because the database enforces it.

Every column in a table has a data type that determines what kind of values it can hold. The meter_id column holds text — a sequence of characters like 'CH-BERN-00142.' The daily_kw column holds decimal numbers like 18.34. The date column holds calendar dates like 2025-01-15. Choosing the right data type for each column is one of the most important decisions a data engineer makes when designing a table, because the type affects storage size, query performance, mathematical accuracy, and what operations are valid. You cannot compute an average of text values. You cannot search for a date pattern inside a number. The data type defines what the column can do.
Numeric Types

SQL provides several numeric types because different numbers have different requirements. INTEGER stores whole numbers (no decimals) in 4 bytes and holds values up to roughly 2.1 billion — suitable for counts, IDs that are numbers, and quantities. BIGINT uses 8 bytes and holds values up to roughly 9.2 quintillion — needed when INTEGER is not large enough, such as for row counts in very large tables. NUMERIC (also called DECIMAL) stores exact decimal values with a specified precision and scale — NUMERIC(8,2) holds numbers up to 999,999.99 with exactly two decimal places, suitable for money and measurements where exact precision matters. DOUBLE PRECISION stores approximate decimal values in 8 bytes — fast and storage-efficient but subject to tiny rounding errors that make it unsuitable for financial calculations.

rows: Turbine RPM, row counts, capacity_mw (whole megawatts)
Row counts for tables with billions of records
Exact decimals with p digits, s after the point
reservoir_level_m as NUMERIC(8,2), price_eur_mwh as NUMERIC(10,4)
daily_kw (energy readings where tiny rounding is acceptable)
Rarely used — DOUBLE PRECISION is preferred for better accuracy

blocks.content: Text Types

SQL stores text in two primary types. VARCHAR(n) stores variable-length text up to n characters — VARCHAR(50) holds any text from 0 to 50 characters, and the database enforces the maximum length. TEXT stores variable-length text with no declared maximum — it can hold anything from an empty string to a multi-megabyte document. In PostgreSQL, VARCHAR and TEXT have essentially identical performance, so the choice is about intent: VARCHAR(50) documents that the column should contain short values (like a region code), while TEXT says the column has no meaningful length limit (like a comment field). HydroAlpes uses VARCHAR for structured identifiers (meter_id as VARCHAR(20), region as VARCHAR(30)) and TEXT for free-form fields like turbine maintenance notes.

blocks.title: Why type choice matters for data engineers
blocks.content: In a CSV file, the daily_kw value '18.34' and the meter_id value 'CH-BERN-00142' are both just text. The database does not know one is a number and the other is an identifier. When you load this data into a table with typed columns, daily_kw becomes a DOUBLE PRECISION that supports SUM, AVG, and comparison operators, while meter_id becomes a VARCHAR that supports string matching and concatenation. The data type is what gives the column its capabilities.
Date and Time Types

Working with dates and times is one of the most common tasks in data engineering, and getting the type right prevents entire categories of bugs. DATE stores a calendar date without a time component — 2025-01-15 — and is the right choice for HydroAlpes' daily meter readings where each row represents one full day. TIMESTAMP stores both date and time — 2025-01-15 14:30:00 — and is needed for turbine telemetry where readings arrive multiple times per hour. TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE adds timezone awareness, which matters when HydroAlpes processes data across Swiss and Austrian time zones. INTERVAL stores a duration — 30 days or 4 hours — and is useful for computing date differences and scheduling offsets.
The BOOLEAN Type

BOOLEAN stores TRUE or FALSE — a two-valued type that represents yes/no conditions. HydroAlpes uses BOOLEAN columns for flags like is_valid (whether a reading passed quality checks), is_pumping (whether a reservoir is in pumping mode), and is_active (whether a meter is currently reporting). Boolean columns are particularly useful in WHERE clauses: WHERE is_valid = TRUE (or simply WHERE is_valid) filters to only valid records. They are also compact — a BOOLEAN typically uses 1 byte of storage.
HydroAlpes' Column Types

-- HydroAlpes' meter_readings table uses these types:
-- meter_id:       VARCHAR(20)        — structured text identifier
-- daily_kw:       DOUBLE PRECISION   — energy reading, rounding acceptable
-- date:           DATE               — one reading per calendar day
-- region:         VARCHAR(30)        — geographic region code
-- customer_type:  VARCHAR(20)        — RESIDENTIAL, COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL
-- substation_id:  VARCHAR(20)        — reference to the substations table

-- HydroAlpes' reservoir_levels table uses these types:
-- reservoir_id:      VARCHAR(20)     — reservoir identifier
-- reservoir_level_m: NUMERIC(8,2)    — exact measurement in meters
-- water_flow_m3s:    NUMERIC(10,2)   — cubic meters per second
-- ts:                TIMESTAMP       — precise measurement time

Notice that HydroAlpes uses DOUBLE PRECISION for daily_kw (energy readings where a rounding error of 0.0000001 kW is negligible) but NUMERIC for reservoir_level_m (water level measurements where exact decimal representation matters for safety thresholds). This is a deliberate design choice: the data type reflects the precision requirement of the business domain, not a generic default. When you design tables, choose the type that matches the data's real-world precision needs.

blocks.title: The string-for-everything trap
blocks.content: It is tempting to store every column as VARCHAR or TEXT — after all, any value can be represented as text. But storing daily_kw as text means you cannot compute SUM(daily_kw) without casting, you cannot filter WHERE daily_kw > 100 without unexpected results (text comparison: '9' > '100'), and you lose all of the database's numeric optimizations. Always use the correct type. If a column holds numbers, make it numeric. If it holds dates, make it DATE.

Columns define the structure. Rows are the data that fills that structure. Each row in HydroAlpes' meter_readings table represents one fact: a specific meter recorded a specific energy consumption on a specific date. The row for meter CH-BERN-00142 on 2025-01-15 says that meter consumed 18.34 kW that day, it belongs to the BERN region, and its customer type is RESIDENTIAL. That row exists independently of every other row in the table — it does not depend on the row before it, the row after it, or any particular position in the table. Understanding how rows work — their independence, their lack of order, and their relationship to the column structure — is essential for writing correct SQL.
One Row, One Fact

Good table design follows a simple rule: each row represents exactly one fact at a consistent level of detail. In meter_readings, the level of detail is one meter, one day. Every row has the same granularity — you will not find one row representing a single meter's daily reading and another row representing an entire region's monthly total in the same table. This consistency is what makes aggregation work: when you write SUM(daily_kw), every row contributes one day's reading for one meter, so the sum means something. If some rows were daily and others were monthly, the sum would be meaningless because you would be adding incompatible units.

This principle is called the grain of the table — the level of detail that each row represents. In meter_readings, the grain is meter-day. In turbine_telemetry, the grain is turbine-timestamp (a reading every few seconds). In energy_prices, the grain is market-zone-hour. Knowing a table's grain is the first thing an experienced data engineer checks before writing a query, because the grain determines what aggregations are valid and how many rows to expect.
Rows Have No Inherent Order

In a Python list, element 0 comes before element 1. In a CSV file, line 5 comes after line 4. In a database table, rows have no position. The database stores rows in whatever physical arrangement it chooses for efficiency — which might be the order of insertion, the order of a primary key index, or something else entirely. When you execute a SELECT query without ORDER BY, the database returns rows in whatever order it finds fastest, and that order can change between executions. This is not a bug — it is a fundamental property of the relational model.

-- Without ORDER BY, the database returns rows in no guaranteed order.
-- These two executions of the same query might return different orderings.

-- First execution might return:
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  3;

Running the same query again might return CH-BERN-07881 first. The WHERE and LIMIT conditions are satisfied either way — the query asked for any 3 rows from BERN, and the database delivered 3 rows from BERN. If you need the most recent readings first, you must say so: ORDER BY date DESC. If you need the highest consumption first, you must say so: ORDER BY daily_kw DESC. SQL never assumes an order — you always ask explicitly.
Row Count and Scale

At HydroAlpes, the meter_readings table accumulates 350,000 new rows every day — one per meter. Over a year, that is roughly 128 million rows. The turbine_telemetry table, with readings every 10 seconds from 1,800 turbines, adds about 15.5 million rows per day. A data engineer must always be aware of how many rows a table contains, because row count affects query performance, storage cost, and the appropriateness of different SQL patterns. A query that runs instantly on 1,000 rows might take minutes on 128 million rows if it is not written carefully.

blocks.title: The grain question
blocks.content: When you encounter a new table, the first question to ask is: what does one row represent? The answer — the grain — tells you what the table measures, what aggregations are meaningful, and how many rows to expect. If meter_readings has a grain of meter-day and there are 350,000 meters, you know a year of data contains roughly 128 million rows. This mental arithmetic guides every query you write against the table.
Rows vs Records

The terms row, record, and tuple all refer to the same thing — one horizontal entry in a table. Row is the most common term in everyday data engineering. Record appears in database documentation and application development. Tuple is the formal mathematical term from relational algebra. This course uses row consistently, but you will encounter all three in the wild. When a colleague says 'check the record for meter CH-BERN-00142' or when documentation mentions 'each tuple in the relation,' they mean the same thing: one row in the table.

Every table should have a way to uniquely identify each row. Without a unique identifier, there is no reliable way to distinguish one row from another — and if you cannot distinguish rows, you cannot update a specific row, delete a specific row, or join rows across tables. The column (or combination of columns) that uniquely identifies each row is called the primary key. It is the most important structural element of a table after the columns themselves, and every well-designed table in a production database has one.
Single-Column Primary Keys

The simplest primary key is a single column whose value is unique for every row. In HydroAlpes' meters table, the meter_id column is the primary key — every meter has a unique identifier like CH-BERN-00142, and no two rows share the same meter_id. In the substations table, substation_id is the primary key. In the turbines table, turbine_id is the primary key. Each of these tables has one row per entity (one meter, one substation, one turbine), and the entity's identifier serves as the natural key.

-- The meters table: meter_id is the primary key.
-- Every meter_id value is unique — no duplicates allowed.
SELECT meter_id, region, customer_type, installation_date
FROM   silver.meters
LIMIT  4;

meter_id       | region    | customer_type | installation_date
---------------+-----------+---------------+------------------
CH-BERN-00142  | BERN      | RESIDENTIAL   | 2019-03-14
CH-BERN-00143  | BERN      | RESIDENTIAL   | 2019-03-14
AT-SLZB-08821  | SALZBURG  | COMMERCIAL    | 2020-07-22
CH-ZRCH-45002  | ZURICH    | RESIDENTIAL   | 2021-01-09

The database enforces the primary key constraint automatically. If a pipeline attempts to insert a second row with meter_id 'CH-BERN-00142', the database rejects the insert with an error — a duplicate key violation. This enforcement prevents a category of data quality issues that plagues systems without primary keys: duplicate records that cause double-counting in aggregations, ambiguous joins, and inconsistent updates.
Composite Primary Keys

Some tables need more than one column to uniquely identify a row. In meter_readings, a single meter_id is not unique — meter CH-BERN-00142 has a row for every day of the year. A single date is not unique either — every meter has a reading on 2025-01-15. But the combination of meter_id and date is unique — there is exactly one reading for meter CH-BERN-00142 on 2025-01-15. This combination is a composite primary key: two columns that together identify each row uniquely.

-- The meter_readings table: (meter_id, date) is the composite primary key.
-- Each meter has exactly one reading per day.
-- The database rejects a second reading for the same meter on the same day.

SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
ORDER BY date DESC
LIMIT  3;

Composite keys are common in fact tables — tables that record events or measurements over time. The key typically combines an entity identifier (which meter, which turbine) with a time dimension (which day, which hour). HydroAlpes' turbine_telemetry table has a composite key of (turbine_id, ts) because each turbine can have multiple readings per day, but only one reading at any given timestamp.
Why Primary Keys Exist

Primary keys serve three purposes that matter to data engineers. First, they prevent duplicate rows — the database will not allow two rows with the same key, which eliminates the double-counting problem before it starts. Second, they enable efficient lookups — the database automatically creates an index on the primary key, making WHERE meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142' fast even in a table with hundreds of millions of rows. Third, they are the anchors for joins — when you connect meter_readings to the meters table, you join on meter_id because it is the primary key that uniquely identifies each meter. You will see this in detail when you learn about foreign keys in the next lesson.

blocks.title: Tables without primary keys
blocks.content: Some tables in production systems lack primary keys — often because someone created a 'quick' staging table and never added the constraint. These tables invite duplicate rows, make targeted updates impossible, and slow down joins. At HydroAlpes, Lena's pipeline design rule is simple: every Silver and Gold table must have a primary key. Bronze tables may lack keys because they store raw data as-is, but anything downstream must be keyed.

HydroAlpes stores meter metadata in one table (meters) and meter readings in another table (meter_readings). The meter_id column appears in both tables — in meters it is the primary key that uniquely identifies each meter, and in meter_readings it is a reference that says which meter produced each reading. This reference — a column in one table that points to a primary key in another table — is called a foreign key. Foreign keys are the mechanism that connects tables, enabling the joins that combine data from multiple sources into richer, more useful datasets.
What a Foreign Key Does

A foreign key creates a relationship between two tables. The table that contains the foreign key is called the child table (or referencing table), and the table whose primary key is referenced is called the parent table (or referenced table). In HydroAlpes' schema, meter_readings is the child and meters is the parent — each reading references a meter. The database can enforce this relationship: if you declare meter_id in meter_readings as a foreign key referencing meters(meter_id), the database will reject any reading whose meter_id does not exist in the meters table. This enforcement is called referential integrity — it guarantees that every reference points to a real record.

blocks.title: Foreign key relationship between meters and meter_readings
blocks.content:
meters (parent)                      meter_readings (child)
┌─────────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────────────┐
│ meter_id  (PK)          │          │ meter_id  (FK → meters)      │
│ region                  │◄─────────│ date                         │
│ customer_type           │          │ daily_kw                     │
│ installation_date       │          │ region                       │
│                         │          │ customer_type                │
└─────────────────────────┘          │ (PK: meter_id + date)        │
                                     └──────────────────────────────┘

   1 meter ──────────────────▶ many readings
   (one row in meters)          (many rows in meter_readings)
blocks.caption: The foreign key meter_id in meter_readings points to the primary key meter_id in meters. One meter has many readings — this is a one-to-many relationship.

visual-recommendation.description: A polished entity-relationship diagram showing the one-to-many relationship between the meters and meter_readings tables, with primary key and foreign key annotations, using standard ER notation.

blocks.content: One-to-Many Relationships

The most common relationship in data engineering is one-to-many: one parent row relates to many child rows. One meter has many readings. One substation connects to many meters. One region contains many substations. In each case, the parent table holds the unique entity (the meter, the substation, the region) and the child table holds the repeated events or associations. The foreign key always lives in the child table — the table on the 'many' side — because that is where the reference occurs for each individual row.
Referential Integrity

When the database enforces a foreign key, it prevents two kinds of invalid data. First, it prevents orphan rows — rows in the child table that reference a parent that does not exist. If someone tries to insert a meter reading for meter 'XX-FAKE-99999' and that meter does not exist in the meters table, the database rejects the insert. Second, it prevents dangling references — if someone tries to delete a meter from the meters table while readings for that meter still exist in meter_readings, the database rejects the delete (or cascades the delete to the readings, depending on configuration). Both protections keep the data consistent.

At HydroAlpes, referential integrity catches a real problem. New meters are sometimes installed in the field before the meter registration team enters them into the meters table. The meter starts reporting readings immediately, but the Bronze pipeline receives readings for a meter_id that does not yet exist in Silver. Without a foreign key, those readings would load into meter_readings with an orphan meter_id — no metadata, no region, no customer type. With the foreign key, the pipeline catches the missing meter at the Silver boundary, logs a warning, and holds the readings in a staging area until the registration team catches up.

blocks.title: Why relationships matter for SQL
blocks.content: Foreign keys are not just a data quality tool — they are the foundation of joins. When you write SELECT ... FROM meter_readings JOIN meters ON meter_readings.meter_id = meters.meter_id, you are following the foreign key relationship from child to parent. Every join you write in Module 6 will follow a relationship defined by a foreign key. Understanding these relationships now makes joins intuitive later.

Primary keys and foreign keys are the most common constraints, but they are not the only rules a database can enforce. SQL provides additional constraint types that let you express data quality rules directly in the table definition. Instead of writing a validation script that checks whether daily_kw is positive, you can declare a constraint that makes the database reject negative values automatically. Instead of relying on your pipeline to never insert a row with a null meter_id, you can make the database guarantee it. Constraints move data quality enforcement from application code into the database itself, where it cannot be bypassed.
NOT NULL

The NOT NULL constraint declares that a column must have a value — it cannot be left empty. If meter_id is declared as NOT NULL, every row must provide a meter_id. Attempting to insert a row without a meter_id (or with an explicit NULL) results in an error. At HydroAlpes, the core identification columns — meter_id, date, turbine_id, reservoir_id — are all NOT NULL because a reading without an identity is useless. Measurement columns like daily_kw might allow NULL to represent genuinely missing data (a meter that failed to report), but the identity columns never allow it.
UNIQUE

The UNIQUE constraint ensures that no two rows have the same value in the constrained column (or combination of columns). A primary key is implicitly UNIQUE and NOT NULL, but you can also apply UNIQUE to non-key columns. In HydroAlpes' meters table, the meter_id is the primary key, but the GPS coordinate pair (latitude, longitude) might also be UNIQUE — two meters should not share the same physical location. If they do, it indicates a data entry error.
CHECK

The CHECK constraint evaluates a condition for every row and rejects any row where the condition is false. This is the most flexible constraint — it can express any rule that involves the row's own values. At HydroAlpes, the meter_readings table uses CHECK (daily_kw >= 0) on the Silver-layer table to ensure that no negative consumption values survive the Bronze-to-Silver transformation. The reservoirs table uses CHECK (reservoir_level_m >= 0 AND reservoir_level_m <= max_level_m) to prevent physically impossible water levels.

-- HydroAlpes' Silver meter_readings table with constraints
-- This is what the CREATE TABLE statement looks like (you will learn
-- the full CREATE TABLE syntax in Module 9 — for now, focus on
-- the constraints after each column)

CREATE TABLE silver.meter_readings (
    meter_id      VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,    -- must have a value
    daily_kw      DOUBLE PRECISION   NOT NULL,    -- must have a value
    date          DATE               NOT NULL,    -- must have a value
    region        VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL,    -- must have a value
    customer_type VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,    -- must have a value
    substation_id VARCHAR(20),                    -- nullable: not all meters mapped yet
    PRIMARY KEY (meter_id, date),                 -- composite key: one reading per meter per day
    CHECK (daily_kw >= 0)                         -- no negative consumption
);

Read the CREATE TABLE statement top to bottom. Each column has a name, a type, and optional constraints. The meter_id column is VARCHAR(20) and NOT NULL — it must always contain a text value up to 20 characters. The daily_kw column is DOUBLE PRECISION and NOT NULL. The substation_id column has no NOT NULL constraint, meaning it allows NULL values — some meters have not yet been mapped to a substation. The PRIMARY KEY clause at the bottom declares the composite key, and the CHECK clause enforces that daily_kw is never negative.
DEFAULT

The DEFAULT constraint provides a value when no value is specified during insertion. If a column has DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE, inserting a row without specifying that column automatically fills it with today's date. At HydroAlpes, audit columns use defaults extensively: created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP records when each row was inserted, and is_valid BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE assumes readings are valid until the quality check pipeline marks them otherwise. Defaults reduce the burden on the insertion code and ensure that administrative columns are always populated.

blocks.title: Constraints as documentation
blocks.content: Beyond enforcement, constraints serve as documentation. When Markus reads the table definition and sees CHECK (daily_kw >= 0), he immediately knows that negative values are invalid — without reading any pipeline code or documentation. Constraints make the data rules visible in the schema itself, which is invaluable when joining a team or debugging a pipeline months after it was written.

HydroAlpes has dozens of tables: meter_readings, meters, turbine_telemetry, turbines, reservoirs, substations, regions, energy_prices, and more. Dumping all of these into a single flat namespace would create confusion — is the meter_readings table the raw Bronze version or the cleaned Silver version? Is the monthly_consumption table a Gold aggregate or a staging artifact? SQL solves this problem with a two-level organizational hierarchy: databases contain schemas, and schemas contain tables. This hierarchy lets you organize tables by purpose, control access by team, and keep different versions of the same data separate.
What a Schema Is

A schema is a named container for tables within a database. Think of it as a folder — tables inside the schema are accessed by prefixing the schema name: silver.meter_readings, gold.regional_consumption. Every database has a default schema (in PostgreSQL, it is called public), and any table created without specifying a schema lands there. But production databases rarely use the default — they organize tables into named schemas that reflect their purpose, their data quality level, or their owning team.

At HydroAlpes, the database uses three schemas that map directly to the medallion architecture. The bronze schema holds raw ingested data with no quality guarantees. The silver schema holds cleaned, validated, typed data with primary keys and constraints. The gold schema holds pre-aggregated datasets optimized for analytical consumption. When Lena writes a query, the schema prefix tells her exactly what quality level she is working with: silver.meter_readings is reliable, bronze.meter_readings is raw.

blocks.title: HydroAlpes' database organization
blocks.content:
Database: hydroalpes_warehouse
│
├── Schema: bronze
│   ├── meter_readings      (raw CSV data, no constraints)
│   ├── turbine_telemetry   (raw SCADA data)
│   ├── weather_forecasts   (raw JSON data)
│   └── energy_prices       (raw market data)
│
├── Schema: silver
│   ├── meter_readings      (cleaned, typed, PK enforced)
│   ├── meters              (dimension table)
│   ├── turbine_telemetry   (cleaned, typed)
│   ├── turbines            (dimension table)
│   ├── reservoirs          (dimension table)
│   ├── substations         (dimension table)
│   └── regions             (dimension table)
│
└── Schema: gold
    ├── regional_consumption   (monthly aggregates by region)
    ├── turbine_performance    (daily performance summaries)
    ├── reservoir_status       (hourly reservoir KPIs)
    └── billing_summary        (monthly billing totals)
blocks.caption: The database contains three schemas aligned with the medallion architecture. Table names can repeat across schemas because the schema prefix makes them unambiguous.

blocks.content: Fully Qualified References

A fully qualified table reference includes the schema name: silver.meter_readings. This is important when the same table name exists in multiple schemas — meter_readings appears in both bronze and silver, and without the schema prefix, the database would use the default schema (public), which is neither. HydroAlpes' coding standard requires fully qualified references in all pipeline queries. Ad-hoc queries in the silver schema may omit the prefix if the user's search path is configured to look in silver first, but Lena's rule is explicit: always include the schema in any query that will be saved, versioned, or shared.

-- Fully qualified: schema.table.column
SELECT silver.meter_readings.meter_id,
       silver.meter_readings.daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  3;

-- With a table alias (more common and readable):
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
LIMIT  3;

The aliased version — FROM silver.meter_readings AS mr — is far more common in practice. You declare the alias once in the FROM clause, then use mr.meter_id and mr.daily_kw throughout the query.
