This keeps the query compact and readable, especially when joining multiple tables (Module 6). The AS keyword is optional in PostgreSQL (FROM silver.meter_readings mr works too), but including it makes the alias declaration explicit.
Databases

A database is the top-level container. A PostgreSQL server can host multiple databases, each fully isolated from the others — tables in one database cannot reference tables in another without special configuration. HydroAlpes uses a single database called hydroalpes_warehouse for all analytical data, with schemas providing the internal organization. Some organizations use separate databases for separate environments (development, staging, production) or for separate business units. The pattern varies, but the principle is the same: databases provide isolation, schemas provide organization within that isolation.

blocks.title: Schema as data quality signal
blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, the schema name is a trust signal. If a query reads from gold.regional_consumption, everyone knows the data has been cleaned, validated, joined, and aggregated. If it reads from bronze.meter_readings, everyone knows the data is raw and unvalidated. This convention means that a reviewer can assess the reliability of a query's inputs just by looking at the schema prefixes — without tracing the full pipeline lineage.

The previous seven lessons introduced tables, columns, data types, rows, primary keys, foreign keys, constraints, and schemas one concept at a time. This lesson assembles those pieces into HydroAlpes' complete junior-level data model — the set of tables, their columns, their types, their keys, and their relationships that you will query throughout the rest of this course. This is the reference you will return to whenever you need to recall a column name, a table relationship, or a data type. Take the time to read through each table carefully — familiarity with this model will make every module from Module 3 onward easier.
Fact Tables and Dimension Tables

HydroAlpes' tables fall into two categories that data engineers call fact tables and dimension tables. Fact tables record events and measurements — things that happen over time. Each row is a reading, a transaction, or an observation. Fact tables grow continuously: meter_readings adds 350,000 rows every day. Dimension tables describe the entities that facts reference — the meters, the turbines, the substations, the regions. Dimension tables change slowly: a new meter is installed occasionally, a substation's capacity is upgraded rarely. Joins connect facts to dimensions to enrich raw measurements with descriptive context.
The Entity-Relationship Diagram

blocks.title: HydroAlpes' junior-level data model
blocks.content:
┌──────────┐
                    │ regions  │
                    │──────────│
                    │region_id │(PK)
                    │name      │
                    │country   │
                    └────┬─────┘
                         │ 1:many
                         ▼
┌────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
│ reservoirs │     │ substations │     │ energy_prices    │
│────────────│     │─────────────│     │──────────────────│
│reservoir_id│(PK) │substation_id│(PK) │market_zone       │(PK)
│name        │     │name         │     │price_timestamp   │(PK)
│max_level_m │     │capacity_mw  │     │price_eur_mwh     │
│region      │(FK) │region       │(FK) └──────────────────┘
└──────┬─────┘     └──────┬──────┘
       │ 1:many           │ 1:many
       ▼                  ▼
┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐
│ turbines   │     │  meters    │
│────────────│     │────────────│
│turbine_id  │(PK) │meter_id    │(PK)
│reservoir_id│(FK) │substation_id│(FK)
│type        │     │region      │(FK)
│capacity_kw │     │customer_type│
└──────┬─────┘     │install_date │
       │ 1:many    └──────┬─────┘
       ▼                  │ 1:many
┌────────────────┐        ▼
│turbine_telemetry│  ┌──────────────┐
│────────────────│  │meter_readings │
│turbine_id  (FK)│  │──────────────│
│ts          (PK)│  │meter_id  (FK)│
│rpm             │  │date      (PK)│
│temp            │  │daily_kw      │
│power_output    │  │region        │
│water_flow_m3s  │  │customer_type │
└────────────────┘  └──────────────┘
blocks.caption: The ER diagram shows how HydroAlpes' tables connect through foreign keys. Fact tables (bottom) record events and reference dimension tables (top and middle) for context. Every arrow represents a foreign key relationship.

visual-recommendation.description: A professionally styled entity-relationship diagram showing HydroAlpes' full junior-level data model with all nine tables, their columns, primary keys, foreign keys, and the one-to-many relationships between them. Color-coded: fact tables in blue, dimension tables in green.

blocks.content: The EEDA Standard Columns

As a member of the European Energy Data Alliance, HydroAlpes uses the EEDA standard columns: meter_id (VARCHAR), daily_kw (DOUBLE PRECISION), date (DATE), region (VARCHAR), customer_type (VARCHAR), substation_id (VARCHAR), and turbine_id (VARCHAR). These columns appear identically in every EEDA member's schema, ensuring cross-company interoperability. HydroAlpes extends the standard with domain-specific columns: reservoir_level_m, water_flow_m3s, pumping_schedule_id, generation_kw, head_pressure_m, and dam_id — columns specific to hydroelectric operations that other EEDA members (wind, solar) do not use.

blocks.title: Reference this lesson often
blocks.content: This data model is the foundation for every query in the course. When Module 5 teaches GROUP BY with a query on meter_readings, check back here for the column names and types. When Module 6 teaches joins, check here for the foreign key relationships. When Module 8 teaches window functions, check here for the grain of each table. Bookmark this lesson — you will return to it frequently.

Every value in a table is either a real value or NULL. NULL means the value is missing, unknown, or not applicable — it is not zero, it is not an empty string, and it is not false. When a HydroAlpes meter fails to report on a given day, the daily_kw column for that day's row might be NULL — not because the consumption was zero, but because the reading is absent. When a meter has not yet been mapped to a substation, its substation_id is NULL — not because the meter has no substation, but because the mapping is unknown. This distinction between 'absent' and 'zero' is one of the most important concepts in SQL, and misunderstanding it is one of the most common sources of bugs.
NULL Is Not Zero

Zero is a value — it means 'measured and found to be zero.' NULL is the absence of a value — it means 'not measured' or 'unknown.' If a meter reported 0.00 kW on a given day, that is a valid reading: the meter was functioning, and the customer consumed no energy (perhaps the building was vacant). If a meter's daily_kw is NULL for a given day, the meter did not report at all — we have no information about consumption. The difference matters for aggregation: SUM should include the 0.00 (it is a valid reading) but should not include NULL (there is no reading to add). SQL handles this correctly — aggregate functions ignore NULLs — but only if you use NULL instead of zero to represent missing data.
Three-Valued Logic

In Python, an expression is either True or False. In SQL, an expression can be TRUE, FALSE, or NULL. This three-valued logic is the consequence of NULL representing the unknown. Consider the question: is NULL > 100? The answer is not FALSE — it is NULL, because we do not know the value, so we cannot determine whether it exceeds 100. Is NULL = NULL? Also NULL — two unknown values might or might not be equal. This behavior surprises every new SQL user, but it follows logically: any operation involving an unknown value produces an unknown result.

-- NULL comparisons always produce NULL, not TRUE or FALSE
-- These conditions are all NULL (unknown), not TRUE or FALSE:

-- Does NULL equal 100?           →  NULL (unknown)
-- Does NULL equal NULL?          →  NULL (unknown)
-- Is NULL greater than 0?        →  NULL (unknown)
-- Is NULL not equal to 'BERN'?   →  NULL (unknown)

-- This means these queries return NO rows for NULL values:
SELECT * FROM silver.meters WHERE substation_id = NULL;     -- returns nothing!
SELECT * FROM silver.meters WHERE substation_id <> 'SUB-01'; -- skips NULL rows!

The first query returns no rows because substation_id = NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE), and WHERE only keeps rows where the condition is TRUE. The correct way to find NULL values is IS NULL: SELECT * FROM silver.meters WHERE substation_id IS NULL. The second query is more subtle — it returns rows where substation_id has a non-NULL value that is not 'SUB-01', but it excludes rows where substation_id is NULL entirely. If you want rows where substation_id is either not 'SUB-01' or is NULL, you must write: WHERE substation_id <> 'SUB-01' OR substation_id IS NULL.
IS NULL and IS NOT NULL

-- Find meters that have NOT been mapped to a substation
SELECT meter_id, region
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id IS NULL;

-- Find meters that HAVE been mapped to a substation
SELECT meter_id, region, substation_id
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id IS NOT NULL;

IS NULL and IS NOT NULL are the only safe operators for testing whether a value is NULL. Never use = NULL or <> NULL — they will silently return wrong results because the comparison produces NULL instead of TRUE. This is the single most common SQL bug for new engineers, and it is subtle because the query runs without errors — it just returns fewer rows than expected. At HydroAlpes, Lena added a linting rule to the pipeline CI that flags any query containing = NULL or <> NULL and requires the developer to rewrite it using IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.

blocks.title: The silent data loss trap
blocks.content: If a WHERE clause compares a nullable column using = or <>, rows with NULL values silently disappear from the result. The query produces no error — it just returns fewer rows than you expect. When debugging a query that returns too few rows, the first thing to check is whether any filter columns contain NULLs and whether the filter correctly handles them.
NULL in Aggregations

SQL aggregate functions — SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, COUNT(column) — ignore NULL values. SUM(daily_kw) adds up all non-NULL values. AVG(daily_kw) computes the average of non-NULL values, using only the non-NULL count as the denominator. COUNT(daily_kw) counts only non-NULL values. COUNT(*) is the exception — it counts all rows regardless of NULLs. This behavior is usually correct (you do not want missing readings to drag down the average), but it can surprise you when NULLs represent zeros that should be included. You will explore this distinction thoroughly in Module 5.

This module introduced the structures that SQL operates on: tables, columns, data types, rows, keys, constraints, schemas, NULLs, and the relationships that connect tables. Before moving to Module 3 — where you will write your first real queries — there is one more concept to internalize: the fundamental difference between how Python and SQL think about data. This difference is not about syntax. It is about the mental model you use to reason about data operations. Getting this mental model right now will make every module that follows significantly easier.
Python Thinks in Rows

When you process data in Python, you typically iterate through rows one at a time. You open a file, read the first row, do something with it, read the second row, do something with it, and continue until the file is exhausted. Even when using Pandas, the mental model is often row-by-row — apply a function to each row, loop through a DataFrame, iterate over groups. The control is explicit: you decide which row to process next, you manage the loop counter, and you accumulate results in variables.

# Python mental model: process rows one at a time
total = 0
count = 0
for row in meter_readings:
    if row['region'] == 'BERN' and row['daily_kw'] is not None:
        total += row['daily_kw']  # accumulate one row at a time
        count += 1
average = total / count
SQL Thinks in Sets

SQL does not process rows one at a time. It operates on entire sets of rows simultaneously. When you write WHERE region = 'BERN', you are not looping through rows and checking each one — you are describing a set: all rows where region equals BERN. When you write AVG(daily_kw), you are not accumulating a running total — you are describing an operation on the entire set. The database decides how to execute it: which rows to read, in what order, using which algorithm. You describe the result. The database produces it.

-- SQL mental model: describe the result, the database handles iteration
SELECT AVG(daily_kw) AS avg_consumption
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  region = 'BERN'
  AND  daily_kw IS NOT NULL;

The SQL version does not mention loops, counters, or accumulators. It describes three things: the computation (AVG of daily_kw), the source (meter_readings), and the filter (BERN region, non-null values). The database handles everything else. This is not just a syntactic difference — it changes how you reason about the problem. In Python, you think about the process: 'for each row, check the condition, add to the total.' In SQL, you think about the result: 'the average of daily_kw for BERN readings.'

left.label: Row-by-Row Thinking (Python)
points: For each row, check if region is BERN
If true, add daily_kw to a running total
After the loop, divide total by count
You control the iteration order
If the loop has a bug, debug the iteration

right.label: Set-Based Thinking (SQL)
points: The set of rows where region equals BERN
Compute AVG(daily_kw) across the set
The database computes the average internally
The database chooses the optimal strategy
If the result is wrong, check the set definition

blocks.content: Why This Matters

Set-based thinking is not just a philosophical preference — it determines whether your SQL queries are correct and efficient. The most common SQL mistakes come from row-by-row thinking: trying to use a loop variable in SQL (there are none), trying to process rows in a specific order without ORDER BY (the database does not guarantee order), or trying to reference the 'previous row' without a window function (SQL rows have no position). Every time you catch yourself thinking 'for each row, do X,' translate it to 'the set of rows where X.' This translation is the core skill of SQL.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 3, you will write your first SELECT queries against HydroAlpes' tables. Every query you write will operate on sets: the set of columns you choose (SELECT), the set of rows from a table (FROM), and the subset that matches your conditions (WHERE). The relational model from this module — tables, types, keys, relationships — provides the structure. Set-based thinking provides the reasoning. Together, they make SQL powerful.

modules.title: Retrieving Data with SELECT
modules.description: Master the SELECT statement as the foundation of every SQL query, learn to choose columns, create expressions, alias results, and eliminate duplicates.

blocks.content: In Module 2 you learned the structures that SQL operates on — tables, columns, data types, keys, and the relationships that connect HydroAlpes' nine tables. Now it is time to actually retrieve data from those tables. The tool for this is the SELECT statement — the single most important statement in SQL. Every analytical query, every data quality check, every pipeline transformation, and every ad-hoc investigation begins with SELECT. You will write thousands of SELECT statements in your career as a data engineer. This lesson introduces its fundamental form: choose columns, name the table, get results.
The Basic Form

A SELECT statement in its simplest form has two parts: SELECT, which names the columns you want, and FROM, which names the table to read from. The database reads the table, extracts the requested columns for every row, and returns the result. No rows are filtered, no rows are sorted, and no rows are modified — the table itself is untouched. The result is a temporary dataset that exists only for the duration of the query. Think of SELECT as a question you ask the database: 'Show me these columns from this table.' The database answers with a result set.

This query retrieves the meter_id and daily_kw columns from every row in the silver.meter_readings table. With 350,000 meters and a year of daily data, that is roughly 128 million rows. In practice, you would rarely retrieve all rows without filtering — but the point here is the structure. SELECT names what you want. FROM names where it lives. The database does the rest.
SELECT Does Not Modify Data

An important property of SELECT: it is read-only. No matter what you write in a SELECT statement, the underlying table is never changed. You can select, filter, sort, aggregate, and join — and the original data remains exactly as it was. This is fundamentally different from Python, where reading a file and writing a modified version are separate operations that you must manage carefully. In SQL, querying data and modifying data use entirely different statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — which you will learn in Module 9). SELECT is always safe to run — it will never accidentally alter your data.

blocks.title: SELECT produces a result set
blocks.content: Every SELECT statement produces a result set — a temporary table with its own columns and rows. This result set exists only for the duration of the query and is not stored anywhere unless you explicitly save it (using CREATE TABLE AS SELECT, which you will learn in Module 9). The result set can have fewer columns than the source table, fewer rows (when filtering), different column names (when aliasing), and even computed columns that do not exist in the source.
A Complete First Query

Here is a more realistic first query — retrieving three columns from HydroAlpes' meter readings and limiting the output to five rows so the result fits on screen. You will learn LIMIT formally in lesson 7, but it is so useful for exploration that it appears here informally. The query reads: select the meter_id, daily_kw, and date columns from the silver.meter_readings table, and return only the first 5 rows.

-- Retrieve three columns, limited to 5 rows for display
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  5;

The result is a table with three columns and five rows. The column names in the result match the column names in the source table. The data types are preserved: meter_id is text, daily_kw is a decimal number, and date is a calendar date. Remember from Module 2 that rows have no inherent order — the five rows returned here are not necessarily the 'first' five inserted. They are simply five rows the database found quickly. If you run this query again, you might get different rows (or the same rows in a different order). To get consistent results, you need ORDER BY — which you will learn in Module 4.
Whitespace and Formatting

SQL ignores extra whitespace. The query above could be written on a single line — SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date FROM silver.meter_readings LIMIT 5; — and it would produce the same result. But readability matters. The convention in data engineering is to put each major clause (SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY) on its own line and to align columns vertically. This formatting makes complex queries scannable at a glance — you can immediately see which columns are selected, which table they come from, and what conditions apply. HydroAlpes' SQL style guide requires this multi-line formatting for any query that will be saved to version control.

blocks.title: The semicolon
blocks.content: Every SQL statement ends with a semicolon (;). In interactive tools like psql, the semicolon tells the client to send the query to the database. Without it, the client waits for more input. In some tools (like DBeaver or notebooks), the semicolon is optional because the tool sends the query when you click 'Run.' This course always includes the semicolon for consistency and because omitting it in scripts causes hard-to-diagnose errors.

The previous lesson showed that SELECT names the columns you want in the result. This lesson focuses on why choosing specific columns matters and how to do it well. In a production data pipeline, selecting only the columns you need is not just a style preference — it affects query performance, network transfer, memory usage, and the clarity of downstream processing. HydroAlpes' meter_readings table has columns for meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, and substation_id. A billing report needs only meter_id, daily_kw, and date. Selecting all six columns when you need three wastes resources and makes the query's intent harder to read.
Choosing Columns by Name

To select specific columns, list their names after SELECT, separated by commas. The order you list them determines the order they appear in the result — and that order does not need to match the order in the table definition. You can list date first and meter_id second even if the table defines meter_id first. You can also list the same column multiple times, though this is rarely useful. The column names must match the table's column names exactly — SQL column names are case-insensitive in PostgreSQL, but using the same case as the table definition is good practice.

-- Select three specific columns in a custom order
-- date first, then meter_id, then daily_kw
SELECT date, meter_id, daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  4;

The result has three columns in the order specified: date, meter_id, daily_kw. The region, customer_type, and substation_id columns are not included — the database never reads them (or at minimum, does not transfer them to the result). This selective reading is more efficient than retrieving all columns because the database can skip the unneeded data entirely, especially when the table is stored in a columnar format like Parquet.
Why Fewer Columns Is Better

Selecting only the columns you need has three practical benefits. First, performance: less data is read from disk, transferred across the network, and loaded into memory. For HydroAlpes' 128 million row meter_readings table, selecting 3 columns instead of 6 can cut I/O time in half. Second, clarity: a query that selects meter_id, daily_kw, date immediately communicates its intent — it is working with meter consumption over time. A query that selects all columns does not signal what it cares about. Third, stability: if someone adds a new column to the table, a query that selects specific columns is unaffected, but a query that selects all columns now includes an unexpected column that might break downstream processing.
The Column List as Documentation

In a production pipeline, the SELECT column list is a form of documentation. When Markus reads a query and sees SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region FROM silver.meter_readings, he knows exactly which columns the downstream process depends on. If someone proposes renaming the region column to operating_region, Markus can search all pipeline queries for the string 'region' and find every query that would break. This discoverability is lost when queries use SELECT * instead of explicit column lists — which is the subject of the next lesson.

-- HydroAlpes' Silver-to-Gold billing pipeline: select only what billing needs
SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw,
       date,
       customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  4;

This query selects the four columns the billing pipeline needs: which meter, how much consumption, which date, and what customer type (because billing rates differ by type). The region and substation_id columns are irrelevant for billing, so they are not selected. The query is self-documenting — anyone reading it can see exactly what data flows into the billing calculation without consulting external documentation or tracing pipeline dependencies.

blocks.title: Column selection is the first design decision
blocks.content: Every query starts with a choice: which columns do I need? This is not a trivial decision. Selecting too many columns wastes resources and obscures intent. Selecting too few means you need to rewrite the query when requirements change. The right set of columns is the minimum set that answers the question you are asking. At HydroAlpes, Lena reviews every pipeline query's SELECT list during code review to verify that no unnecessary columns are included.

sections.title: SELECT * and When to Avoid It

blocks.content: SQL provides a shorthand for selecting every column in a table: SELECT * (pronounced 'select star' or 'select all'). Instead of listing each column by name, the asterisk tells the database to include every column the table defines. This is convenient for quick exploration — when you want to see what a table looks like, SELECT * FROM silver.meters LIMIT 10 shows you all columns and a few rows in one query. But SELECT * in production pipelines is one of the most common sources of maintenance problems in data engineering. This lesson explains why.
SELECT * in Action

-- SELECT * retrieves every column in the table
SELECT *
FROM   silver.meters
LIMIT  3;

meter_id       | region    | customer_type | installation_date | substation_id | is_active
---------------+-----------+---------------+-------------------+---------------+----------
CH-BERN-00142  | BERN      | RESIDENTIAL   | 2019-03-14        | SUB-BERN-04   | true
CH-BERN-00143  | BERN      | RESIDENTIAL   | 2019-03-14        | SUB-BERN-04   | true
AT-SLZB-08821  | SALZBURG  | COMMERCIAL    | 2020-07-22        | SUB-SLZB-01   | true

The result contains six columns — every column defined in the meters table. You did not need to know the column names in advance; the asterisk retrieved them all. For exploration and debugging, this is exactly what you want: a quick look at the table's structure and content. But notice that the result includes columns you might not need (installation_date, is_active) and that the column order is determined by the table definition, not by your query. You have no control over what appears or in what order.
Three Reasons to Avoid SELECT *

The first problem is fragility. When someone adds a column to a table — say, a new last_maintenance_date column to the meters table — every query that uses SELECT * against that table now returns the new column. If your pipeline writes the result to a downstream table with a fixed schema, the extra column causes a schema mismatch error. If your pipeline feeds a dashboard, the dashboard might display the new column unexpectedly. The fix is a pipeline rebuild, a deployment, and wasted debugging time — all because a column was added to a table your query did not explicitly reference.

The second problem is performance. SELECT * retrieves every column, including large text fields, binary data, or columns that the query's consumer never uses. For HydroAlpes' meter_readings table, selecting all columns when you only need meter_id and daily_kw means the database reads and transfers the region, customer_type, and substation_id columns for 128 million rows — gigabytes of unnecessary I/O. In columnar storage formats like Parquet, the database can skip unneeded columns entirely when you name them explicitly, but SELECT * forces it to read everything.
When SELECT * Is Acceptable

rows: You want to see the table's full structure quickly
You need to inspect all columns to find the issue
Fragile to schema changes, wastes I/O
EXISTS only checks for row existence, not column values
Unclear intent, breaks when columns change
* in COUNT means 'all rows,' not 'all columns'

blocks.content: The rule at HydroAlpes is straightforward: use SELECT * for interactive exploration, never for pipeline queries. When Lena reviews a pull request and sees SELECT * in a pipeline SQL file, she requests a change to explicit column names. The time spent listing columns is repaid many times over by the stability and clarity it provides. An explicit column list survives schema changes, documents the query's data dependencies, and enables the database to optimize reads.

blocks.title: COUNT(*) is different from SELECT *
blocks.content: The asterisk in COUNT(*) does not mean 'all columns.' It means 'count all rows, regardless of NULLs.' COUNT(*) is always correct for counting total rows and does not have the maintenance problems of SELECT *. Do not avoid COUNT(*) out of a blanket aversion to asterisks — it is the right tool for its job.

When you select a column, the result set uses the column's original name by default. If you select daily_kw, the result column is called daily_kw. But sometimes the original name is unclear, too technical, or does not match what the consumer expects. HydroAlpes' billing team expects a column called consumption_kwh, not daily_kw. A calculated column like daily_kw * 30 has no name at all — the database generates an unhelpful label like '?column?' or 'expr0'. Column aliases solve both problems: they let you rename any column in the result set without changing the underlying table.
The AS Keyword

-- Rename columns in the result using AS
SELECT meter_id        AS meter,
       daily_kw        AS consumption_kwh,
       date            AS reading_date,
       customer_type   AS segment
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  4;

The result columns are now called meter, consumption_kwh, reading_date, and segment — the names after AS. The underlying table is unchanged; meter_id is still called meter_id in silver.meter_readings. The alias exists only in the result set. This is exactly what the billing team needs: a result with column names that match their reporting conventions, produced from a table with column names that match the engineering conventions.
Aliases for Computed Columns

Aliases become essential when you compute new values in SELECT. An expression like daily_kw * 0.001 converts kilowatts to megawatts, but without an alias, the result column has no meaningful name. Different databases generate different placeholder names for unnamed expressions — PostgreSQL uses ?column?, Spark SQL uses a hash, BigQuery uses f0_. None of these are useful. An alias gives the computed column a clear, descriptive name that downstream consumers can reference.

-- Without an alias, the computed column has no useful name
SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw * 0.001
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  3;

-- With an alias, the intent is clear
SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw * 0.001 AS daily_mw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  3;

The second version — with the alias daily_mw — is immediately readable. Anyone looking at the result knows the column contains daily energy in megawatts. This matters especially in pipelines where the result feeds a downstream table or dashboard: the alias becomes the column name that every downstream consumer references. A bad alias name (like val or x) is almost as unhelpful as no alias at all.
AS Is Optional (But Use It)

In PostgreSQL and most SQL dialects, the AS keyword is optional. You can write SELECT meter_id meter instead of SELECT meter_id AS meter, and the database treats meter as the alias. However, omitting AS makes the query harder to read — it looks like a typo (did the author mean to write a comma between meter_id and meter?). HydroAlpes' style guide requires AS for all aliases. The extra keyword costs nothing and makes the intent unambiguous.

blocks.title: Aliases and execution order
blocks.content: Remember from Module 1 (lesson 7) that SELECT executes at step 5 — after FROM and WHERE. This means you cannot use a SELECT alias in a WHERE clause: WHERE consumption_kwh > 100 fails because the alias does not exist yet when WHERE runs. You must use the original expression: WHERE daily_kw > 100. ORDER BY is the exception — it executes after SELECT, so ORDER BY consumption_kwh works.

SELECT is not limited to retrieving columns as they are stored. It can compute new values using arithmetic operations, function calls, and combinations of multiple columns. These computed values are called expressions. An expression like daily_kw * 0.001 creates a new column that does not exist in the table — the database computes it on the fly for every row in the result. Expressions are one of the most powerful features of SELECT because they let you transform data at query time without modifying the underlying table. Every unit conversion, percentage calculation, and derived metric in HydroAlpes' Gold layer starts as an expression in a SELECT clause.
Arithmetic Expressions

SQL supports the standard arithmetic operators: + (addition), - (subtraction), * (multiplication), / (division), and % (modulo — the remainder after division). These operators work on numeric columns and produce numeric results. When you write daily_kw * 0.001, the database multiplies every row's daily_kw value by 0.001 and includes the result in the output. The original daily_kw column in the table is not changed — the multiplication happens only in the result set.

-- Arithmetic expressions: unit conversions for HydroAlpes' energy data
SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw,                          -- original value in kilowatts
       daily_kw * 0.001 AS daily_mw,      -- convert to megawatts
       daily_kw * 30    AS est_monthly_kw, -- estimate monthly consumption
       daily_kw / 24.0  AS avg_hourly_kw  -- average hourly rate
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  4;

Each expression produces a new column in the result. The database computes daily_mw by multiplying each row's daily_kw by 0.001, est_monthly_kw by multiplying by 30, and avg_hourly_kw by dividing by 24.0. Notice the 24.0 instead of 24 — in some databases, dividing an integer by an integer produces an integer result (truncating the decimal part). Using 24.0 ensures decimal division. PostgreSQL handles this correctly either way, but the explicit decimal is a good habit for portability.
Multi-Column Expressions

Expressions can combine multiple columns. If the meter_readings table had both a consumption column and a generation column (as it does for prosumer meters in other EEDA companies), you could compute the net energy with consumption - generation. At HydroAlpes, the energy_prices table has price_eur_mwh and the meter_readings table has daily_kw. After joining these tables (which you will learn in Module 6), you could compute the daily cost with daily_kw * 0.001 * price_eur_mwh — converting kilowatts to megawatts and multiplying by the price per megawatt-hour.
String Expressions

-- String concatenation with || (PostgreSQL)
-- Build a readable label from region and customer_type
SELECT meter_id,
       region || ' - ' || customer_type AS meter_label
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  4;

meter_id       | meter_label
---------------+--------------------------
CH-BERN-00142  | BERN - RESIDENTIAL
CH-BERN-00143  | BERN - RESIDENTIAL
AT-SLZB-08821  | SALZBURG - COMMERCIAL
CH-ZRCH-45002  | ZURICH - RESIDENTIAL

The || operator concatenates strings in PostgreSQL. It joins the region value, a literal dash with spaces, and the customer_type value into a single text column. This is useful for creating human-readable labels, building composite identifiers, or formatting output for reports. Different databases use different syntax for concatenation — Spark SQL and MySQL use the CONCAT() function instead of || — but the concept is the same. This course notes dialect differences like this in callouts when they are important.
Literal Values

You can include literal values — constants that are the same for every row — in a SELECT expression. This is useful for adding labels, units, or fixed values to the result. The literal 'kW' in SELECT daily_kw, 'kW' AS unit adds a column called unit with the value 'kW' in every row. Numeric literals work the same way: SELECT meter_id, 2025 AS report_year adds a constant column. Literals are also used inside expressions: daily_kw * 0.001 uses the numeric literal 0.001.

blocks.title: Expressions keep the original data safe
blocks.content: Every expression in SELECT produces a new computed column in the result set — it never modifies the stored data. You can experiment freely with unit conversions, string formatting, and derived calculations knowing that the underlying table is untouched. This safety is one of the reasons SQL is excellent for data exploration: you can try calculations, check results, and refine your logic without any risk of corrupting the source data.

Sometimes you do not want every row — you want every unique value. How many distinct regions does HydroAlpes operate in? What customer types exist in the meter_readings table? Which dates have readings? These questions ask for the set of unique values in a column, not for every row that contains those values. SQL provides the DISTINCT keyword to eliminate duplicate rows from the result set, keeping only one copy of each unique combination of selected columns.
DISTINCT on a Single Column

region
---------
BERN
ZURICH
SALZBURG
INNSBRUCK

Without DISTINCT, this query would return 128 million rows — one for every reading — with the same four region values repeated millions of times. With DISTINCT, it returns exactly 4 rows: the unique set of regions. The database scans the column, identifies the unique values, and returns each one once. This is one of the simplest and most common SQL operations: extracting the distinct values from a categorical column to understand what exists in the data.
DISTINCT on Multiple Columns

When DISTINCT applies to multiple columns, it eliminates rows where the entire combination of selected columns is duplicated. Each unique combination appears exactly once in the result set. This is useful for understanding which combinations exist in the data — for example, which region-customer_type pairs are present in HydroAlpes' meter readings, or which dates have recorded data for which regions.

-- Find the distinct region/customer_type combinations
SELECT DISTINCT region, customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings
ORDER BY region, customer_type;

region    | customer_type
----------+--------------
BERN      | COMMERCIAL
BERN      | INDUSTRIAL
BERN      | RESIDENTIAL
INNSBRUCK | COMMERCIAL
INNSBRUCK | INDUSTRIAL
INNSBRUCK | RESIDENTIAL
SALZBURG  | COMMERCIAL
SALZBURG  | INDUSTRIAL
SALZBURG  | RESIDENTIAL
ZURICH    | COMMERCIAL
ZURICH    | INDUSTRIAL
ZURICH    | RESIDENTIAL

The result has 12 rows: 4 regions × 3 customer types. Every combination is present, which tells Lena that each region has all three customer types represented in the data. If one combination were missing — say, INNSBRUCK had no INDUSTRIAL meters — the missing row would be immediately visible in the result. Running DISTINCT on multiple columns is a quick and effective way to verify data completeness across categorical dimensions.
When DISTINCT Masks a Problem

DISTINCT removes duplicates from the result set, but it does not explain why duplicates exist. If a query against meter_readings returns duplicate rows and you add DISTINCT to make them go away, you have hidden the problem rather than fixing it. Duplicates in a fact table usually indicate a pipeline bug — the same reading was inserted twice. Adding DISTINCT to the consumption query masks the double-counting rather than addressing it. At HydroAlpes, Lena's rule is: if you need DISTINCT to get correct results from a table that has a primary key, investigate the duplicates before adding DISTINCT.

blocks.title: DISTINCT has a cost
blocks.content: DISTINCT requires the database to sort or hash the result set to identify duplicates. On large result sets — millions of rows — this can be expensive in terms of memory and processing time. If you are selecting from a table with a primary key and your result should already be unique (one row per meter per day), adding DISTINCT is a waste. Use DISTINCT intentionally, for questions about unique values, not as a blanket fix for unexpected duplicates.

HydroAlpes' meter_readings table contains 128 million rows of annual data. Retrieving all of them for a quick data check would flood your terminal, overwhelm your notebook, and waste time. SQL provides the LIMIT clause to restrict the number of rows returned — LIMIT 10 means 'give me at most 10 rows.' This is the most commonly used clause during data exploration: you want to see what the data looks like, not download the entire table. LIMIT is also the foundation for pagination — returning results in pages of fixed size, which you will encounter in API designs and reporting systems.
LIMIT — Restricting Row Count

-- Return at most 5 rows
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  5;

The query returns 5 rows even though the table has millions. If the table had fewer than 5 rows, LIMIT 5 would return all of them — LIMIT sets a maximum, not a fixed count. The rows returned are not the 'first' 5 in any meaningful order — without ORDER BY, the database returns whichever 5 rows it finds fastest. For exploration, this does not matter. For any query where the specific rows matter — the top 5 consumers, the most recent 5 readings — you must combine LIMIT with ORDER BY.

-- The top 5 highest daily readings — LIMIT with ORDER BY
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5;

Now the result is meaningful: the five highest-consuming meters on January 15, 2025, all of them industrial or large commercial customers. ORDER BY daily_kw DESC sorts the rows from highest to lowest, and LIMIT 5 keeps only the top five. This ORDER BY plus LIMIT pattern is one of the most useful combinations in SQL — you will use it for top-N queries, recent-record queries, and sampling throughout your career.
OFFSET — Skipping Rows

OFFSET skips a specified number of rows before starting to return results. Combined with LIMIT, it enables pagination. LIMIT 10 OFFSET 0 returns rows 1–10. LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10 returns rows 11–20. LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20 returns rows 21–30. This is how web applications display results in pages — 'showing results 11–20 of 1,523.' For data engineering, OFFSET is less common than LIMIT, but it appears in reporting queries and debugging scenarios.

-- Page 1: rows 1-5 (skip 0)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5 OFFSET 0;

-- Page 2: rows 6-10 (skip 5)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5 OFFSET 5;
Dialect Differences

LIMIT is supported by PostgreSQL, MySQL, Spark SQL, and BigQuery. SQL Server uses a different syntax: SELECT TOP 5 meter_id, daily_kw FROM meter_readings. The ANSI SQL standard uses FETCH FIRST 5 ROWS ONLY — which PostgreSQL also supports but which is less commonly seen in practice. When moving queries between databases, LIMIT is one of the clauses most likely to need adjustment. This course uses LIMIT because it is the most widely supported syntax across the platforms data engineers use.

blocks.title: LIMIT is your exploration tool
blocks.content: Every time you encounter a new table, the first thing to run is SELECT * FROM schema.table LIMIT 10. This gives you a quick look at the column names, the data types (from the values), the data quality (are there NULLs? unexpected values?), and the general shape of the data — all without waiting for millions of rows to load. At HydroAlpes, Markus starts every investigation with a LIMIT query.

A SQL query is not just an instruction to the database — it is a communication to the next engineer who reads it. That engineer might be a colleague reviewing your pipeline, a future team member maintaining code you wrote months ago, or your future self debugging a production issue at 2 AM. Comments and formatting make the difference between a query that is immediately understandable and one that requires 20 minutes of reverse-engineering. This lesson covers the two comment syntaxes, the formatting conventions that HydroAlpes follows, and the principles that make SQL readable.
Single-Line Comments

A single-line comment starts with two dashes (--) and extends to the end of the line. Everything after -- on that line is ignored by the database and has no effect on query execution. Single-line comments are the most common comment type in SQL because most annotations are brief — a short note explaining why a specific column is selected or why a particular filter condition exists.

-- Daily consumption report for the billing team
-- Author: Lena, 2025-01-20
-- Source: Silver meter readings, filtered to January 2025

SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw,
       date,
       customer_type   -- billing rates differ by customer type
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  date <  '2025-02-01'  -- January only, using half-open interval
ORDER BY date, meter_id;

The header comments document the query's purpose, author, and data source. The inline comments explain why certain columns are selected and why the date filter uses a half-open interval (>= start, < end of next month) rather than BETWEEN. These comments answer the 'why' questions that the SQL syntax alone cannot: why this column, why this date format, why this filter pattern.
Multi-Line Comments

Multi-line comments start with /* and end with */. Everything between these markers is ignored by the database engine, even when the comment spans across multiple lines. Multi-line comments are useful for longer explanations that span several sentences, for temporarily disabling a section of a query during debugging, and for header blocks that describe the purpose, author, and business context of complex pipeline queries.

/*
  HydroAlpes Regional Consumption Summary
  Produces monthly totals per region for the Gold layer.
  
  Business rule: only include validated readings (is_valid = TRUE).
  RESIDENTIAL and COMMERCIAL are billed at different rates,
  so customer_type is preserved for downstream rate application.
*/
SELECT region,
       customer_type,
       SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY region, customer_type
ORDER BY region, customer_type;
Formatting Conventions

Readable SQL follows a consistent visual structure. HydroAlpes' style guide specifies five rules. First, major clauses (SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY) start on a new line, left-aligned. Second, column lists are indented under SELECT, one column per line, with commas at the beginning or end of each line (HydroAlpes uses trailing commas). Third, conditions in WHERE are indented and aligned using AND at the start of each condition line. Fourth, keywords are uppercase (SELECT, FROM, WHERE) and column names are lowercase (meter_id, daily_kw). Fifth, table aliases are short, lowercase, and consistent (mr for meter_readings, m for meters).

left.label: Hard to Read
points: SELECT meter_id,daily_kw,date,region FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE customer_type='RESIDENTIAL' AND date>='2025-01-01' AND date<'2025-02-01' ORDER BY date;

right.label: Easy to Read
points:
SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw,
       date,
       region
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
  AND  date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  date < '2025-02-01'
ORDER BY date;

blocks.content: Both versions produce identical results. But the formatted version can be understood in seconds — you can see the selected columns, the source table, the three filter conditions, and the sort order without reading a single character carefully. The unformatted version requires you to parse the entire string mentally to find where WHERE starts and what conditions apply. For a query this simple, the difference is manageable. For a 50-line query with joins, subqueries, and window functions, formatting is the difference between comprehension and confusion.

blocks.title: Comments explain why, not what
blocks.content: Good SQL comments explain the business logic, the design decision, or the non-obvious reason behind a clause. They do not repeat what the SQL already says. The comment '-- filter to RESIDENTIAL' on WHERE customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL' adds nothing. The comment '-- billing rates differ by customer type' explains why the filter matters. Comment the why, not the what.

So far, every query has referenced a single table with straightforward column names. But as queries grow more complex — especially when joining multiple tables in Module 6 — you need precise ways to reference which table a column belongs to and concise ways to avoid repeating long table names. This lesson covers three reference patterns: fully qualified names, table aliases, and the situations where qualification is required versus optional. Mastering these patterns now will make multi-table queries significantly easier later.
Fully Qualified Names

A fully qualified column reference includes the schema name, the table name, and the column name, separated by dots: silver.meter_readings.daily_kw. This is the most explicit way to reference a column — there is no ambiguity about which table or schema the column belongs to. For single-table queries, fully qualified names are unnecessarily verbose. For multi-table queries — especially when two tables have a column with the same name — they prevent the database from rejecting your query with an ambiguous column error.

-- Fully qualified: schema.table.column
-- Explicit but verbose for single-table queries
SELECT silver.meter_readings.meter_id,
       silver.meter_readings.daily_kw,
       silver.meter_readings.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  3;
Table Aliases

A table alias is a short name you assign to a table in the FROM clause, which you can then use throughout the query instead of the full table name. The alias is declared after the table name (with or without AS): FROM silver.meter_readings AS mr. After this declaration, mr refers to silver.meter_readings everywhere in the query. Aliases make queries dramatically more readable, especially when joining multiple tables.

-- Table alias: FROM silver.meter_readings AS mr
-- Now 'mr' refers to meter_readings throughout the query
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
LIMIT  3;

The alias mr replaces the full table name in every column reference throughout the query: mr.meter_id instead of silver.meter_readings.meter_id. The query becomes shorter and cleaner while remaining completely unambiguous. Aliases are especially valuable in joins, where you might reference meter_readings.meter_id and meters.meter_id in the same query — short aliases like mr and m make the distinction compact and immediately readable.
HydroAlpes' Alias Conventions

rows: Three letters to avoid confusion with r (regions)

blocks.content: Consistent aliases matter because they reduce cognitive load. When Markus sees mr.daily_kw in a query, he immediately knows it refers to meter_readings without checking the FROM clause. When every engineer on the team uses the same aliases, queries become readable across the entire codebase. Inconsistent aliases — using mr in one query, readings in another, and x in a third — force readers to check the FROM clause every time they encounter a qualified column.
When Qualification Is Required

For single-table queries, column qualification (mr.meter_id) is optional — since there is only one table, the database knows where every column comes from. For multi-table queries (joins), qualification is required whenever two tables share a column name. If meter_readings and meters both have a column called region, writing just region in the SELECT clause is ambiguous — the database does not know which table's region you mean and returns an error. You must write mr.region or m.region to resolve the ambiguity. You will encounter this directly in Module 6.

blocks.title: Always qualify in production queries
blocks.content: HydroAlpes' style guide requires table qualification (alias.column) in all pipeline queries, even for single-table queries. The reason: a single-table query might become a join later when requirements change. If the original query uses unqualified column names, adding a join could introduce ambiguity errors that require rewriting every column reference. Qualifying from the start prevents this maintenance burden.

This module introduced the SELECT statement — the foundation of every SQL query you will write. You learned to choose specific columns, avoid SELECT *, rename columns with aliases, compute new values with expressions, eliminate duplicates with DISTINCT, restrict output with LIMIT, write readable comments, and use table aliases for clear references. This lesson brings those pieces together into a complete, realistic query against HydroAlpes' data and traces each element back to the lesson where it was introduced.
A Complete Query

-- HydroAlpes: Daily consumption summary for January 2025
-- Used by the billing team for monthly invoice generation
-- Author: Lena, reviewed by Markus

SELECT mr.meter_id,                              -- lesson 2: specific columns
       mr.daily_kw        AS consumption_kwh,      -- lesson 4: alias
       mr.daily_kw * 0.001 AS consumption_mwh,     -- lesson 5: expression
       mr.date            AS reading_date,          -- lesson 4: alias
       mr.region,                                   -- lesson 2: specific column
       mr.customer_type   AS segment                -- lesson 4: alias
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr                  -- lesson 9: table alias
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'                      -- filtering (Module 4)
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'                      -- half-open interval
ORDER BY mr.date, mr.meter_id                       -- sorting (Module 4)
LIMIT  10;                                          -- lesson 7: limit output

Every element of this query traces back to a lesson in this module. The explicit column list (lesson 2) documents exactly what data the billing team needs. The aliases (lesson 4) rename columns to match the billing team's expectations. The expression daily_kw * 0.001 (lesson 5) computes megawatt-hours alongside kilowatt-hours. The table alias mr (lesson 9) makes column references compact. The comments (lesson 8) explain the query's purpose and authorship. LIMIT 10 (lesson 7) restricts the output for display. The WHERE clause and ORDER BY preview Module 4, where you will learn them formally.
What This Module Did Not Cover

This module taught SELECT in its purest form — choosing and computing columns from a single table. Several important capabilities were deliberately left for later modules. WHERE (filtering rows by condition) and ORDER BY (sorting results) are covered in Module 4. GROUP BY (aggregating rows into summaries) and HAVING (filtering groups) are covered in Module 5. JOIN (combining data from multiple tables) is covered in Module 6. These capabilities build on SELECT — every query in those modules starts with the SELECT foundation you built here.
The SELECT Checklist

rows: Am I selecting only the columns I need?
Do my result columns have clear, meaningful names?
Can I compute derived values directly in SELECT?
Do I need unique values, or am I hiding a data problem?
Am I restricting output for exploration, or do I need all rows?
Would another engineer understand this query without asking me?
Are my column references qualified and consistent?

blocks.content: Run this checklist mentally before finalizing any query. Not every query needs every element — a quick exploratory query does not need comments or aliases, and a debugging query might legitimately use SELECT * with LIMIT. But a pipeline query that will be saved to version control, deployed to production, and maintained by the team should pass every check. This discipline is what separates a query that works today from a query that works reliably for years.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 4, you will learn to control which rows appear in your result (WHERE) and in what order (ORDER BY). SELECT chooses the columns. WHERE chooses the rows. Together, they let you ask precise questions: 'Show me the meter_id and daily_kw for residential customers in BERN during January 2025, sorted by consumption descending.' Every word after 'Show me' maps to a SQL clause — and Module 4 teaches the ones this module left out.

modules.title: Filtering and Sorting Data
modules.description: Master the WHERE clause for filtering rows, learn comparison and logical operators, understand pattern matching and null handling, and sort results with ORDER BY.

blocks.content: In Module 3 you learned to choose which columns appear in a query's result using SELECT. But every query so far returned every row in the table — all 128 million meter readings, all 350,000 meters, all 1,800 turbines. In practice, you almost never want every row. You want the readings from January. The meters in the BERN region. The turbines with output above a threshold. The WHERE clause is how you tell the database which rows to keep and which to discard. It is the most frequently used clause after SELECT and FROM, and mastering it is essential for writing precise, efficient queries against HydroAlpes' data.
How WHERE Works

WHERE appears after FROM and contains a condition — a logical expression that evaluates to TRUE, FALSE, or NULL for each row. The database scans the rows (or uses an index to find them efficiently) and keeps only those where the condition evaluates to TRUE. Rows where the condition is FALSE are excluded. Rows where the condition is NULL are also excluded — remember from Module 2 (lesson 9) that NULL means unknown, and an unknown condition is not TRUE. This behavior is consistent and predictable: WHERE keeps TRUE rows and discards everything else.

-- Keep only readings from the BERN region
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

Every row in the result has region = 'BERN'. Rows from ZURICH, SALZBURG, and INNSBRUCK were evaluated by the WHERE condition, found to be FALSE (their region does not equal 'BERN'), and excluded. The table itself is unchanged — WHERE filters the result set, not the stored data. This is the same read-only property you learned about SELECT in Module 3 (lesson 1): queries never modify the underlying table.
Execution Order Reminder

Recall the logical execution order from Module 1 (lesson 7): FROM runs first (identify the table), WHERE runs second (filter rows), and SELECT runs fifth (compute the output columns). This means WHERE cannot reference column aliases defined in SELECT — the aliases do not exist yet when WHERE executes. If you wrote SELECT daily_kw AS consumption WHERE consumption > 100, the database would return an error because consumption is not defined at the WHERE stage. Use the original column name: WHERE daily_kw > 100. This is one of the most common mistakes for new SQL users, and understanding the execution order prevents it entirely.
WHERE with Different Data Types

The condition in WHERE must compare compatible types. Text values are enclosed in single quotes: WHERE region = 'BERN'. Numeric values are unquoted: WHERE daily_kw > 100. Date values use single quotes in the ISO format: WHERE date = '2025-01-15'. Boolean values can be written as TRUE or FALSE without quotes: WHERE is_active = TRUE (or simply WHERE is_active, since TRUE is the default comparison for booleans). Mixing types — such as comparing a text column to an unquoted number — produces either an error or unexpected results depending on the database, so always match the type of the literal to the type of the column.

blocks.title: Single quotes, not double quotes
blocks.content: SQL uses single quotes for text and date literals: 'BERN', '2025-01-15'. Double quotes are reserved for identifiers (column names, table names) in PostgreSQL and most SQL dialects. Writing WHERE region = "BERN" tells the database to look for a column called BERN, not a text value — and fails with a 'column not found' error. Always use single quotes for values.

The WHERE clause uses comparison operators to express conditions. These operators compare a column's value against a literal, another column, or an expression, and produce TRUE or FALSE for each row. SQL provides six comparison operators that work across all data types — numbers, text, dates, and timestamps. Understanding what each operator does and how it behaves with different types is the first step toward writing precise filters against HydroAlpes' data.
The Six Operators

rows: Exclude industrial meters from residential analysis
Find unusually low readings (possible meter fault)

blocks.content: Numeric Comparisons

-- Find meters with high daily consumption (above 500 kW)
-- These are typically industrial or large commercial customers
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.region, mr.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.daily_kw > 500
  AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5;

meter_id       | daily_kw  | region    | customer_type
---------------+-----------+-----------+--------------
AT-INNB-91002  |  2,847.30 | INNSBRUCK | INDUSTRIAL
CH-ZRCH-88431  |  2,103.50 | ZURICH    | INDUSTRIAL
AT-SLZB-77210  |  1,942.80 | SALZBURG  | INDUSTRIAL
CH-BERN-65004  |  1,871.20 | BERN      | INDUSTRIAL
AT-INNB-43318  |  1,654.90 | INNSBRUCK | INDUSTRIAL

The condition daily_kw > 500 evaluates each row's daily_kw value and keeps only those exceeding 500 kW. All five results are INDUSTRIAL customers, which makes sense — residential meters rarely exceed 50 kW in a day. This query combines WHERE with ORDER BY and LIMIT to find the top consumers on a specific date, a pattern you will use frequently for outlier detection and capacity planning at HydroAlpes.
Date Comparisons

-- Find readings from the first week of January 2025
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-07'
  AND  mr.region = 'ZURICH'
ORDER BY mr.date, mr.meter_id
LIMIT  5;

Date comparisons work naturally in SQL because the ISO date format (YYYY-MM-DD) sorts chronologically as text. The condition date >= '2025-01-01' AND date <= '2025-01-07' selects exactly the first seven days of January. The date literal must be in single quotes because it is a string that the database interprets as a date. PostgreSQL automatically converts the string to a DATE type for comparison against the date column. You will see a cleaner way to express date ranges — the BETWEEN operator — in lesson 5.
Text Comparisons

Text comparisons with = check for exact matches, including case. WHERE region = 'BERN' matches 'BERN' but not 'bern', 'Bern', or 'BERN ' (note the trailing space). This strictness is a feature: the database does exactly what you ask. At HydroAlpes, region codes are stored in uppercase by convention, so case is not an issue. But when working with external data where case is inconsistent, you may need functions like UPPER() or LOWER() to normalize before comparing — a topic covered in the built-in functions that appear in later courses. For now, remember that text comparison is exact and case-sensitive.

blocks.title: The equality operator is a single =
blocks.content: Python uses == for equality comparison and = for assignment. SQL uses = for both equality (in WHERE) and aliasing (in SELECT ... AS). There is no == in SQL. Writing WHERE region == 'BERN' produces a syntax error in PostgreSQL. This is the most common typo for Python developers learning SQL.

sections.title: Combining Conditions with AND and OR

blocks.content: Most real-world filters require more than one condition. HydroAlpes' billing team needs residential customers in BERN during January 2025 — that is three conditions combined. The AND and OR operators let you combine multiple conditions into a single WHERE clause. AND requires both conditions to be true for a row to pass. OR requires at least one condition to be true. These operators are simple individually, but combining them without care leads to one of the most common SQL mistakes: ambiguous logic caused by operator precedence.
AND — Both Must Be True

-- Residential customers in BERN during January 2025
-- All three conditions must be true for a row to appear
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.region = 'BERN'
  AND  mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
LIMIT  5;

Every row in the result satisfies all four conditions: the region is BERN, the customer type is RESIDENTIAL, the date is on or after January 1, and the date is before February 1. If any single condition is false for a row, that row is excluded. AND is the more restrictive operator — adding another AND condition can only reduce the number of rows returned, never increase it. Notice the date filter uses a half-open interval (>= start, < start of next month) rather than <= '2025-01-31'. This pattern is safer because it works for any month regardless of how many days it has.
OR — At Least One Must Be True

-- Meters in either BERN or ZURICH (the Swiss regions)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.region = 'BERN'
   OR  mr.region = 'ZURICH'
LIMIT  5;

meter_id       | daily_kw | region
---------------+----------+-------
CH-BERN-00142  |    18.34 | BERN
CH-BERN-00143  |     7.91 | BERN
CH-ZRCH-45002  |    22.17 | ZURICH
CH-ZRCH-00001  |    14.82 | ZURICH
CH-BERN-00001  |    12.40 | BERN

A row passes if either condition is true — region is BERN or region is ZURICH. Rows from SALZBURG and INNSBRUCK are excluded because neither condition is true for them. OR is the more permissive operator — adding another OR condition can only increase the number of rows returned. You will see a cleaner way to express 'region is one of these values' — the IN operator — in lesson 5.
The Precedence Trap

AND has higher precedence than OR, which means the database evaluates AND first when both appear in the same WHERE clause without parentheses. This creates a trap. Consider the condition: WHERE region = 'BERN' OR region = 'ZURICH' AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'. You might read this as 'BERN or ZURICH, but only industrial.' The database reads it as 'BERN (any type), or ZURICH industrial' — because AND binds tighter than OR. The result includes all BERN customers (residential, commercial, industrial) plus only the industrial customers from ZURICH. This is almost certainly not what you intended.

-- WRONG: AND has higher precedence than OR
-- This returns ALL Bern customers + only Zurich industrial
WHERE region = 'BERN' OR region = 'ZURICH' AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'

-- The database interprets this as:
WHERE region = 'BERN' OR (region = 'ZURICH' AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL')

-- CORRECT: Use parentheses to group the OR first
WHERE (region = 'BERN' OR region = 'ZURICH') AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'

The fix is always parentheses. When you combine AND and OR, use parentheses to make the grouping explicit, even when the default precedence would produce the correct result. Parentheses cost nothing, prevent ambiguity, and make the query's logic readable to the next engineer. HydroAlpes' SQL style guide requires parentheses whenever AND and OR appear in the same WHERE clause — no exceptions.

blocks.title: Always use parentheses with mixed AND/OR
blocks.content: The precedence trap is one of the most common and hardest-to-debug SQL mistakes. The query runs without errors, returns results, and looks plausible — but the filter logic is wrong. A billing query that silently includes wrong customers can go unnoticed for weeks. Parentheses make the intent explicit and prevent this entire category of bugs.

Sometimes the easiest way to describe the rows you want is to describe the rows you do not want. HydroAlpes' data quality team needs all readings except those from a known batch of faulty meters. The capacity planning team needs all substations except those currently offline for maintenance. SQL provides the NOT operator to negate any condition: NOT reverses TRUE to FALSE and FALSE to TRUE. It appears before the condition it negates and can be combined with other operators to express exclusion patterns cleanly.
NOT with Comparison Operators

-- All customer types except INDUSTRIAL
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  NOT mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'
  AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

-- Equivalent and more readable:
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.customer_type <> 'INDUSTRIAL'
  AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

Both queries produce the same result — readings where customer_type is not INDUSTRIAL. The second form using <> is more common and more readable for simple negations. NOT is more useful when negating compound conditions or operators that have no direct negation equivalent. For example, NOT (region = 'BERN' AND customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL') excludes the specific combination of BERN residential customers while including BERN commercial, BERN industrial, and all customers from other regions.
NOT IN, NOT BETWEEN, NOT LIKE

NOT combines naturally with the operators you will learn in lesson 5 (BETWEEN and IN) and lesson 6 (LIKE). NOT IN excludes rows matching any value in a list: WHERE region NOT IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH') keeps only SALZBURG and INNSBRUCK rows. NOT BETWEEN excludes a range: WHERE date NOT BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31' keeps readings outside January. NOT LIKE excludes pattern matches: WHERE meter_id NOT LIKE 'CH-%' keeps only Austrian meters (those not starting with 'CH-'). Each of these operators is covered in detail in its own lesson — this lesson establishes that NOT is the universal negation that works with all of them.
NOT and NULL

NOT follows three-valued logic: NOT TRUE is FALSE, NOT FALSE is TRUE, and NOT NULL is NULL. This means NOT cannot turn a NULL condition into TRUE. If a row has substation_id = NULL and you write WHERE NOT substation_id = 'SUB-01', the expression evaluates as NOT NULL, which is NULL — and the row is excluded. To include rows where substation_id is either not 'SUB-01' or is NULL, you must write: WHERE substation_id <> 'SUB-01' OR substation_id IS NULL. This interaction between NOT and NULL is subtle but important — it is the same three-valued logic principle from Module 2 (lesson 9).
Readability Considerations

Negation makes conditions harder to read. WHERE NOT (region = 'BERN' OR customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL') requires the reader to mentally invert two conditions and understand De Morgan's law (NOT(A OR B) = NOT A AND NOT B). The equivalent positive form — WHERE region <> 'BERN' AND customer_type <> 'INDUSTRIAL' — is easier to parse because each condition reads directly. Use NOT when it makes the intent clearer (NOT IN is very readable), but prefer positive conditions when the negation adds unnecessary mental overhead.

blocks.title: When NOT is clearest
blocks.content: NOT is clearest when paired with set operators: NOT IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH') reads naturally as 'not in the set of BERN and ZURICH.' NOT BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-03-31' reads as 'outside the first quarter.' NOT LIKE 'CH-%' reads as 'not a Swiss meter.' In each case, the NOT version is more readable than the equivalent positive condition.

The comparison operators from lesson 2 are powerful but verbose for common patterns. Filtering to a date range requires two conditions: date >= '2025-01-01' AND date <= '2025-03-31'. Filtering to a set of regions requires multiple OR conditions: region = 'BERN' OR region = 'ZURICH' OR region = 'INNSBRUCK'. SQL provides two operators that express these patterns more concisely: BETWEEN for ranges and IN for sets. Both are syntactic shortcuts — the database converts them to the equivalent comparison or OR expressions internally — but they make queries significantly more readable.
BETWEEN — Range Checks

-- Readings from Q1 2025 (January through March)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-03-31'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
LIMIT  5;

BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends — the condition date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-03-31' includes both January 1 and March 31. This is equivalent to date >= '2025-01-01' AND date <= '2025-03-31'. The inclusive behavior is important to remember: BETWEEN includes the boundary values. For monthly filters where you do not want to remember the last day of the month, the half-open pattern (date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01') from lesson 3 is often safer than BETWEEN. For quarterly or yearly ranges where both boundaries are known, BETWEEN is cleaner.

blocks.title: BETWEEN with timestamps is tricky
blocks.content: If the column is a TIMESTAMP (not a DATE), BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31' includes January 1 at 00:00:00 but excludes January 31 at any time after midnight — because '2025-01-31' is interpreted as '2025-01-31 00:00:00'. To include the entire day of January 31, use BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31 23:59:59' or the half-open pattern with < '2025-02-01'. HydroAlpes' meter_readings uses DATE (not TIMESTAMP), so this issue does not arise there — but it matters for turbine_telemetry, which uses TIMESTAMP.
IN — Membership Checks

-- Readings from the Swiss regions (BERN and ZURICH)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.region IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH')
  AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

-- Equivalent but more verbose:
-- WHERE (mr.region = 'BERN' OR mr.region = 'ZURICH')

meter_id       | daily_kw | region
---------------+----------+-------
CH-BERN-00142  |    18.34 | BERN
CH-BERN-00143  |     7.91 | BERN
CH-ZRCH-45002  |    22.17 | ZURICH
CH-ZRCH-00001  |    14.82 | ZURICH
CH-BERN-00001  |    12.40 | BERN

IN checks whether a column's value matches any value in the parenthesized list. It is equivalent to chaining multiple OR conditions but far more readable, especially for longer lists. WHERE customer_type IN ('RESIDENTIAL', 'COMMERCIAL', 'INDUSTRIAL') is cleaner than three OR conditions. The list can contain any number of values — IN ('BERN') is valid (equivalent to = 'BERN'), and IN with hundreds of values is common when filtering by a list generated by another system. You will see IN with subqueries in Module 7, where the list is produced by a nested query rather than hardcoded.
NOT BETWEEN and NOT IN

Both operators work with NOT for exclusion patterns. NOT BETWEEN excludes a range: WHERE daily_kw NOT BETWEEN 0 AND 100 finds readings outside the 0–100 kW range — either negative (sensor errors) or very high (industrial customers). NOT IN excludes a set: WHERE region NOT IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH') keeps only Austrian readings from SALZBURG and INNSBRUCK. These negated forms read naturally and are often more readable than the equivalent positive conditions, especially when the excluded set is smaller than the included set.

blocks.title: BETWEEN and IN as readability tools
blocks.content: BETWEEN and IN do not add new capabilities — everything they do can be written with comparison operators and OR. Their value is readability. A query with WHERE date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-12-31' AND region IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH', 'SALZBURG') communicates its intent in one scan. The equivalent with six comparison operators and two OR chains requires careful reading to understand the same thing.

Exact equality (=) and set membership (IN) work when you know the complete value you are looking for. But sometimes you need to match a pattern: all meters whose ID starts with 'CH-' (Swiss meters), all substations whose name contains 'Alpine', or all turbine types that end with '-H' (horizontal axis). SQL provides the LIKE operator for pattern matching on text columns. LIKE uses two wildcard characters — % for any sequence of characters and _ for exactly one character — to express patterns that the equality operator cannot.
The % Wildcard

The percent sign (%) matches any sequence of zero or more characters. It is the most commonly used wildcard. The pattern 'CH-%' matches any text that starts with 'CH-' followed by anything: 'CH-BERN-00142', 'CH-ZRCH-45002', 'CH-'. The pattern '%BERN%' matches any text containing 'BERN' anywhere: 'CH-BERN-00142', 'SUB-BERN-04', 'BERN'. The pattern '%00142' matches any text ending with '00142'. You can combine multiple % wildcards in a single pattern to match complex structures.

-- Find all Swiss meters (meter_id starts with 'CH-')
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id LIKE 'CH-%'
  AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

-- Find all Austrian meters (meter_id starts with 'AT-')
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id LIKE 'AT-%'
  AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;

-- Swiss meters:
meter_id       | daily_kw | region
---------------+----------+-------
CH-BERN-00142  |    18.34 | BERN
CH-BERN-00143  |     7.91 | BERN
CH-ZRCH-45002  |    22.17 | ZURICH
CH-BERN-00001  |    12.40 | BERN
CH-ZRCH-00001  |    14.82 | ZURICH

-- Austrian meters:
meter_id       | daily_kw | region
---------------+----------+-----------
AT-SLZB-08821  |   142.60 | SALZBURG
AT-INNB-33410  |   487.30 | INNSBRUCK
AT-SLZB-00001  |    19.44 | SALZBURG
AT-INNB-00001  |    21.07 | INNSBRUCK
AT-SLZB-00002  |    13.85 | SALZBURG

HydroAlpes' meter IDs follow a structured format: country code (CH or AT), region abbreviation (BERN, ZRCH, SLZB, INNB), and a numeric sequence. LIKE with the % wildcard lets you filter by any part of this structure. This is particularly useful when the structured information is embedded in the ID rather than stored in a separate column — a common pattern in real-world data where identifiers carry implicit metadata.
The _ Wildcard

The underscore (_) matches exactly one character. The pattern 'CH-BERN-0014_' matches 'CH-BERN-00142', 'CH-BERN-00143', and any other meter where the last character varies. The pattern '___-BERN%' matches any three-character prefix followed by '-BERN' and anything after. The _ wildcard is less common than % but useful for fixed-length patterns where you need to match specific positions while allowing variation in others.
LIKE Is Case-Sensitive

In PostgreSQL, LIKE is case-sensitive: WHERE region LIKE 'bern%' does not match 'BERN'. If you need case-insensitive matching, PostgreSQL provides the ILIKE operator: WHERE region ILIKE 'bern%' matches 'BERN', 'Bern', and 'bern'. ILIKE is a PostgreSQL extension — it is not part of the ANSI SQL standard. Other databases use different approaches: SQL Server's LIKE is case-insensitive by default, and Spark SQL provides the rlike function for regex matching. At HydroAlpes, data is stored in consistent uppercase, so case sensitivity is rarely an issue — but when working with external data, ILIKE or UPPER() normalization is essential.

blocks.title: LIKE vs full-text search
blocks.content: LIKE with a leading wildcard — WHERE name LIKE '%Alpine%' — forces the database to scan every row because an index cannot help when the pattern starts with an unknown sequence. For occasional queries this is fine.
