Gap Detection with LAG

-- Detect gaps: find days where a meter missed a reading
-- A gap occurs when the previous reading date is more than 1 day ago
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.date,
       LAG(mr.date) OVER (
         PARTITION BY mr.meter_id
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS prev_date,
       mr.date - LAG(mr.date) OVER (
         PARTITION BY mr.meter_id
         ORDER BY mr.date
       ) AS days_since_prev
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <= '2025-01-31'
ORDER BY mr.date;

LAG(date) returns the previous reading's date for each meter. Subtracting the two dates gives the number of days between consecutive readings. If days_since_prev is 1, the meter reported on consecutive days (no gap). If it is 2 or more, the meter missed one or more days. Filtering to WHERE days_since_prev > 1 gives you every gap for every meter. This replaces the CROSS JOIN scaffold approach from Module 6 (lesson 5) with a simpler, more efficient window function pattern.
Percentage of Partition Total

-- Each meter's share of its region's total consumption
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY mr.region) AS region_total,
       ROUND(
         mr.daily_kw / SUM(mr.daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY mr.region) * 100,
         4
       ) AS pct_of_region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  5;

The percentage is computed by dividing each meter's daily_kw by the partition total — SUM(daily_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY region). The partition total is the same for every BERN row (145,908.20), but each row's percentage is unique because daily_kw varies. The top-consuming industrial meter (CH-BERN-65004) accounts for 1.28% of BERN's total daily consumption — a remarkable concentration of energy use in a single facility. This pattern is the foundation of contribution analysis in any domain.

blocks.title: Window functions replace complexity
blocks.content: Every pattern in this lesson has a non-window alternative: deduplication via self-joins, gap detection via scaffold cross-joins, percentages via subqueries with GROUP BY. The window function versions are consistently shorter, more readable, and often faster. Once you are comfortable with OVER, PARTITION BY, and ORDER BY, most analytical problems become one-expression solutions.

This module introduced window functions as aggregations that preserve row-level detail. You learned the OVER clause, PARTITION BY for defining windows, ORDER BY for running computations, ROW_NUMBER/RANK/DENSE_RANK for ranking, LAG/LEAD for row comparisons, frame specifications for moving averages, and FIRST_VALUE/LAST_VALUE for boundary values. This final lesson compares window functions directly with GROUP BY across six dimensions and provides a decision framework for choosing between them — because the two tools solve overlapping but distinct problems.
The Six Dimensions

rows: Collapses rows: one output row per group
Preserves rows: every input row appears in output
Computes across each group, result replaces the group
Computes across each partition, result is added as a column
Cannot compute running totals without subqueries
Running totals and moving averages are native with ORDER BY
ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK are window-only functions
LAG and LEAD access previous/next rows
Summary reports: totals, averages, counts per category
Analytical reports: row-level detail with contextual aggregates

blocks.content: When to Use GROUP BY

Use GROUP BY when the business question asks for a summary — a single number per category. 'Total consumption per region' is a GROUP BY question (4 result rows). 'Number of active meters per customer type' is a GROUP BY question (3 result rows). 'Average daily reading per month' is a GROUP BY question (12 result rows). The defining characteristic: the individual rows do not matter, only the aggregated result. GROUP BY is simpler, uses less memory (fewer output rows), and produces results that are immediately consumable by dashboards and reports.
When to Use Window Functions

Use window functions when the business question requires both individual rows and aggregate context. 'Each meter's consumption alongside its region's average' is a window function question. 'The top 3 consuming meters per region' is a window function question (requires ROW_NUMBER). 'Day-over-day consumption change per meter' is a window function question (requires LAG). 'Each meter's percentage of regional total' is a window function question. The defining characteristic: the individual rows matter, and the aggregate provides context for each row.
The Complete Analytical Toolkit

With Modules 5 through 8 complete, you now have the full analytical SQL toolkit. GROUP BY (Module 5) produces summaries. Joins (Module 6) combine tables. Subqueries and CTEs (Module 7) compose complex logic. Window functions (this module) add per-row analytics without collapsing detail. Together, these tools handle any analytical question HydroAlpes encounters. The remaining two modules — creating tables (Module 9) and SQL across the stack (Module 10) — expand your toolkit beyond querying into data infrastructure and platform awareness.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 9, you will learn the other side of SQL: creating and modifying the tables that you have been querying throughout this course. CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, ALTER TABLE, and views are the DDL and DML statements that data engineers use to build the pipeline infrastructure — the Silver and Gold tables, the staging areas, and the analytical views that make HydroAlpes' data platform work.

modules.title: Creating and Modifying Tables
modules.description: Learn the DDL and DML statements that data engineers use to create pipeline infrastructure: CREATE TABLE, data types, constraints, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and ALTER TABLE.

blocks.content: Every module so far has queried tables that already exist — the meter_readings, meters, substations, and regions tables in HydroAlpes' Silver schema. But those tables did not appear by magic. Someone — Lena, when she designed the pipeline — created them using the CREATE TABLE statement. CREATE TABLE is the SQL statement that defines a new table's structure: its name, its columns, their data types, and the constraints that govern what data is allowed. For a data engineer, CREATE TABLE is the tool that builds the pipeline infrastructure — the Silver and Gold tables, the staging areas, and the intermediate tables that transformation pipelines write to.
DDL vs DML

SQL statements fall into two categories. Data Definition Language (DDL) statements define and modify the structure of database objects — tables, schemas, views, indexes. CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE, and CREATE VIEW are DDL. Data Manipulation Language (DML) statements read and modify the data inside those structures — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE are DML. Modules 3 through 8 were entirely DML — querying and analyzing existing data. This module introduces DDL alongside the DML statements that populate and maintain tables. The distinction matters operationally: DDL changes structure (and can break downstream queries), while DML changes data (and can corrupt analytical results).
The Basic Syntax

-- Create a simple staging table for incoming meter readings
CREATE TABLE staging.meter_readings_raw (
    meter_id       VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    daily_kw       DOUBLE PRECISION,
    reading_date   DATE               NOT NULL,
    region_code    VARCHAR(30),
    customer_type  VARCHAR(20),
    loaded_at      TIMESTAMP          DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

The statement creates a table called meter_readings_raw in the staging schema. Each line inside the parentheses defines one column: a name, a data type, and optional constraints. The meter_id column is VARCHAR(20) and NOT NULL — it must always have a value. The daily_kw column is DOUBLE PRECISION with no NOT NULL constraint — it allows NULL for meters that fail to report. The loaded_at column has a DEFAULT value: if no value is provided during insertion, the database automatically records the current timestamp. The table is empty after creation — it has structure but no data.
Schema Placement

The schema prefix in staging.meter_readings_raw determines where the table lives. At HydroAlpes, tables are created in schemas that reflect their role: staging for temporary pipeline workspace, silver for validated data, and gold for analytical outputs (Module 2, lesson 7). Creating a table without a schema prefix places it in the default schema (public in PostgreSQL), which is ambiguous and should be avoided in production. Lena's pipeline always specifies the schema explicitly — both in CREATE TABLE and in every query that references the table.
IF NOT EXISTS

-- CREATE TABLE fails if the table already exists
-- IF NOT EXISTS prevents the error — the table is left unchanged
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS staging.meter_readings_raw (
    meter_id       VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    daily_kw       DOUBLE PRECISION,
    reading_date   DATE               NOT NULL,
    region_code    VARCHAR(30),
    customer_type  VARCHAR(20),
    loaded_at      TIMESTAMP          DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

Without IF NOT EXISTS, running CREATE TABLE twice produces an error: 'relation already exists.' With IF NOT EXISTS, the second execution silently succeeds without modifying the existing table. This is essential for pipeline scripts that run repeatedly — the CREATE TABLE statement should be idempotent, meaning it produces the same result whether it runs once or a hundred times. HydroAlpes' pipeline scripts always use IF NOT EXISTS for every CREATE TABLE to ensure the pipeline can be safely rerun after a failure.

blocks.title: CREATE TABLE does not validate against existing tables
blocks.content: IF NOT EXISTS checks only whether the table name already exists. It does not verify that the existing table has the same columns, types, or constraints as the CREATE TABLE statement specifies. If the existing table has different columns, the statement silently succeeds and the mismatch goes undetected. At HydroAlpes, Lena uses a separate schema validation check after CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS to verify the table's structure matches expectations.

Module 2 (lesson 2) introduced data types from the reader's perspective — recognizing that meter_id is text and daily_kw is a number. This lesson revisits data types from the creator's perspective: when you design a table, which type should each column be? The choice affects storage size, query performance, mathematical precision, and what operations are valid. A wrong type choice can cause subtle bugs that surface months later — rounding errors in billing calculations, truncated identifiers, or date comparisons that silently fail. Getting types right at creation time saves enormous debugging effort later.
The Decision Framework

rows: Documents max length, prevents data quality issues
meter_id as VARCHAR(20), region as VARCHAR(30)
Supports date arithmetic, no time component

blocks.content: NUMERIC vs DOUBLE PRECISION

The most important type decision for data engineers is when to use NUMERIC versus DOUBLE PRECISION. NUMERIC stores exact decimal values — NUMERIC(10,4) stores exactly 123456.7890 with no rounding. DOUBLE PRECISION stores approximate values — the number 0.1 cannot be represented exactly in binary floating point, so 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1 might equal 0.30000000000000004 instead of 0.3. For energy readings where a rounding error of 0.0000001 kW is negligible, DOUBLE PRECISION is fine and performs better. For financial calculations where EUR 0.01 must be exact, NUMERIC is mandatory. HydroAlpes uses DOUBLE PRECISION for energy measurements and NUMERIC for pricing and billing amounts.
VARCHAR vs TEXT

In PostgreSQL, VARCHAR(n) and TEXT have identical storage and performance characteristics — the only difference is that VARCHAR(n) enforces a maximum length. The length limit serves as documentation and as a guardrail: if meter_id is VARCHAR(20) and a data source sends a 50-character identifier, the database rejects the insert rather than silently accepting data that does not match the expected format. TEXT accepts any length without complaint. HydroAlpes uses VARCHAR(n) for all columns with a known maximum length (identifiers, codes, classifications) and TEXT only for genuinely unbounded content (notes, descriptions, error messages).
TIMESTAMP vs TIMESTAMPTZ

TIMESTAMP stores a date and time without timezone information. TIMESTAMPTZ (TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) stores the same but converts to UTC internally and converts back to the session's timezone on retrieval. For HydroAlpes, which operates across Swiss and Austrian time zones, TIMESTAMPTZ is the safer choice for any column that records when something happened (loaded_at, measured_at). TIMESTAMP without timezone is appropriate for columns that represent a date-time in a known, fixed context — like a scheduled maintenance window that is always in local time. When in doubt, use TIMESTAMPTZ — it prevents the category of bugs where two systems in different timezones interpret the same timestamp differently.

blocks.title: Types are the first line of data quality
blocks.content: A column's data type is the database's first validation check. If daily_kw is DOUBLE PRECISION, the database rejects any attempt to insert text into it. If meter_id is VARCHAR(20), the database rejects identifiers longer than 20 characters. If reading_date is DATE, the database rejects invalid dates like '2025-02-30.' These type-level rejections catch data quality issues at the moment of insertion — before they propagate through the pipeline and corrupt downstream tables.

Module 2 (lesson 6) introduced constraints as rules the database enforces. This lesson shows how to declare them in CREATE TABLE and demonstrates the error messages each constraint produces when violated. Understanding these errors is practical, not academic — when a pipeline insert fails because of a constraint violation, the error message tells you exactly which rule was broken and which row caused the failure. Constraints turn silent data corruption into loud, immediate errors that the pipeline can catch, log, and alert on.
PRIMARY KEY

-- Silver meter_readings table with a composite primary key
CREATE TABLE silver.meter_readings (
    meter_id       VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    daily_kw       DOUBLE PRECISION   NOT NULL,
    date           DATE               NOT NULL,
    region         VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL,
    customer_type  VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    substation_id  VARCHAR(20),
    PRIMARY KEY (meter_id, date)
);

-- Attempt to insert a duplicate: same meter, same date
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings VALUES
  ('CH-BERN-00142', 18.34, '2025-01-15', 'BERN', 'RESIDENTIAL', 'SUB-BERN-04'),
  ('CH-BERN-00142', 19.50, '2025-01-15', 'BERN', 'RESIDENTIAL', 'SUB-BERN-04');
-- ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint

The PRIMARY KEY (meter_id, date) constraint ensures that no two rows share the same meter_id and date combination. The second insert row has the same key as the first and is rejected with a clear error message that names the violated constraint. This automatic duplicate prevention is one of the primary key's most valuable features for data engineers — it catches duplicate ingestion at the database level rather than letting duplicates propagate into downstream aggregations where they cause double-counting.
FOREIGN KEY

-- Add a foreign key that references the meters table
CREATE TABLE silver.meter_readings (
    meter_id       VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    daily_kw       DOUBLE PRECISION   NOT NULL,
    date           DATE               NOT NULL,
    region         VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL,
    customer_type  VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    substation_id  VARCHAR(20),
    PRIMARY KEY (meter_id, date),
    FOREIGN KEY (meter_id) REFERENCES silver.meters (meter_id)
);

-- Attempt to insert a reading for a non-existent meter
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
VALUES ('XX-FAKE-99999', 10.0, '2025-01-15', 'BERN', 'RESIDENTIAL', NULL);
-- ERROR: insert or update violates foreign key constraint

The FOREIGN KEY constraint ensures that every meter_id in meter_readings exists in the meters table. Attempting to insert a reading for a meter that does not exist (XX-FAKE-99999) produces an immediate error. This referential integrity check prevents the orphan row problem from Module 6 (lesson 3) — readings that cannot be joined with meter metadata because the meter does not exist. The trade-off is that the meters table must be loaded before meter_readings, creating a dependency ordering that the pipeline must respect.
CHECK Constraints

-- CHECK constraints express business rules directly in the schema
CREATE TABLE silver.meter_readings (
    meter_id       VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    daily_kw       DOUBLE PRECISION   NOT NULL
                   CHECK (daily_kw >= 0),          -- no negative consumption
    date           DATE               NOT NULL
                   CHECK (date >= '2020-01-01'),   -- no dates before system launch
    region         VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL
                   CHECK (region IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH', 'INNSBRUCK', 'SALZBURG')),
    customer_type  VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL
                   CHECK (customer_type IN ('RESIDENTIAL', 'COMMERCIAL', 'INDUSTRIAL')),
    substation_id  VARCHAR(20),
    PRIMARY KEY (meter_id, date)
);

Each CHECK constraint expresses a business rule. No negative consumption values. No dates before the system launched in 2020. Region must be one of four valid codes. Customer type must be one of three valid classifications. If any of these rules are violated during an INSERT or UPDATE, the database rejects the statement with an error that identifies the failing constraint. These constraints codify the data quality rules that would otherwise live only in pipeline code or documentation — making them visible, enforceable, and impossible to bypass.

blocks.title: Constraints vs pipeline validation
blocks.content: Some data engineers put all validation logic in the pipeline code and use no constraints on the tables. Others put all validation in constraints and keep the pipeline simple. HydroAlpes uses both: the pipeline validates data before inserting (to produce clear error messages and route bad data to a quarantine table), and the tables have constraints as a safety net (to catch anything the pipeline missed). The constraint is the last line of defense — it should never trigger in normal operation, but when it does, it prevents corruption.

CREATE TABLE builds the structure. INSERT adds data. Every row in every table at HydroAlpes arrived via an INSERT statement — whether executed directly by a pipeline script, indirectly through a COPY command, or implicitly through a CREATE TABLE AS SELECT. INSERT is the DML statement that moves data into a table, and understanding its forms — single-row, multi-row, and INSERT from SELECT — is essential for building the pipeline stages that populate Silver and Gold tables.
Single-Row Insert

-- Insert one row with explicit column names
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
    (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id)
VALUES
    ('CH-BERN-00142', 18.34, '2025-01-15', 'BERN', 'RESIDENTIAL', 'SUB-BERN-04');

The column list after the table name specifies which columns receive values, and the VALUES clause provides the values in the same order. Including the column list is strongly recommended — it documents which columns are being populated and protects the statement from breaking when new columns are added to the table. Without the column list, INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings VALUES (...) depends on the column order matching the table definition, which is fragile and difficult to read.
Multi-Row Insert

-- Insert multiple rows in a single statement
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
    (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id)
VALUES
    ('CH-BERN-00142', 18.34, '2025-01-15', 'BERN', 'RESIDENTIAL', 'SUB-BERN-04'),
    ('CH-BERN-00143',  7.91, '2025-01-15', 'BERN', 'RESIDENTIAL', 'SUB-BERN-04'),
    ('AT-SLZB-08821', 142.60, '2025-01-15', 'SALZBURG', 'COMMERCIAL', 'SUB-SLZB-01');

Multiple value sets separated by commas insert several rows in a single statement. This is significantly faster than executing separate INSERT statements for each row because the database processes the batch in one transaction, reducing the per-statement overhead. In practice, multi-row INSERT with VALUES is used for small batches — dozens or hundreds of rows. For large-scale data loading (hundreds of thousands of rows), INSERT from SELECT or the COPY command is far more efficient.
INSERT from SELECT

-- Insert the result of a query into another table
-- This is how HydroAlpes populates Gold tables from Silver data
INSERT INTO gold.regional_consumption
    (region, customer_type, month, total_kw, meter_count)
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date) AS month,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)             AS total_kw,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id)  AS meter_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type, DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date);

INSERT from SELECT is the workhorse of data engineering pipelines. The SELECT query computes the Gold-layer aggregation — regional consumption totals grouped by region, customer type, and month — and INSERT pipes the result directly into the gold.regional_consumption table. No intermediate files, no manual steps. The SELECT can include joins, CTEs, window functions — anything you learned in Modules 3 through 8. This single statement replaces what would be dozens of lines of procedural code in Python: query, transform, insert.

blocks.title: INSERT from SELECT is the pipeline pattern
blocks.content: Most Silver-to-Gold transformations at HydroAlpes follow this pattern: INSERT INTO gold.table SELECT ... FROM silver.table WHERE ... GROUP BY .... The SELECT defines the transformation logic. The INSERT directs the output to the target table. Combined with a preceding DELETE or TRUNCATE to clear old data, this three-step pattern (clear, compute, insert) is the foundation of incremental pipeline design.

INSERT adds new rows. UPDATE changes values in rows that already exist. When HydroAlpes discovers that a batch of readings had a miscalibrated sensor — all daily_kw values are 10% too high for meters in substation SUB-BERN-04 on January 15 — the correction requires UPDATE, not INSERT. UPDATE modifies specified columns in rows that match a WHERE condition, leaving all other rows and columns untouched. It is the most dangerous DML statement because a missing or incorrect WHERE clause can modify every row in the table.
The Syntax

-- Correct miscalibrated readings: reduce daily_kw by 10%
-- for all meters at substation SUB-BERN-04 on January 15
UPDATE silver.meter_readings
SET    daily_kw = daily_kw * 0.9
WHERE  substation_id = 'SUB-BERN-04'
  AND  date = '2025-01-15';

The SET clause specifies which columns to change and their new values. The WHERE clause identifies which rows to modify. Only rows matching both conditions are updated — readings at SUB-BERN-04 on January 15. All other rows remain unchanged. The SET expression can reference the column's current value (daily_kw * 0.9 reduces each value by 10%), use literal values (SET region = 'BERN'), or use subqueries. The UPDATE returns a count of affected rows — 'UPDATE 12,340' tells you how many rows were modified, which you should verify against your expectation.
The Missing WHERE Disaster

-- DANGEROUS: no WHERE clause updates EVERY row in the table
UPDATE silver.meter_readings
SET    daily_kw = daily_kw * 0.9;
-- This modifies ALL 128 million rows — almost certainly not intended

Without a WHERE clause, UPDATE modifies every row in the table. This is rarely intentional and can corrupt an entire dataset in seconds. The database does not warn you — it executes the statement and reports 'UPDATE 128,000,000.' Recovering from this requires restoring from a backup or rerunning the entire pipeline from Bronze. At HydroAlpes, Lena's CI system flags any UPDATE statement without a WHERE clause as an error during code review. The rule is absolute: every UPDATE must have a WHERE clause, and the WHERE clause must be tested with a SELECT first.
Test with SELECT First

-- STEP 1: Verify which rows will be affected BEFORE updating
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, substation_id
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  substation_id = 'SUB-BERN-04'
  AND  date = '2025-01-15'
LIMIT  5;
-- Review the results. Confirm these are the rows you intend to update.

-- STEP 2: Only then run the UPDATE with the same WHERE clause
UPDATE silver.meter_readings
SET    daily_kw = daily_kw * 0.9
WHERE  substation_id = 'SUB-BERN-04'
  AND  date = '2025-01-15';

The test-with-SELECT pattern is a safety practice that every data engineer should follow. Write the WHERE clause, run it as a SELECT to see which rows match, verify the count and sample values, and only then convert the SELECT to an UPDATE with the same WHERE clause. This practice costs an extra minute and prevents catastrophic data corruption. Lena runs this pattern in a transaction (which you will learn about in Module 10) so the UPDATE can be rolled back if the result looks wrong.

blocks.title: UPDATE is permanent
blocks.content: Once committed, an UPDATE cannot be undone without restoring from a backup. Unlike SELECT (which is read-only), UPDATE modifies the actual stored data. Always test the WHERE clause with SELECT first, always wrap UPDATEs in transactions when possible, and always verify the affected row count against your expectation. If UPDATE reports modifying 128 million rows when you expected 12,000, something is wrong.

DELETE removes rows from a table. At HydroAlpes, DELETE serves two purposes: data retention (removing Bronze readings older than 90 days after they have been validated into Silver) and data correction (removing rows that should never have been inserted — test data, duplicate ingestion batches, or readings from decommissioned meters). Like UPDATE, DELETE requires a WHERE clause to target specific rows — and like UPDATE, a missing WHERE clause removes every row in the table. The same safety practices apply: test with SELECT first, verify the count, and wrap the operation in a transaction.
The Syntax

-- Remove Bronze readings older than 90 days (data retention policy)
DELETE FROM bronze.meter_readings
WHERE  loaded_at < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days';

-- Remove a specific batch of test data that was accidentally loaded
DELETE FROM silver.meter_readings
WHERE  meter_id LIKE 'TEST-%'
  AND  date = '2025-01-15';

DELETE FROM names the table and WHERE specifies which rows to remove. The first example removes old Bronze data using a date comparison against the loaded_at timestamp. The second removes accidentally inserted test rows identified by a meter_id prefix pattern. Both statements return a count of deleted rows. DELETE is permanent once committed — the rows cannot be recovered without a backup or a reinsert from another source. At HydroAlpes, Bronze data can be reingested from the source files, but Silver and Gold data must be recomputed from Bronze if incorrectly deleted.
TRUNCATE vs DELETE

When you need to remove all rows from a table (not a subset), TRUNCATE TABLE is faster than DELETE without a WHERE clause. DELETE processes rows one at a time, logging each deletion for potential rollback. TRUNCATE deallocates the table's storage pages in bulk, which is dramatically faster on large tables. TRUNCATE TABLE gold.regional_consumption removes all rows instantly, regardless of table size. The trade-off: TRUNCATE cannot be rolled back in some databases (PostgreSQL does support transactional TRUNCATE), and it resets auto-increment sequences. HydroAlpes uses TRUNCATE for the daily Gold table refresh: TRUNCATE the Gold table, then INSERT from the Silver query.
CASCADE and Foreign Keys

If a row you are deleting is referenced by a foreign key in another table, the DELETE fails with a foreign key violation error. If the meters table has a foreign key from meter_readings, you cannot delete a meter from meters while readings for that meter still exist in meter_readings. You must either delete the readings first (manual cascade), define the foreign key with ON DELETE CASCADE (automatic cascade), or use ON DELETE SET NULL (set the foreign key column to NULL instead of deleting the child rows). At HydroAlpes, foreign keys use ON DELETE RESTRICT (the default) — deletion of a referenced parent row is blocked, forcing the engineer to handle the dependency chain explicitly.

blocks.title: The same safety rules as UPDATE
blocks.content: DELETE without WHERE removes every row in the table. Test the WHERE clause with SELECT first. Verify the row count. Wrap in a transaction if possible. The test-with-SELECT pattern from lesson 5 applies identically to DELETE: write the WHERE, run it as SELECT COUNT(*) to verify the count, then convert to DELETE.

Tables evolve. HydroAlpes' SCADA system starts reporting a new turbine metric (vibration frequency) that the turbine_telemetry table does not have a column for. A regulatory change requires the meter_readings table to store the tariff code that was active when each reading was recorded. A column that was originally nullable needs to be made NOT NULL after a data cleanup. ALTER TABLE is the DDL statement that modifies an existing table's structure — adding columns, removing columns, changing data types, and modifying constraints — without dropping and recreating the table.
ADD COLUMN

ADD COLUMN adds a new column to every existing row. For rows that already exist, the new column is set to NULL (or to the DEFAULT value if one is specified). The tariff_code column has no default, so all existing rows get NULL — the pipeline will populate it going forward. The is_validated column defaults to FALSE, so all existing rows are marked as not yet validated. ADD COLUMN is an online operation in PostgreSQL — it does not lock the table or block concurrent reads and writes, making it safe to run in production.
DROP COLUMN, RENAME COLUMN, ALTER TYPE

-- Remove a column that is no longer needed
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings
  DROP COLUMN tariff_code;

-- Rename a column (PostgreSQL syntax)
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings
  RENAME COLUMN customer_type TO segment;

-- Change a column's data type
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings
  ALTER COLUMN region TYPE VARCHAR(50);  -- widen from 30 to 50 characters

Each ALTER TABLE operation modifies one aspect of the table's structure. DROP COLUMN removes a column and all its data — this is irreversible without a backup. RENAME COLUMN changes a column's name — every query that references the old name will break. ALTER COLUMN TYPE changes a column's data type — widening a VARCHAR from 30 to 50 is safe, but narrowing from 50 to 30 fails if any existing value exceeds 30 characters. Each of these operations has downstream consequences: queries, views, and pipelines that reference the affected column must be updated.
Schema Changes in Production

Changing a table's structure in production is risky because downstream consumers depend on the existing schema. Adding a column is the safest change — existing queries continue to work because they do not reference the new column. Renaming or dropping a column breaks every query that references it. Changing a column's type can break queries that depend on the type's behavior (casting rules, comparison semantics). At HydroAlpes, schema changes follow a protocol: add the new column first, update all downstream queries to reference the new column, verify everything works, and only then drop the old column. This gradual migration avoids breaking changes.

blocks.title: Additive changes are safe; breaking changes need coordination
blocks.content: Adding a nullable column is always safe — no existing query breaks. Everything else — dropping, renaming, narrowing, adding NOT NULL to an existing column — is a breaking change that requires coordinating with every downstream consumer. At HydroAlpes, Lena's rule is: 'Add freely, change carefully, drop only after confirming nothing references it.' This principle applies to every data platform, not just HydroAlpes.

CREATE TABLE defines structure and INSERT populates data — two separate statements. CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (commonly called CTAS) combines both into one: it creates a new table and populates it with the result of a query in a single statement. The table's columns, types, and data all come from the SELECT query. CTAS is one of the most frequently used patterns in data engineering because it turns any analytical query into a materialized table — a physical dataset that dashboards, reports, and downstream pipelines can query directly without recomputing the transformation every time.
The Syntax

-- Create a Gold-layer table from a Silver-layer query in one statement
CREATE TABLE gold.january_billing AS
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)              AS total_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) * 0.001      AS total_mwh,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id)   AS active_meters,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw)             AS avg_daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type
ORDER BY mr.region, mr.customer_type;

-- The resulting table has 12 rows (4 regions × 3 customer types)
-- with columns: region, customer_type, total_kw, total_mwh, active_meters, avg_daily_kw
-- Column types are inferred from the SELECT expressions

One statement creates the gold.january_billing table and fills it with 12 rows of aggregated data. The column names come from the SELECT aliases (total_kw, total_mwh, active_meters, avg_daily_kw). The column types are inferred from the expressions — SUM produces DOUBLE PRECISION, COUNT produces BIGINT, AVG produces DOUBLE PRECISION. No explicit type declarations are needed. The resulting table is a regular table that can be queried, indexed, and referenced by other queries.
CTAS Limitations

CTAS does not inherit constraints from the source tables. The gold.january_billing table has no primary key, no foreign keys, and no CHECK constraints — even though the source silver.meter_readings table has all of these. If you need constraints on the CTAS output, you must add them separately with ALTER TABLE after creation. CTAS also fails if the target table already exists — unlike IF NOT EXISTS for regular CREATE TABLE, there is no standard CTAS IF NOT EXISTS in PostgreSQL. The common pattern is DROP TABLE IF EXISTS followed by CTAS, or using a temporary table that is renamed after creation.
The DROP-and-Recreate Pattern

-- The daily Gold refresh pattern: drop old table, create new one
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS gold.january_billing;

CREATE TABLE gold.january_billing AS
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) AS active_meters
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS removes the old table (or does nothing if it does not exist), and CTAS creates a fresh version with the latest data. This pattern is simple and effective for Gold tables that are fully recomputed on each pipeline run. The downside is that between the DROP and the CTAS, the table does not exist — any query that tries to read it during this window will fail. For high-availability systems, the pattern is instead CTAS into a temporary name, then rename — but the basic drop-and-recreate is sufficient for most batch pipelines at HydroAlpes.

blocks.title: CTAS is the materialization tool
blocks.content: CTAS materializes a query result into a physical table. This is the core concept behind dbt's materializations (which you will encounter in Module 10) and behind every Gold-layer table at HydroAlpes. The SELECT defines the logic. CTAS stores the result. Downstream consumers query the stored result instead of recomputing the logic. This separation between computation (the SELECT) and storage (the table) is the foundation of scalable data pipeline design.

A table stores data. A view stores a query. When you create a view, the database saves the SELECT statement — not the result. Every time someone queries the view, the database executes the saved SELECT and returns the current result. Views are virtual tables: they look and behave like tables in SELECT queries (you can filter, join, and aggregate from them), but they contain no data of their own. Views serve two purposes for data engineers: they abstract complexity (hiding a 4-table join behind a simple view name) and they restrict access (showing analysts only the columns they need while hiding sensitive data).
CREATE VIEW

-- Create a view that abstracts the 4-table join from Module 6
CREATE VIEW silver.enriched_readings AS
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       m.customer_type,
       s.name          AS substation_name,
       s.capacity_mw,
       r.name          AS region_name,
       r.country
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.substations AS s
    ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.regions AS r
    ON m.region = r.region_id;

The view silver.enriched_readings wraps the 4-table join in a single name. Analysts can now write SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, region_name FROM silver.enriched_readings WHERE date = '2025-01-15' without knowing that the data comes from four joined tables. The complex join logic is defined once in the view and used by everyone who needs enriched readings. If the join logic changes (a new dimension table is added), only the view definition needs to be updated — every query that references the view automatically gets the new logic.
Views vs Tables

left.label: Table (CTAS)
points: Stores data on disk — query reads from physical storage
Fast to query (data is pre-computed)
Stale: reflects data at creation time
Takes storage space
Good for: Gold-layer analytical outputs queried frequently

right.label: View (CREATE VIEW)
points: Stores a query — data is computed fresh on every access
Slower to query (join/aggregation runs every time)
Always current: reflects the latest data in source tables
No additional storage
Good for: abstractions, access control, simplifying complex joins

blocks.content: When to Use Views

Views are ideal when the underlying data changes frequently and consumers always need the latest version — for example, an analyst dashboard that should always show today's data. Views are also ideal for hiding complexity: the 4-table join for enriched readings is a prime candidate. At HydroAlpes, Lena creates views for analyst-facing interfaces (where simplicity matters) and CTAS tables for pipeline-facing outputs (where performance matters). The rule of thumb: if the same complex query would be copy-pasted into multiple downstream queries, extract it into a view.
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW

-- Update the view definition without dropping it first
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW silver.enriched_readings AS
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       m.customer_type,
       s.name          AS substation_name,
       s.capacity_mw,
       r.name          AS region_name,
       r.country,
       mr.daily_kw * 0.001 AS daily_mwh   -- new column added
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.regions AS r ON m.region = r.region_id;

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW modifies the view definition in place — existing queries that reference the view continue to work, and the new column (daily_mwh) is available immediately. This is one of the advantages of views over CTAS tables for schema evolution: adding a column to a view does not require recreating the object or migrating data. At HydroAlpes, views are updated in the pipeline's migration scripts alongside any schema changes, ensuring that the view definition stays synchronized with the underlying tables.

blocks.title: Views are the interface layer
blocks.content: In a well-designed data platform, analysts never query Silver tables directly. They query views that abstract the joins, apply standard filters, and expose only the relevant columns. If the underlying table structure changes (a column is renamed, a table is split), only the view definition needs to be updated — the analysts' queries continue to work unchanged. Views are the interface between the data engineering team (who control the tables) and the data consumers (who write queries).

This module taught the DDL and DML statements that build and maintain database infrastructure: CREATE TABLE for structure, data types and constraints for quality enforcement, INSERT for adding data, UPDATE and DELETE for modifying data, ALTER TABLE for schema evolution, CTAS for materializing query results, and views for abstraction. This final lesson synthesizes these tools into the complete DDL script that creates HydroAlpes' Silver-layer schema — the schema you have been querying since Module 3.
The Complete Silver Schema

-- HydroAlpes Silver-layer schema
-- This script creates the complete structure from Module 2, lesson 8

CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS silver;

-- Dimension tables (created first because fact tables reference them)

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.regions (
    region_id    VARCHAR(30)   PRIMARY KEY,
    name         VARCHAR(100)  NOT NULL,
    country      VARCHAR(5)    NOT NULL CHECK (country IN ('CH', 'AT'))
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.substations (
    substation_id  VARCHAR(20)   PRIMARY KEY,
    name           VARCHAR(100)  NOT NULL,
    capacity_mw    INTEGER       NOT NULL CHECK (capacity_mw > 0),
    region         VARCHAR(30)   NOT NULL REFERENCES silver.regions (region_id)
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.meters (
    meter_id          VARCHAR(20)   PRIMARY KEY,
    region            VARCHAR(30)   NOT NULL REFERENCES silver.regions (region_id),
    customer_type     VARCHAR(20)   NOT NULL
                      CHECK (customer_type IN ('RESIDENTIAL','COMMERCIAL','INDUSTRIAL')),
    installation_date DATE          NOT NULL,
    substation_id     VARCHAR(20)   REFERENCES silver.substations (substation_id),
    is_active         BOOLEAN       DEFAULT TRUE
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.reservoirs (
    reservoir_id    VARCHAR(20)   PRIMARY KEY,
    name            VARCHAR(100)  NOT NULL,
    max_level_m     NUMERIC(8,2)  NOT NULL CHECK (max_level_m > 0),
    region          VARCHAR(30)   NOT NULL REFERENCES silver.regions (region_id)
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.turbines (
    turbine_id    VARCHAR(20)   PRIMARY KEY,
    reservoir_id  VARCHAR(20)   NOT NULL REFERENCES silver.reservoirs (reservoir_id),
    type          VARCHAR(30)   NOT NULL,
    capacity_kw   INTEGER       NOT NULL CHECK (capacity_kw > 0)
);

-- Fact tables (created after dimensions because of foreign key references)

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.meter_readings (
    meter_id       VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL REFERENCES silver.meters (meter_id),
    daily_kw       DOUBLE PRECISION   NOT NULL CHECK (daily_kw >= 0),
    date           DATE               NOT NULL,
    region         VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL REFERENCES silver.regions (region_id),
    customer_type  VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    substation_id  VARCHAR(20),
    PRIMARY KEY (meter_id, date)
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.energy_prices (
    market_zone      VARCHAR(10)   NOT NULL,
    price_timestamp  TIMESTAMP     NOT NULL,
    price_eur_mwh    NUMERIC(10,4) NOT NULL CHECK (price_eur_mwh >= 0),
    PRIMARY KEY (market_zone, price_timestamp)
);

The script creates the schema, then dimension tables in dependency order (regions first because substations and meters reference it), then fact tables last because they reference the dimension tables. Every table uses IF NOT EXISTS for idempotency. Every column has an explicit type. Identification columns are NOT NULL. Business rules are enforced with CHECK constraints: positive capacity, valid country codes, valid customer types, non-negative consumption. Foreign keys enforce referential integrity across the entire model. This script is the infrastructure that every query in Modules 3 through 8 depended on.
The DDL Checklist

rows: Every table has a PRIMARY KEY
Prevents duplicate rows, enables efficient lookups and joins
Ensures every row can be uniquely identified
Enforces referential integrity across the data model
Catches invalid data at insertion time, not downstream
Types match the data's precision requirements
NUMERIC for exact values, DOUBLE PRECISION for measurements
IF NOT EXISTS on every CREATE TABLE
Makes the script idempotent — safe to rerun
Dimension tables before fact tables to satisfy foreign keys

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 10, you will connect everything you have learned to the broader data engineering stack. SQL does not operate in isolation — it runs inside Spark, inside cloud warehouses, inside dbt, and inside pipeline orchestrators. Module 10 shows how the SQL skills from Modules 1 through 9 transfer to every platform in the modern data stack, introduces query plans and indexes at a conceptual level, and closes the course with a complete picture of SQL's role in the data engineer's toolkit.

modules.title: SQL in the Data Engineering Pipeline
modules.description: Understand how SQL operates across the data engineering stack — inside Spark SQL, inside cloud warehouses, inside dbt, and inside pipeline orchestration — and synthesize the entire course into a complete analytical toolkit.

blocks.content: Every SQL query you have written in this course runs against a PostgreSQL database — a single server processing data from disk. But the same SQL syntax works inside Apache Spark, a distributed engine that processes data across clusters of machines. Spark SQL lets you write standard SQL queries against DataFrames that hold terabytes of data distributed across dozens of executors. For HydroAlpes, this means the billing aggregation query from Module 5 — SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw) FROM meter_readings GROUP BY region — runs against 128 million rows on the Spark cluster using the same syntax you already know. The SQL transfers directly; only the execution engine changes.
How Spark SQL Works

In a PySpark pipeline, data is loaded into a distributed structure called a DataFrame. To query that DataFrame with SQL, you register it as a temporary view — giving it a table name that Spark SQL can reference — and then pass a SQL string to the spark.sql() function. The SQL is parsed by Spark's query optimizer (called Catalyst), which builds an execution plan, optimizes it, and distributes the work across the cluster's executors. The result is another DataFrame that you can further transform, join, or write to storage. The SQL string inside spark.sql() is standard SQL — the same SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, JOIN, and window functions you learned in Modules 3 through 8.

# PySpark: register a DataFrame as a view, then query it with SQL
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('hydroalpes-billing').getOrCreate()

# Load Silver meter readings into a DataFrame
df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meter_readings/')

# Register the DataFrame as a temporary SQL view
df.createOrReplaceTempView('meter_readings')

# Write standard SQL — same syntax as PostgreSQL
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT region,
           customer_type,
           SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
           COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id) AS active_meters
    FROM   meter_readings
    WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01'
      AND  date <  '2025-02-01'
    GROUP BY region, customer_type
    ORDER BY total_kw DESC
""")

result.show()  # Display the result

The SQL inside the triple-quoted string is identical to what you would write against PostgreSQL. The SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, and ORDER BY clauses are standard. The only difference is the surrounding Python code that loads the data and registers the view. At HydroAlpes, some pipeline engineers write transformations entirely in Spark SQL, some use the PySpark DataFrame API, and most use a mix — choosing SQL for complex aggregations and joins where it is more readable, and the DataFrame API for programmatic transformations like dynamic column generation. The SQL skills from this course apply directly in both approaches.
What Differs from PostgreSQL

Spark SQL supports most ANSI SQL features but has some differences. It uses LIMIT instead of PostgreSQL's FETCH FIRST. String concatenation uses CONCAT() instead of ||. Date functions have slightly different names (DATE_ADD instead of + INTERVAL). Spark SQL does not support UPDATE, DELETE, or ALTER TABLE on regular DataFrames — these operations require Delta Lake tables. Window functions, CTEs, subqueries, and all join types work identically. The core analytical SQL you learned in Modules 3 through 8 transfers without modification; the adjustments are in utility functions and DDL, not in the analytical core.

blocks.title: SQL is the bridge between single-server and distributed
blocks.content: Learning SQL on PostgreSQL and then using it on Spark is one of the most common career transitions in data engineering. The analytical thinking — set-based reasoning, aggregation patterns, join strategies, window function design — is identical. Only the execution environment changes. This is why HydroAlpes teaches SQL before Spark: the SQL foundation makes Spark SQL immediately productive.

Cloud data warehouses — Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, and Azure Synapse — are managed services that store and query large analytical datasets. Unlike PostgreSQL (which you manage yourself) or Spark (which requires cluster configuration), cloud warehouses are fully managed: you write SQL, the service handles storage, compute, scaling, and optimization. For data engineers, cloud warehouses are the consumption layer of the data platform — the place where analysts, dashboards, and reporting tools query the Gold-layer data that pipelines produce. SQL is the primary interface to every cloud warehouse.
What Stays the Same

The core SQL you learned in this course works on every cloud warehouse without modification. SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT — all standard. INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN, CROSS JOIN — all standard. CTEs (WITH clause), window functions (OVER, PARTITION BY, ROW_NUMBER, LAG, LEAD), subqueries, and aggregate functions — all standard. CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and views — all supported with minor syntax variations. A data engineer who knows ANSI SQL can start writing productive queries on any cloud warehouse within minutes, not days.
What Differs

The differences cluster in three areas: date functions (every warehouse has its own syntax for date arithmetic), semi-structured data handling (JSON, arrays, nested structures), and the cost model (which affects how you write queries — in BigQuery, SELECT * is expensive because you pay per byte scanned). The analytical core — the SQL you spent nine modules learning — is portable. The platform-specific adjustments are learnable in a few hours because you already understand what the query should do; you only need to look up the function name for how to express it on the new platform.

blocks.title: Cloud warehouses are SQL-first
blocks.content: Unlike Spark (which has both a Python API and a SQL API), cloud warehouses are SQL-first: SQL is the primary and often only interface. Snowflake SQL, BigQuery SQL, and Redshift SQL are the languages that analysts, data engineers, and BI tools use to interact with these platforms. Investing in SQL skills is investing in fluency on every cloud warehouse — present and future.

dbt (data build tool) is a transformation framework that has become one of the most widely adopted tools in modern data engineering. Its core idea is radical in its simplicity: every transformation is a SELECT statement. You write a SQL file containing a SELECT query, dbt wraps it in a CREATE TABLE AS SELECT or CREATE VIEW, and manages the execution, dependencies, testing, and documentation. The SELECT you write in a dbt model is exactly the SQL you learned in this course — CTEs, joins, window functions, aggregations. dbt adds the infrastructure around the SQL but does not change the SQL itself.
A dbt Model Is a SELECT

-- dbt model file: models/gold/regional_consumption.sql
-- This file contains ONLY a SELECT statement
-- dbt wraps it in CREATE TABLE AS SELECT automatically

WITH january_readings AS (
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region
    FROM   {{ ref('silver_meter_readings') }}
    WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01'
      AND  date <  '2025-02-01'
),
aggregated AS (
    SELECT region,
           SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
           COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id) AS active_meters
    FROM   january_readings
    GROUP BY region
)
SELECT region,
       total_kw,
       total_kw * 0.001 AS total_mwh,
       active_meters
FROM   aggregated

The SQL inside the dbt model is standard: two CTEs and a final SELECT, using exactly the patterns from Module 7 (lesson 8). The only non-standard element is {{ ref('silver_meter_readings') }} — a dbt function that references another model by name instead of a hardcoded table name. dbt resolves this reference at runtime, builds a dependency graph between models, and executes them in the correct order. The SQL writer does not manage execution order, table creation, or dependencies — dbt handles all of that. The writer focuses entirely on the transformation logic expressed in SQL.
Why dbt Matters for SQL Skills

dbt has made SQL the dominant transformation language in analytics engineering. Before dbt, many transformations were written in Python or proprietary ETL tools. dbt demonstrated that SQL — specifically, composable SELECT statements with CTEs — is sufficient for the vast majority of data transformations. This shift means that SQL proficiency is no longer optional for data engineers — it is the primary skill. Every CTE pattern you learned in Module 7, every join from Module 6, every window function from Module 8, and every aggregation from Module 5 is directly applicable in dbt models. The SQL you know is the SQL that dbt uses.

blocks.title: CTEs are the language of dbt
blocks.content: dbt's recommended style is to write each model as a chain of CTEs — exactly the pattern from Module 7 (lesson 8). The first CTE references source data, subsequent CTEs transform it progressively, and the final SELECT produces the output. If you are comfortable chaining CTEs, you are comfortable writing dbt models. This is not a coincidence — dbt was designed around the CTE pattern because CTEs make SQL transformations readable, testable, and composable.

Data quality is not a separate discipline from SQL — it is SQL applied to the question 'is this data correct?' Every data quality check at HydroAlpes is a SQL query that uses the techniques from Modules 3 through 8: filtering for anomalies (WHERE), counting NULLs (COUNT), detecting duplicates (GROUP BY with HAVING), verifying referential integrity (LEFT JOIN with IS NULL), and checking value ranges (CHECK constraints and WHERE conditions). This lesson presents the complete data quality query library that HydroAlpes' team runs daily against the Silver layer.
Completeness Checks

-- How complete is each column? Compare COUNT(*) with COUNT(column)
SELECT COUNT(*)              AS total_rows,
       COUNT(meter_id)       AS meter_id_present,
       COUNT(daily_kw)       AS daily_kw_present,
       COUNT(substation_id)  AS substation_present,
       ROUND(COUNT(substation_id)::NUMERIC / COUNT(*) * 100, 1)
                             AS substation_pct
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

This query computes the fill rate for each column in yesterday's readings. If total_rows is 350,000 and substation_present is 349,500, the substation mapping is 99.86% complete — 500 meters are unmapped. Running this daily detects sudden drops: if substation_present drops from 349,500 to 300,000, a pipeline problem affected the substation join. The percentage column makes trends visible over time when the results are logged to a monitoring table.
Uniqueness Checks

-- Detect duplicate readings (should be zero rows if PRIMARY KEY is enforced)
SELECT meter_id, date, COUNT(*) AS copies
FROM   silver.meter_readings
GROUP BY meter_id, date
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY copies DESC
LIMIT  10;
Referential Integrity Checks

-- Find readings that reference meters not in the meters table (orphans)
SELECT mr.meter_id, COUNT(*) AS orphan_readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  m.meter_id IS NULL
GROUP BY mr.meter_id
ORDER BY orphan_readings DESC;

The LEFT JOIN plus IS NULL pattern from Module 6 (lesson 3) finds readings whose meter_id does not exist in the meters table. These orphan readings cannot be enriched with customer type, substation, or region information — they are incomplete records that affect every downstream join. Grouping by meter_id and counting shows which unregistered meters have the most orphan readings, directing the operations team to prioritize their registration. At HydroAlpes, this query runs daily and feeds an automated alert when orphan count exceeds a threshold.
Range and Consistency Checks

-- Value range check: find readings outside expected bounds
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  daily_kw < 0                           -- negative = sensor error
   OR  daily_kw > 5000                         -- above 5 MW = implausible
   OR  (customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL' AND daily_kw > 100)  -- residential > 100 kW
ORDER BY daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  20;

Range checks combine simple WHERE conditions with domain knowledge. Negative consumption is physically impossible. Readings above 5,000 kW (5 MW) exceed any single meter's capacity. Residential readings above 100 kW are implausible — even large homes rarely exceed 50 kW per day. Each rule catches a different category of data quality issue: sensor malfunctions, unit conversion errors, and misclassified customer types. These checks are the SQL equivalent of the CHECK constraints from Module 9 (lesson 3), but applied as monitoring queries rather than insertion guards.

blocks.title: Data quality is just SQL
blocks.content: Every data quality check in this lesson uses techniques from earlier modules: COUNT and NULL comparison (Module 5), GROUP BY with HAVING (Module 5), LEFT JOIN with IS NULL (Module 6), WHERE with compound conditions (Module 4). There is no special data quality language or tool — SQL itself is the data quality tool. A data engineer who can write precise analytical queries can write precise quality checks. The skills are the same.

Before writing a pipeline transformation or a reporting query, a data engineer must understand the data: how many rows does the table have, what values exist in each column, are there outliers, are there gaps, what is the distribution? This exploratory work is done entirely in SQL, using the techniques from Modules 3 through 8. This lesson presents the standard exploration workflow that Markus runs whenever he encounters a new table — a sequence of queries that builds understanding from structure to content to distribution.
Step 1: Structure and Sample

-- See the table's columns and a few sample rows
SELECT *
FROM   silver.meter_readings
LIMIT  10;

-- Count total rows and date range
SELECT COUNT(*)    AS total_rows,
       MIN(date)   AS earliest,
       MAX(date)   AS latest
FROM   silver.meter_readings;

The first query uses SELECT * (acceptable for exploration, per Module 3 lesson 3) to see all columns and sample values. The second query establishes scale: how many rows, and what time period does the data cover. At HydroAlpes, 128 million rows spanning 2024-01-01 to 2025-01-31 tells Markus this is roughly 13 months of daily data for 350,000 meters. These two queries take seconds and provide the foundation for every subsequent exploration query.
Step 2: Cardinality and Distribution

-- How many distinct values in each categorical column?
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT region)        AS regions,
       COUNT(DISTINCT customer_type)  AS types,
       COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id)       AS meters,
       COUNT(DISTINCT date)           AS dates
FROM   silver.meter_readings;

-- What is the distribution of readings by customer type?
SELECT customer_type,
       COUNT(*)                           AS readings,
       ROUND(COUNT(*)::NUMERIC / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct
FROM   silver.meter_readings
GROUP BY customer_type
ORDER BY readings DESC;

The distribution query uses a window function (SUM OVER()) from Module 8 to compute each customer type's percentage of total readings. The result shows that 80% of readings come from residential meters, 15% from commercial, and 5% from industrial. This distribution shapes every analytical decision: residential dominates by count, but industrial dominates by consumption volume. Knowing this before writing a billing query prevents surprises. The exploration queries use the full toolkit — GROUP BY, COUNT DISTINCT, window functions — in a lightweight, fast workflow.
Step 3: Outliers and Anomalies

-- Statistical profile of the daily_kw column
SELECT MIN(daily_kw)                          AS min_kw,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY daily_kw) AS p25,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY daily_kw) AS median,
       AVG(daily_kw)                           AS mean,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY daily_kw) AS p75,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY daily_kw) AS p99,
       MAX(daily_kw)                           AS max_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';

The percentile profile reveals the data's shape. If the median is 0.82 kW but the mean is 1.46 kW, the distribution is right-skewed — a small number of industrial meters pull the average up. The 99th percentile (p99) shows where the extreme values begin, and the gap between p99 and MAX indicates how far the outliers extend. This statistical profile is the first thing an experienced data engineer examines when a downstream aggregation produces unexpected results — it reveals whether the issue is in the data's distribution or in the query's logic.

blocks.title: Exploration before transformation
blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, every new pipeline starts with 30 minutes of SQL exploration: sample the data, check the cardinality, profile the distributions, find the outliers. This investment prevents hours of debugging later. A pipeline built on assumptions about the data ('all readings are positive,' 'every meter reports daily') fails when those assumptions are wrong. Exploration replaces assumptions with facts.

When you write a SQL query, the database does not execute it literally — it builds an execution plan that determines the most efficient way to produce the requested result. The EXPLAIN statement shows this plan. Understanding query plans is how a data engineer moves from writing correct SQL to writing fast SQL. This lesson introduces the concept at a high level — you will not become an expert in plan reading today, but you will understand what a plan is, why it matters, and how to use EXPLAIN to start diagnosing slow queries.
What EXPLAIN Shows

-- EXPLAIN shows the plan without executing the query
EXPLAIN
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region;

QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------
 HashAggregate  (cost=18502.00..18502.04 rows=4 width=40)
   Group Key: region
   ->  Seq Scan on meter_readings mr  (cost=0.00..17252.00 rows=350000 width=38)
         Filter: (date = '2025-01-15'::date)

The plan reads bottom to top. The database starts with a sequential scan (Seq Scan) of the meter_readings table, reading every row and applying the date filter. The estimated result is 350,000 rows. Then it performs a hash aggregate (HashAggregate) to group by region and compute the SUM, producing 4 rows. The cost numbers are the optimizer's estimate of how expensive each step is — higher cost means more work. This plan tells you that the database is scanning the entire table to find January 15 rows, which might be slow on a very large table.
Sequential Scan vs Index Scan

The most important distinction in a query plan is how the database reads the table. A sequential scan (Seq Scan) reads every row in the table and checks the filter condition — efficient for large result sets but slow when only a small fraction of rows match. An index scan (Index Scan) uses an index to jump directly to the matching rows — fast when the filter is selective (few rows match) but slower than sequential scan when most rows match. The database's query optimizer chooses between these based on the filter's selectivity and the available indexes. When a query is slow, checking the plan for sequential scans on large tables is the first diagnostic step.
EXPLAIN ANALYZE

EXPLAIN shows the planned execution. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs the query and shows both the plan and the real execution times. This is more useful for diagnosing performance problems because it shows actual row counts (not just estimates) and actual time spent at each step. If the optimizer estimates 350,000 rows but the actual count is 3.5 million, the statistics are stale and the optimizer is making bad decisions. At HydroAlpes, Markus uses EXPLAIN ANALYZE when a query that normally takes 2 seconds suddenly takes 30 — the actual-versus-estimated comparison usually reveals the cause.

blocks.title: Query plans are a mid-level skill
blocks.content: Reading and optimizing query plans is a deep topic that the SQL Mid-Level Track covers in detail. For now, knowing that EXPLAIN exists, that plans read bottom-to-top, and that sequential scans on large tables are a common performance bottleneck gives you the vocabulary to participate in performance discussions with your team. When a senior engineer says 'check the plan for a seq scan,' you know what they mean.

When the database performs a sequential scan to find rows where date = '2025-01-15', it reads every row in the table — all 128 million of them — and checks each one against the condition. On a large table, this takes time. An index is a separate data structure that the database maintains alongside the table, organized to make specific lookups fast. Think of a book index: instead of reading every page to find a topic, you look up the page number in the index and go directly there. A database index does the same thing for row lookups: it maps column values to row locations, enabling the database to find matching rows without scanning the entire table.
How Indexes Work

An index on the date column of meter_readings stores each distinct date value alongside pointers to the rows that contain that date. When the database needs rows where date = '2025-01-15', it looks up '2025-01-15' in the index (a fast operation, similar to a binary search), gets the row pointers, and reads only those rows from the table. Instead of scanning 128 million rows, it reads the index entry and then fetches roughly 350,000 rows directly. The larger the table and the more selective the filter, the bigger the speedup. Primary keys automatically get indexes — the composite primary key (meter_id, date) on meter_readings already provides fast lookups by meter and date.
Creating an Index

CREATE INDEX builds the index structure. The name (idx_meter_readings_date) follows a convention: idx prefix, table name, column name. After creation, the database automatically uses the index when it estimates that an index scan is faster than a sequential scan. You do not change your queries — the optimizer decides whether to use the index based on the filter's selectivity. If you query WHERE date = '2025-01-15' (selecting 0.3% of rows), the optimizer will likely use the index. If you query WHERE date >= '2024-01-01' (selecting 80% of rows), the optimizer will likely skip the index and sequential scan instead.
The Trade-Off

Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes. Every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must also update the index, which adds overhead. An index also consumes storage — the index on 128 million rows can be several gigabytes. The trade-off determines indexing strategy: create indexes on columns that appear frequently in WHERE clauses (date, region, meter_id) and JOIN conditions. Do not create indexes on columns that are rarely filtered or on tables that are write-heavy and rarely queried. At HydroAlpes, Silver tables (read-heavy, query-optimized) have multiple indexes. Bronze tables (write-heavy, rarely queried directly) have no indexes except the primary key.

blocks.title: Indexes are infrastructure, not query syntax
blocks.content: You never reference an index in a SQL query. You create it once, and the optimizer uses it automatically when it helps. This is a consequence of SQL's declarative nature: you describe what data you want, and the database decides how to retrieve it efficiently — including whether to use an index. Understanding that indexes exist and knowing when to create them is a junior-level awareness skill. Designing optimal index strategies is a mid-level topic.

When a pipeline refreshes a Gold table, it typically performs two operations: delete the old data and insert the new data. If the delete succeeds but the insert fails (because of a bug, a disk error, or a network timeout), the Gold table is empty — downstream dashboards show no data, and the billing team gets blank reports. Transactions solve this problem by grouping multiple SQL statements into a single atomic unit: either all statements succeed (and the changes become permanent), or any failure rolls back all changes (and the table returns to its previous state). Transactions are the mechanism that makes multi-statement pipelines safe.
BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK

-- A transaction groups multiple statements into one atomic operation
BEGIN;  -- Start the transaction

-- Step 1: Remove old January billing data
DELETE FROM gold.regional_consumption
WHERE  month = '2025-01-01';

-- Step 2: Insert fresh January billing data
INSERT INTO gold.regional_consumption
    (region, customer_type, month, total_kw, meter_count)
SELECT region, customer_type,
       DATE_TRUNC('month', date) AS month,
       SUM(daily_kw), COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id)
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY region, customer_type, DATE_TRUNC('month', date);

COMMIT;  -- Make both changes permanent (only if both succeeded)

BEGIN starts the transaction. Every statement between BEGIN and COMMIT runs inside the transaction. If all statements succeed, COMMIT makes the changes permanent. If any statement fails (or if you explicitly call ROLLBACK), all changes since BEGIN are undone — the DELETE is reversed, and the Gold table returns to its previous state. The table is never in a half-updated state: it either has the old data or the new data, never an empty table. This atomicity — all or nothing — is the fundamental guarantee of transactions.
Why Transactions Matter for Pipelines

Without transactions, a pipeline that performs DELETE followed by INSERT has a window of vulnerability: between the two statements, the table is empty. Any query that reads the table during this window sees no data. With a transaction, the DELETE and INSERT are invisible to other queries until COMMIT — other sessions see the old data until the moment COMMIT completes, at which point they instantly see the new data. This isolation means dashboards and reports always see a consistent, complete dataset, never a partially refreshed table.

At HydroAlpes, every Gold-layer refresh runs inside a transaction. The pipeline begins a transaction, deletes the target period, inserts the fresh data, verifies the row count matches expectations, and commits. If the row count is wrong (a signal that something went wrong in the Silver layer), the pipeline calls ROLLBACK instead of COMMIT, logs the anomaly, and alerts the team. The Gold table remains unchanged until the issue is resolved and the pipeline reruns successfully.

blocks.title: Transactions protect against partial failure
blocks.content: The key principle: if your pipeline performs multiple write operations that must succeed together, wrap them in a transaction. DELETE + INSERT, UPDATE + INSERT, or any multi-step modification should be atomic. Transactions are the difference between a pipeline that corrupts data on failure and a pipeline that safely rolls back to the previous state. This is a foundational concept that the mid-level track explores in depth.

This lesson steps back from syntax and mechanics to address the habits that separate production-quality SQL from ad-hoc queries. Every technique in this course can be applied well or poorly. A CTE chain that is logically correct but uses meaningless aliases (t1, t2, t3) is harder to maintain than one with descriptive names (january_readings, region_totals, enriched). A join that works today but uses SELECT * breaks when a column is added tomorrow. The difference between a junior engineer who writes SQL and a junior engineer who writes good SQL is not knowledge of more functions — it is discipline in applying the functions they know.
The Seven Habits

rows: Explicit column lists (never SELECT * in pipelines)
Readable top-to-bottom, each step independently testable
Consistent formatting (clauses on separate lines, aligned)
Scannable at a glance, reviewable in pull requests
Meaningful aliases (mr for meter_readings, not t1)
Comments that explain why, not what
Business logic and design decisions preserved
Test WHERE with SELECT before UPDATE or DELETE
Version-controlled SQL files (not ad-hoc notebook cells)

blocks.content: Version Control for SQL

Production SQL should live in version-controlled files — Git repositories with pull requests, code review, and change history — not in notebook cells or ad-hoc query editor sessions. At HydroAlpes, every pipeline query is a .sql file in the team's Git repository. Changes go through pull requests where Lena reviews the SQL for correctness, readability, and adherence to the team's style guide. If a query breaks in production, the team can trace the change that caused it, revert to the previous version, and rerun the pipeline. This workflow is identical to how software engineers manage application code — and it is the standard in modern data engineering.
The HydroAlpes Style Guide Summary

Keywords uppercase (SELECT, FROM, WHERE). Column names lowercase (meter_id, daily_kw). One clause per line. Column lists indented under SELECT, one per line. Conditions indented under WHERE with AND at the start of each line. Table aliases short and consistent across the codebase (mr, m, s, r). Comments in a header block for every pipeline query: purpose, author, date, source tables. No SELECT * in any file committed to the repository. Every CTE has a descriptive name. Every JOIN has an explicit type (LEFT JOIN, not just JOIN). These conventions make every engineer's SQL look the same, which means any engineer can read and maintain any query.

blocks.title: Habits compound over time
blocks.content: A style guide followed consistently for 100 queries creates a codebase that any team member can navigate instantly. The same 100 queries written without conventions create a codebase where each query requires 10 minutes of orientation before you can understand it. The investment in discipline pays off not in the first query but in the hundredth — when the team has grown, the original author has moved on, and a new engineer needs to modify a query they have never seen before.

Ten modules. One hundred lessons. Forty-two hours. You started in Module 1 with no SQL knowledge — just Python experience and the question 'what is this language that every data platform speaks?' You end here with a complete analytical SQL toolkit: you can retrieve data, filter it, sort it, aggregate it, join it across tables, compose complex logic with CTEs, compute running analytics with window functions, build table infrastructure with DDL, and connect your SQL skills to Spark, cloud warehouses, and dbt. This is not a partial foundation — it is the complete set of SQL skills that a junior data engineer uses every day.
The HydroAlpes Journey

Every concept in this course came to life through HydroAlpes' data. You learned SELECT by choosing columns from meter readings (Module 3). You learned WHERE by filtering to BERN residential customers in January (Module 4). You learned GROUP BY by computing regional consumption totals that power HydroAlpes' billing reports (Module 5). You learned joins by connecting meter readings with substations, regions, and energy prices across four tables (Module 6). You learned CTEs by building the multi-step Gold-layer billing query that chains filter, aggregate, and enrich stages (Module 7). You learned window functions by ranking turbines within each reservoir and computing day-over-day consumption changes for individual meters (Module 8). You learned CREATE TABLE by building the Silver schema that every query depended on (Module 9). And in this module, you saw how that same SQL runs inside Spark, inside warehouses, and inside dbt.

The HydroAlpes data model — meter_readings, meters, substations, regions, turbines, reservoirs, energy_prices — is now as familiar to you as any dataset you have worked with. The column names (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type) are second nature. The four Alpine regions (BERN, ZURICH, INNSBRUCK, SALZBURG) feel like real places. Lena's pipeline design principles and Markus's debugging patterns are part of your mental toolkit. This familiarity is not accidental — it is the result of sustained practice with consistent data, and it mirrors how data engineers work in real companies: deep familiarity with the domain data, built over months of daily querying.
What You Can Do Now

rows: Write SELECT queries with expressions, aliases, and DISTINCT
Filter with WHERE, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE, IS NULL, and compound conditions
Aggregate with COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP BY, and HAVING
Join tables: INNER, LEFT, FULL OUTER, CROSS, and self-joins
Compose logic with subqueries, CTEs, EXISTS, and recursive CTEs
Compute analytics with ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, and moving averages
Build infrastructure with CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CTAS, and views
Apply SQL inside Spark, cloud warehouses, and dbt

blocks.content: What Comes Next

The SQL Mid-Level Track picks up where this course ends. You know how to write correct SQL — the mid-level track teaches you to write fast SQL and production-resilient SQL. You will learn to read and optimize query plans, design indexing strategies for large tables, write advanced window function patterns, handle slowly changing dimensions, use materialized views for performance, and build the testing and monitoring framework that keeps production SQL reliable. The shift from junior to mid-level is the shift from 'I can write a query' to 'I can write a query that runs efficiently at scale and handles edge cases gracefully.'

blocks.title: You are ready
blocks.content: You can query any table in HydroAlpes' data model. You can write the aggregations that power billing reports. You can build the joins that produce Gold-layer analytical datasets. You can compose multi-step transformations with CTEs that read like pipeline stages. You can rank, compare, and window across partitions. You can create the tables and views that make all of this possible. These are not classroom skills — they are the skills that data engineers use on their first day, their hundredth day, and every day in between. The foundation is built. Go use it.
